# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Crystallography — The Foundation of Materials Science

### 1.1.1 Crystal
A **crystal** is a solid material whose constituent atoms, ions, or molecules are arranged in a highly ordered, repeating three-dimensional pattern that extends throughout the entire solid body. This long-range periodic arrangement is what fundamentally distinguishes crystalline solids from amorphous (non-crystalline) solids like glass or rubber.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition to Memorize:**
> *"A crystal is a solid composed of atoms, ions, or molecules arranged in a repetitive three-dimensional pattern, exhibiting long-range order and definite geometric shape bounded by plane surfaces (crystal faces) that are the external expression of internal ordered structure."*

**Conceptual Analogy — The Lego City:**
Imagine building an entire city out of identical Lego bricks. If every brick is placed in the exact same orientation, stacked row upon row in a perfect grid, you get a structure like a crystal — predictable, symmetrical, and infinitely repeatable. Now imagine throwing those same Lego bricks into a pile and gluing them randomly. That pile is essentially an **amorphous solid** — same bricks, but no order. The crystal is the orderly city; the amorphous solid is the chaotic pile.

### 1.1.2 Space Lattice
A **space lattice** (also called Bravais lattice, named after Auguste Bravais, 1848) is a mathematical abstraction — an infinite array of points in three-dimensional space where each point has identical surroundings to every other point. These points represent the positions of atoms (or atomic groups) in a crystal.

Mathematically, a space lattice is defined by **three translation vectors** $\vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{c}$ such that any lattice point can be reached from any other by:
$$\vec{R} = n_1\vec{a} + n_2\vec{b} + n_3\vec{c}$$

where $n_1, n_2, n_3$ are integers.

**The 14 Bravais Lattices** are grouped into **7 crystal systems**:

| Crystal System | Bravais Lattices | Lattice Parameters |
|---|---|---|
| Cubic | Simple (SC), Body-Centered (BCC), Face-Centered (FCC) | $a=b=c$, $\alpha=\beta=\gamma=90°$ |
| Tetragonal | Simple, Body-Centered | $a=b\neq c$, $\alpha=\beta=\gamma=90°$ |
| Orthorhombic | Simple, Body-Centered, Face-Centered, Base-Centered | $a\neq b\neq c$, all angles $90°$ |
| Hexagonal | Simple | $a_1=a_2=a_3\neq c$, angles $90°,120°$ |
| Rhombohedral | Simple | $a=b=c$, $\alpha=\beta=\gamma\neq 90°$ |
| Monoclinic | Simple, Base-Centered | $a\neq b\neq c$, $\alpha=\gamma=90°\neq\beta$ |
| Triclinic | Simple | $a\neq b\neq c$, $\alpha\neq\beta\neq\gamma$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Out of the 14 Bravais lattices, only **3 belong to the Cubic system** — SC, BCC, and FCC — and these three are the most frequently tested in board examinations.

### 1.1.3 Unit Cell
A **unit cell** is the smallest repeating three-dimensional block of a crystal lattice that, when repeated by translation in all three directions, generates the entire crystal. It is characterized by:

- **Lattice parameters:** Six — three edge lengths ($a, b, c$) and three inter-axial angles ($\alpha, \beta, \gamma$).
- **Atoms per unit cell ($n$):** Counts how many whole atoms belong to one unit cell.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Wallpaper Pattern:**
Think of a tiled bathroom wall. Each individual tile is the unit cell. When you repeat that tile across the entire wall (using translations), you get the full pattern (the crystal). The unit cell captures every essential feature of the wall design.

---

## 1.2 The Three Principal Metallic Crystal Structures

### 1.2.1 Body-Centered Cubic (BCC)
In BCC, **8 atoms sit at the corners** of a cube and **1 atom occupies the body center**. Common BCC metals: Iron (α-Fe at room temperature), Chromium (Cr), Molybdenum (Mo), Tungsten (W), Vanadium (V), Sodium (Na).

### 1.2.2 Face-Centered Cubic (FCC)
In FCC, **8 atoms at corners** + **6 atoms at the centers of each face**. Common FCC metals: Aluminum (Al), Copper (Cu), Gold (Au), Silver (Ag), Nickel (Ni), Lead (Pb) at certain temperatures, Iron (γ-Fe above 912°C).

### 1.2.3 Hexagonal Close-Packed (HCP)
HCP has atoms arranged in two interpenetrating hexagonal layers forming an ABAB stacking. **Coordination number = 12** (like FCC). Common HCP metals: Magnesium (Mg), Zinc (Zn), Titanium (Ti), Cobalt (Co), Beryllium (Be), Cadmium (Cd).

**Conceptual Analogy — Packing Oranges in a Box:**
- SC = oranges at the 8 corners only (poor packing, like a fruit bowl with minimal stacking)
- BCC = add 1 in the middle (denser)
- FCC = put oranges on every face (very dense — like stacking cannonballs)
- HCP = stacking spheres in alternating offset rows (equally dense as FCC — the most efficient packing in 3D space)

---

## 1.3 Coordination Number and Radius Ratio

### 1.3.1 Coordination Number (CN)
The **coordination number** is the number of nearest neighbor atoms directly touching a given atom in the crystal structure. It quantifies how tightly atoms are packed.

| Structure | Coordination Number | Packing Factor |
|---|---|---|
| SC | 6 | 0.52 |
| BCC | 8 | 0.68 |
| FCC | 12 | 0.74 |
| HCP | 12 | 0.74 |

### 1.3.2 Radius Ratio Rule
The **radius ratio** $\frac{r}{R}$ (where $r$ = radius of cation, $R$ = radius of anion) determines which coordination geometry a binary ionic compound will adopt.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Radius Ratio Rule Thresholds:**
> - $0.155 \leq r/R < 0.225$ → **Triangular** (CN = 3)
> - $0.225 \leq r/R < 0.414$ → **Tetrahedral** (CN = 4)
> - $0.414 \leq r/R < 0.732$ → **Octahedral** (CN = 6)
> - $0.732 \leq r/R < 1.0$ → **Cubic (CN = 8)**
> - $r/R = 1.0$ → **Close-packed (CN = 12)**

**Intuitive Explanation:** Think of a small ball (cation) sitting in a hole between larger balls (anions). If the small ball is too tiny, it "rattles around" and falls into a lower-coordination triangular hole. As it grows larger, it pushes the larger balls outward and stabilizes in a higher-coordination geometry.

---

## 1.4 Polymorphism and Allotropy

### 1.4.1 Polymorphism
**Polymorphism** is the ability of a solid material to exist in more than one crystal structure (phase), depending on conditions of temperature and/or pressure. This is a general term applicable to all materials.

### 1.4.2 Allotropy
**Allotropy** is a special case of polymorphism, specifically applied to **pure elemental solids**. The different crystal structures of the same element are called **allotropes**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Classic KTU Example — Iron (Fe):**
> - α-Fe (BCC) — stable below 912°C
> - γ-Fe (FCC) — stable between 912°C and 1394°C
> - δ-Fe (BCC) — stable between 1394°C and 1538°C (melting point)
>
> **Carbon (C):** Diamond (cubic) vs. Graphite (hexagonal) vs. Fullerene, Graphene
> **Titanium (Ti):** α-Ti (HCP) below 882°C; β-Ti (BCC) above 882°C

---

## 1.5 Phase Diagrams — The Roadmap of Materials

A **phase diagram** (also called equilibrium diagram or constitutional diagram) is a graphical map showing the **stable phases** of a material system as a function of **temperature, pressure, and composition**. For condensed matter (solid + liquid), pressure is usually held constant (atmospheric) and we plot **Temperature vs. Composition**.

**Intuitive Analogy — The Weather Map:**
A phase diagram is to a metallurgist what a weather map is to a meteorologist. It tells you: "At this temperature and this composition, you WILL find these phases coexisting in equilibrium." It's a predictive tool.

---

## 1.6 Solid Solutions and Hume-Rothery's Rules

### 1.6.1 Solid Solutions
A **solid solution** is a solid phase containing two or more elements, where the atoms of one (solute) are incorporated into the crystal lattice of the other (solvent) without forming a new distinct phase.

Two types:
- **Substitutional solid solution:** Solute atoms replace solvent atoms at lattice sites (e.g., Cu-Ni).
- **Interstitial solid solution:** Small solute atoms (e.g., C, N, H) occupy the voids (interstices) between solvent atoms (e.g., Fe-C austenite).

### 1.6.2 Hume-Rothery's Rules
**William Hume-Rothery** (1899–1968) formulated empirical rules predicting when two metals will form an **extensive substitutional solid solution**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 5 Hume-Rothery Rules:**
> 1. **Atomic size factor:** Difference in atomic radii must be **≤ 15%**.
> 2. **Same crystal structure:** Both metals must have the same structure (both FCC, both BCC, etc.).
> 3. **Similar valency:** Metals should have the same valence. A metal of higher valency dissolves more of a lower-valency metal than vice versa.
> 4. **Similar electronegativity:** Close electronegativity values prevent compound formation.
> 5. **Nearly equal chemical affinity** — no strong tendency to form compounds.

**Analogy — Compatibility in a Hostel:**
Imagine moving new roommates (solute atoms) into a shared room (solvent lattice). They'll get along well if:
- Their personalities (size) are similar (Rule 1)
- They have similar habits (crystal structure) (Rule 2)
- They share the same "social status" (valency) (Rule 3)
- They don't repel each other chemically (Rule 4, 5)

---

## 1.7 The Iron-Carbon Phase Diagram (Fe-Fe₃C)

The **Iron-Carbon equilibrium diagram** is arguably the **most important diagram in all of metallurgy** because it is the foundation of all steel and cast iron design.

**Key Components:**
- **Ferrite (α-Fe):** BCC iron, very low C solubility (max **0.022 wt% C at 727°C**)
- **Austenite (γ-Fe):** FCC iron, much higher C solubility (max **2.14 wt% C at 1147°C**)
- **Cementite (Fe₃C):** Iron carbide, contains **6.67 wt% C**, hard and brittle
- **Pearlite:** Eutectoid mixture of ferrite + cementite (88% ferrite, 12% cementite)
- **Ledeburite:** Eutectic mixture of austenite + cementite (at 4.3 wt% C, 1147°C)

**Critical Temperatures & Points:**
| Point | Temperature | Composition | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Eutectoid | **727°C** | **0.77 wt% C** | Pearlite forms |
| Eutectic | **1147°C** | **4.30 wt% C** | Ledeburite forms |
| Peritectic | **1493°C** | **0.16 wt% C** | δ → γ + L |
| A₁ | 727°C | — | Lower critical temp. |
| A₃ | 727–912°C | varies | γ → α + Fe₃C start |
| Acm | 727°C | varies | Cementite solubility limit |

---

## 1.8 Heat Treatment

Heat treatment is the controlled heating and cooling of metals/alloys in the solid state to obtain desired **microstructure and properties** (hardness, strength, ductility, toughness).

### 1.8.1 Annealing
**Annealing** is a general term for heat treatment that produces a **soft, ductile state** by reducing hardness, internal stresses, and refining grain structure. Three stages: **Recovery → Recrystallization → Grain growth**.

### 1.8.2 Normalizing
**Normalizing** is heating steel **30–50°C above the upper critical temperature (Ac₃ or Acm)**, holding for uniform temperature, then cooling in **still air**. The result is a **finer pearlite** than annealing because air cooling is faster.

### 1.8.3 Hardening (Quenching)
**Hardening** is heating steel above Ac₃ (for hypoeutectoid) or Ac₁ (for hypereutectoid), holding for austenitization, then rapidly quenching in water/oil/polymer to form **Martensite** (supersaturated BCT structure — extremely hard and brittle).

### 1.8.4 Spheroidizing
**Spheroidizing** is prolonged heating just below A₁ (~700°C), producing **globular/spheroidal cementite particles** in a ferrite matrix — the softest possible structure, ideal for **machining**.

### 1.8.5 Tempering
**Tempering** is reheating hardened (martensitic) steel to a temperature **below Ac₁ (150–650°C)**, holding, then cooling. It **reduces brittleness** while retaining most hardness by transforming brittle martensite into tempered martensite (ferrite + finely dispersed cementite).

---

## 1.9 Surface Hardening Methods

These are heat treatments applied only to the **surface layer** of steel to create a hard, wear-resistant exterior while maintaining a tough, ductile core.

### 1.9.1 Methods with No Change in Surface Composition
The bulk chemistry stays the same; only the phase changes from austenite to martensite at the surface due to rapid heating + quenching.

| Method | Heat Source | Typical Depth |
|---|---|---|
| **Flame Hardening** | Oxy-acetylene / oxy-propane flame | 1–6 mm |
| **Induction Hardening** | High-frequency AC electromagnetic induction | 1–6 mm (controllable) |
| **Laser Hardening** | Focused laser beam | 0.5–2 mm (precise) |
| **Electron Beam Hardening** | Focused electron beam in vacuum | 0.1–1 mm (vacuum only) |

### 1.9.2 Methods with Change in Surface Composition
These methods **diffuse new atoms (C, N) into the surface**, altering chemistry, then quench.

| Method | Diffusing Element | Process | Key Reaction |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Carburizing** | Carbon | Heat in C-rich environment (gas, liquid, pack, vacuum) at 850–950°C | Surface C ↑ → high-carbon martensite on quenching |
| **Nitriding** | Nitrogen | Heat in NH₃ gas at 500–550°C (below A₁) | Forms Fe₄N, Fe₂₃N (nitride compounds) — no quenching needed |

**Analogy — Surface Hardening = Toasting Bread:**
Think of plain bread as low-carbon steel (soft, ductile). Carburizing/Nitriding is like coating the bread with butter and sugar before toasting — the *outside* becomes a different, harder, more wear-resistant layer, while the *inside* stays soft and chewy. The "no-change" methods are like simple toasting — the bread is still bread, just dehydrated and crisp on the outside.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Atomic Packing Factor (APF) Derivation Framework

The **Atomic Packing Factor (APF)** is defined as the fraction of volume in a unit cell occupied by atoms (treated as hard spheres), assuming atomic touching along specific directions.

$$\text{APF} = \frac{V_{\text{atoms in unit cell}}}{V_{\text{unit cell}}} = \frac{n \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3}{a^3}$$

where $n$ = number of atoms per unit cell, $r$ = atomic radius, $a$ = lattice parameter.

---

### 2.1.1 Simple Cubic (SC) — APF Derivation Logic
- **Step 1:** Atoms per unit cell $n = 1$ (8 corners × 1/8 = 1)
- **Step 2:** Atoms touch along cube edge → $a = 2r$
- **Step 3:** Substitute in APF formula:

$$\text{APF} = \frac{1 \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3}{(2r)^3} = \frac{\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3}{8r^3} = \frac{\pi}{6} \approx 0.524$$

---

### 2.1.2 Body-Centered Cubic (BCC) — APF Derivation Logic
- **Step 1:** $n = 2$ (8 × 1/8 corners + 1 body center)
- **Step 2:** Atoms touch along **body diagonal** → $4r = \sqrt{3}\,a$
- **Step 3:** Solve for $a$:

$$a = \frac{4r}{\sqrt{3}}$$

- **Step 4:** Compute APF:

$$\text{APF} = \frac{2 \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3}{\left(\frac{4r}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^3} = \frac{\frac{8}{3}\pi r^3}{\frac{64 r^3}{3\sqrt{3}}} = \frac{8\pi r^3 \cdot 3\sqrt{3}}{3 \cdot 64 r^3} = \frac{\sqrt{3}\,\pi}{8} \approx 0.680$$

---

### 2.1.3 Face-Centered Cubic (FCC) — APF Derivation Logic
- **Step 1:** $n = 4$ (8 × 1/8 corners + 6 × 1/2 faces)
- **Step 2:** Atoms touch along **face diagonal** → $4r = \sqrt{2}\,a$
- **Step 3:** Solve for $a$:

$$a = \frac{4r}{\sqrt{2}} = 2\sqrt{2}\,r$$

- **Step 4:** Compute APF:

$$\text{APF} = \frac{4 \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3}{(2\sqrt{2}\,r)^3} = \frac{\frac{16}{3}\pi r^3}{16\sqrt{2}\,r^3} = \frac{\pi}{3\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.740$$

---

### 2.1.4 Hexagonal Close-Packed (HCP) — APF Derivation Logic
- **Step 1:** $n = 6$ (12 × 1/6 corners on top + bottom + 3 atoms in middle layer + 2 face centers inside cell)
- **Step 2:** Ideal c/a ratio:

$$\frac{c}{a} = \sqrt{\frac{8}{3}} \approx 1.633$$

- **Step 3:** APF:

$$\text{APF} = \frac{6 \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3}{a^2 \cdot c \cdot \sin 60°} = \frac{8\pi}{\sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{c}{a} \cdot 3} \approx 0.740$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** FCC and HCP have **identical packing efficiency (74%)** — both are **close-packed** structures. Only the stacking sequence differs (ABCABC for FCC, ABAB for HCP).

---

## 2.2 Void Analysis in Unit Cells

Understanding voids is critical for understanding **interstitial solid solutions** (e.g., C in Fe — carbon occupies octahedral voids in austenite).

### 2.2.1 Octahedral Voids
- **SC:** 1 void per unit cell, at body center
- **BCC:** 3 voids per unit cell
- **FCC:** 4 voids per unit cell, located at edge centers and body center
- **HCP:** 6 voids per unit cell
- **Radius of void vs. atom:** $r_{\text{void}} = 0.414\,R$ (FCC)

### 2.2.2 Tetrahedral Voids
- **FCC:** 8 voids per unit cell
- **HCP:** 12 voids per unit cell
- **Radius of void vs. atom:** $r_{\text{void}} = 0.225\,R$ (FCC)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** In FCC iron (austenite), carbon atoms occupy **octahedral voids** (not tetrahedral) because C atoms (radius 0.077 nm) are too large for tetrahedral voids in γ-Fe (which would require C radius ≤ 0.225 × R_Fe ≈ 0.028 nm — too small).

---

## 2.3 Density of Unit Cell Formula

The theoretical density of a crystalline material is:

$$\rho = \frac{n \cdot M}{V_C \cdot N_A} = \frac{n \cdot M}{a^3 \cdot N_A}$$

where:
- $n$ = atoms per unit cell
- $M$ = molar mass (kg/mol or g/mol)
- $V_C$ = volume of unit cell = $a^3$ for cubic
- $N_A$ = Avogadro's number = **6.022 × 10²³ atoms/mol**

---

## 2.4 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

| # | Concept | Formula | Typical Units / Values |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | APF (general) | $\text{APF} = \dfrac{n \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3}{V_C}$ | dimensionless, 0 to 1 |
| 2 | APF (SC) | $\text{APF} = \dfrac{\pi}{6}$ | 0.524 |
| 3 | APF (BCC) | $\text{APF} = \dfrac{\pi\sqrt{3}}{8}$ | 0.680 |
| 4 | APF (FCC) | $\text{APF} = \dfrac{\pi}{3\sqrt{2}}$ | 0.740 |
| 5 | APF (HCP, ideal) | $\text{APF} = \dfrac{\pi}{3\sqrt{2}}$ | 0.740 |
| 6 | BCC: a vs r | $a = \dfrac{4r}{\sqrt{3}}$ | $a \approx 2.31\,r$ |
| 7 | FCC: a vs r | $a = 2\sqrt{2}\,r$ | $a \approx 2.83\,r$ |
| 8 | HCP: c/a ideal | $\dfrac{c}{a} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8}{3}}$ | 1.633 |
| 9 | Density | $\rho = \dfrac{nM}{a^3 N_A}$ | g/cm³ or kg/m³ |
| 10 | Octahedral void radius | $r_{\text{oct}} = 0.414\,R$ | FCC |
| 11 | Tetrahedral void radius | $r_{\text{tet}} = 0.225\,R$ | FCC |
| 12 | Radius ratio — Triangular | $0.155 \leq r/R < 0.225$ | CN = 3 |
| 13 | Radius ratio — Tetrahedral | $0.225 \leq r/R < 0.414$ | CN = 4 |
| 14 | Radius ratio — Octahedral | $0.414 \leq r/R < 0.732$ | CN = 6 |
| 15 | Radius ratio — Cubic | $0.732 \leq r/R \leq 1.0$ | CN = 8 |
| 16 | Hume-Rothery size rule | $\dfrac{\vert r_1 - r_2 \vert}{r_1} \times 100\% \leq 15\%$ | — |
| 17 | Eutectoid composition (Fe-C) | $0.77\,\text{wt\% C}$ at 727°C | — |
| 18 | Eutectic composition (Fe-C) | $4.30\,\text{wt\% C}$ at 1147°C | — |
| 19 | Max C in α-Fe | $0.022\,\text{wt\%}$ at 727°C | — |
| 20 | Max C in γ-Fe | $2.14\,\text{wt\%}$ at 1147°C | — |
| 21 | Cementite Fe₃C | $6.67\,\text{wt\% C}$ | — |
| 22 | Pearlite composition | $88\%\,\alpha\text{-ferrite} + 12\%\,\text{Fe}_3\text{C}$ | — |
| 23 | Eutectoid fraction (Pearlite) — lever rule | $W_{\text{pearlite}} = \dfrac{C_0 - C_\alpha}{C_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}} - C_\alpha}$ | — |
| 24 | Proeutectoid ferrite fraction | $W_\alpha = \dfrac{C_{\text{eutectoid}} - C_0}{C_{\text{eutectoid}} - C_\alpha}$ | — |
| 25 | Proeutectoid cementite fraction | $W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}} = \dfrac{C_0 - C_{\text{eutectoid}}}{C_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}} - C_{\text{eutectoid}}}$ | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lever Rule (KTU Hot Topic):** At any two-phase region, the *fraction* of each phase is computed by drawing a horizontal "tie-line" at the given temperature, and the fraction of one phase = (length of opposite segment) / (total tie-line length).

---

## 2.5 Phase Diagram Reading — Step Logic

For a binary phase diagram (T vs composition):

1. **Identify the system** from the diagram — is it isomorphous (Cu-Ni), eutectic (Pb-Sn), or peritectic?
2. **Locate the alloy composition** $C_0$ on the x-axis.
3. **Draw a vertical line** at $C_0$ — this is the **alloy line**.
4. **Draw horizontal tie-lines** at temperatures of interest — these intersect the phase boundaries.
5. **Apply the lever rule** between tie-line endpoints to compute phase fractions.
6. **For microstructural constituents** (e.g., proeutectoid + eutectoid), apply lever rule across the entire two-phase region at room temperature.

---

## 2.6 Engineering Utility of These Concepts

| Concept | Real-World Engineering Application |
|---|---|
| Crystal structure (BCC/FCC/HCP) | Determines ductility, formability, magnetic properties |
| APF | Predicts density, mechanical strength, diffusion rates |
| Solid solutions | Design of alloys (brass, bronze, stainless steel) |
| Hume-Rothery rules | Predict alloy compatibility before melting — saves cost |
| Fe-Fe₃C diagram | Foundation for every steel specification (AISI/SAE grades) |
| Heat treatment | Tailors hardness for gears, springs, cutting tools |
| Surface hardening | Shafts, cams, gears — wear-resistant skin + tough core |

---

## 2.7 Heat Treatment Comparison Matrix

| Treatment | Heating Temperature | Cooling Medium | Resulting Microstructure | Hardness (HRC approx.) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Annealing | Above A₃ + 30°C | Furnace (slow) | Coarse pearlite + ferrite | 10–20 |
| Normalizing | Above A₃ + 50°C | Still air | Fine pearlite + ferrite | 20–30 |
| Hardening (Quenching) | Above A₃ | Water/oil/polymer | Martensite | 55–65 |
| Spheroidizing | Just below A₁ (700°C) | Furnace | Spheroidal Fe₃C in ferrite | 5–10 (softest) |
| Tempering (low) | 150–250°C | Air | Tempered martensite (ε-carbide) | 55–60 |
| Tempering (high) | 400–650°C | Air | Tempered martensite (coarse Fe₃C) | 25–35 |

---

## 2.8 Surface Hardening Process Parameters

### 2.8.1 Flame Hardening
- **Heat source:** Oxy-fuel flame (~3000°C)
- **Procedure:** Surface heated to austenite → quenched by water spray
- **Pros:** Low equipment cost, portable, suitable for large parts
- **Cons:** Manual skill dependent, less precise depth control

### 2.8.2 Induction Hardening
- **Heat source:** High-frequency AC coil (kHz range)
- **Procedure:** Eddy currents heat surface by skin effect → quench
- **Depth:** Controlled by frequency (higher freq → shallower depth)
- **Pros:** Fast, clean, controllable, repeatable
- **Cons:** Specialized coil needed for each part geometry

### 2.8.3 Laser Hardening
- **Heat source:** CO₂ or Nd:YAG laser beam
- **Procedure:** Surface heated by laser scanning → self-quench by bulk
- **Depth:** 0.5–2 mm
- **Pros:** Highly localized, minimal distortion, can target specific spots
- **Cons:** Equipment cost, reflective surface issue

### 2.8.4 Electron Beam Hardening
- **Heat source:** Focused electron beam (in vacuum)
- **Procedure:** Beam scans surface; vacuum prevents oxidation
- **Depth:** Very shallow (0.1–1 mm)
- **Pros:** Extremely precise, no oxidation
- **Cons:** Must be done in vacuum chamber — limits part size

### 2.8.5 Carburizing
- **Mechanism:** Carbon diffusion into surface at 850–950°C
- **Variants:** Gas (CH₄/C₃H₈), Liquid (cyanide bath), Pack (charcoal + BaCO₃), Vacuum (low pressure)
- **Result:** Surface C content ↑ from ~0.2% to 0.8–1.0% → high-carbon martensite after quench

### 2.8.6 Nitriding
- **Mechanism:** N diffusion from NH₃ gas at 500–550°C (below A₁ — no austenitization!)
- **Compounds formed:** Fe₄N, Fe₂₃N (extremely hard, ~1000 HV)
- **Advantages over carburizing:**
  - No quenching needed (already hard)
  - Lower distortion
  - Higher surface hardness
  - Better wear and fatigue resistance
- **Disadvantages:**
  - Slower (long cycle time)
  - Limited to special nitriding steels (containing Cr, Mo, Al, V)

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 EXHAUSTIVE DERIVATION: BCC Atomic Packing Factor

### Problem Statement
Derive the atomic packing factor (APF) for the Body-Centered Cubic (BCC) crystal structure, starting from first principles.

### Step 1: Determine the number of atoms per unit cell ($n$)

In BCC, atoms are located at:
- **8 corner positions**, each shared between **8 unit cells** → contribution = $\frac{1}{8}$ per corner

$$n_{\text{corner}} = 8 \times \frac{1}{8} = 1 \text{ atom}$$

- **1 body-center position**, fully enclosed in the unit cell → contribution = 1

$$n_{\text{center}} = 1 \times 1 = 1 \text{ atom}$$

- **Total atoms per unit cell:**

$$n = n_{\text{corner}} + n_{\text{center}} = 1 + 1 = 2$$

### Step 2: Establish the geometric relationship between lattice parameter $a$ and atomic radius $r$

In a BCC unit cell, the corner atoms do **not** touch each other directly along the cube edges. Instead, the **body-centered atom touches the corner atoms along the body diagonal**.

The length of the body diagonal of a cube with edge $a$ is:

$$d_{\text{body}} = \sqrt{a^2 + a^2 + a^2} = \sqrt{3\,a^2} = a\sqrt{3}$$

Along this body diagonal, the following atoms are encountered in sequence:
1. Half of a corner atom (radius $r$)
2. Full body-centered atom (diameter $2r$)
3. Half of the opposite corner atom (radius $r$)

Hence:

$$a\sqrt{3} = 4r$$

Solving for $a$:

$$a = \frac{4r}{\sqrt{3}}$$

### Step 3: Compute the volume of atoms in the unit cell

The volume of a single spherical atom is:

$$V_{\text{atom}} = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3$$

Total volume occupied by atoms in one unit cell:

$$V_{\text{atoms}} = n \cdot V_{\text{atom}} = 2 \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 = \frac{8}{3}\pi r^3$$

### Step 4: Compute the volume of the unit cell

$$V_{\text{cell}} = a^3 = \left(\frac{4r}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^3 = \frac{64\,r^3}{3\sqrt{3}}$$

### Step 5: Compute the APF

$$\text{APF} = \frac{V_{\text{atoms}}}{V_{\text{cell}}} = \frac{\frac{8}{3}\pi r^3}{\frac{64\,r^3}{3\sqrt{3}}}$$

Cancel common factors ($\frac{r^3}{3}$):

$$\text{APF} = \frac{8\pi}{\frac{64}{\sqrt{3}}} = \frac{8\pi\sqrt{3}}{64} = \frac{\pi\sqrt{3}}{8}$$

Numerical evaluation:

$$\text{APF} = \frac{3.14159 \times 1.73205}{8} = \frac{5.44139}{8} \approx 0.6802$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** APF (BCC) = $\dfrac{\pi\sqrt{3}}{8} \approx 0.680$ or **68.02%**.

---

## 3.2 EXHAUSTIVE DERIVATION: FCC Atomic Packing Factor

### Step 1: Atoms per unit cell

$$n = 8 \times \frac{1}{8} + 6 \times \frac{1}{2} = 1 + 3 = 4$$

### Step 2: Geometry

In FCC, atoms touch along the **face diagonal**:

$$4r = \sqrt{2}\,a \implies a = \frac{4r}{\sqrt{2}} = 2\sqrt{2}\,r$$

### Step 3: Volumes

$$V_{\text{atoms}} = 4 \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 = \frac{16}{3}\pi r^3$$

$$V_{\text{cell}} = (2\sqrt{2}\,r)^3 = 16\sqrt{2}\,r^3$$

### Step 4: APF

$$\text{APF} = \frac{\frac{16}{3}\pi r^3}{16\sqrt{2}\,r^3} = \frac{\pi}{3\sqrt{2}}$$

Rationalize:

$$\text{APF} = \frac{\pi\sqrt{2}}{6} \approx \frac{3.14159 \times 1.41421}{6} = \frac{4.44288}{6} \approx 0.7405$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** APF (FCC) = $\dfrac{\pi}{3\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.740$ or **74.05%**.

---

## 3.3 EXHAUSTIVE DERIVATION: Density of Copper (FCC)

### Problem
Given: Cu has FCC structure, atomic radius $r = 0.1278$ nm, atomic mass $M = 63.54$ g/mol, Avogadro number $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ atoms/mol. Find the theoretical density and compare with experimental value (8.94 g/cm³).

### Step 1: Lattice parameter

For FCC:

$$a = 2\sqrt{2}\,r = 2\sqrt{2} \times 0.1278 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{cm} = 3.6147 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{cm}$$

### Step 2: Volume of unit cell

$$V_C = a^3 = (3.6147 \times 10^{-8})^3 = 4.7256 \times 10^{-23}\,\text{cm}^3$$

### Step 3: Apply density formula

$$\rho = \frac{nM}{V_C N_A} = \frac{4 \times 63.54}{4.7256 \times 10^{-23} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}}$$

$$\rho = \frac{254.16}{28.456} \approx 8.93\,\text{g/cm}^3$$

### Step 4: Compare with experimental

Theoretical = **8.93 g/cm³** vs. Experimental = **8.94 g/cm³** — agreement within 0.1% ✓

---

## 3.4 EXHAUSTIVE DERIVATION: Lever Rule Applied to Fe-C Hypoeutectoid Steel

### Problem
A plain carbon steel contains **0.45 wt% C**. It is slowly cooled from austenitic region to just below the eutectoid temperature (727°C). Calculate the:
1. Weight fraction of total ferrite and total cementite
2. Weight fraction of proeutectoid ferrite and pearlite

### Step 1: Identify positions on the diagram

At 727°C (just below eutectoid):
- Composition of ferrite: $C_\alpha = 0.022\,\text{wt\% C}$
- Composition of cementite: $C_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}} = 6.67\,\text{wt\% C}$
- Eutectoid composition: $C_{\text{eutectoid}} = 0.77\,\text{wt\% C}$
- Alloy composition: $C_0 = 0.45\,\text{wt\% C}$

Since $0.022 < 0.45 < 0.77$ → this is a **hypoeutectoid steel**.

### Step 2: Weight fraction of proeutectoid ferrite

Using lever rule between ferrite ($C_\alpha = 0.022$) and eutectoid pearlite (treated as a single "constituent" at $C_{\text{eutectoid}} = 0.77$):

$$W_{\text{proeutectoid }\alpha} = \frac{C_{\text{eutectoid}} - C_0}{C_{\text{eutectoid}} - C_\alpha} = \frac{0.77 - 0.45}{0.77 - 0.022}$$

$$W_{\text{proeutectoid }\alpha} = \frac{0.32}{0.748} = 0.4278 \approx 42.8\%$$

### Step 3: Weight fraction of pearlite

$$W_{\text{pearlite}} = 1 - W_{\text{proeutectoid }\alpha} = 1 - 0.4278 = 0.5722 \approx 57.2\%$$

### Step 4: Weight fraction of total ferrite (within pearlite + proeutectoid)

Ferrite inside pearlite is 88% of pearlite:

$$W_{\text{eutectoid }\alpha} = 0.88 \times 0.5722 = 0.5035 \approx 50.4\%$$

Total ferrite:

$$W_{\alpha,\text{total}} = W_{\text{proeutectoid }\alpha} + W_{\text{eutectoid }\alpha} = 0.4278 + 0.5035 = 0.9313 \approx 93.1\%$$

### Step 5: Weight fraction of total cementite

$$W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C},\text{total}} = 1 - 0.9313 = 0.0687 \approx 6.9\%$$

### Verification via direct lever rule on full $(\alpha + \text{Fe}_3\text{C})$ region

$$W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}} = \frac{C_0 - C_\alpha}{C_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}} - C_\alpha} = \frac{0.45 - 0.022}{6.67 - 0.022} = \frac{0.428}{6.648} = 0.0644 \approx 6.4\%$$

$$W_{\alpha,\text{total}} = \frac{C_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}} - C_0}{C_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}} - C_\alpha} = \frac{6.67 - 0.45}{6.648} = \frac{6.22}{6.648} = 0.9356 \approx 93.6\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Small discrepancy** arises because pearlite is treated as a microconstituent vs. treating individual phases. Both approaches are accepted in KTU valuation.

---

## 3.5 Hume-Rothery Size Rule Application

### Problem
Predict whether Cu (r = 0.128 nm, FCC) and Zn (r = 0.133 nm, HCP) will form a substantial solid solution.

### Step 1: Compute percentage size difference

$$\%\Delta r = \frac{\vert r_{\text{Zn}} - r_{\text{Cu}} \vert}{r_{\text{Cu}}} \times 100\% = \frac{\vert 0.133 - 0.128 \vert}{0.128} \times 100\%$$

$$\%\Delta r = \frac{0.005}{0.128} \times 100\% = 3.91\%$$

### Step 2: Check against Hume-Rothery rule

Rule says $\%\Delta r \leq 15\%$. We have **3.91% < 15%** ✓ → **PASS**

### Step 3: Check other rules (qualitative)
- **Crystal structure:** Cu is FCC, Zn is HCP — **FAIL** (different structures)
- **Valency:** Cu = +1/+2, Zn = +2 — close, partial pass
- **Electronegativity:** Cu = 1.90, Zn = 1.65 — close ✓
- **Compound formation:** Brass (Cu-Zn) does form some ordered phases (CuZn) — partial concern

### Conclusion
Despite a small size mismatch, **Cu and Zn do form a substitutional solid solution (brass)** over a wide range (up to ~40% Zn in FCC α-brass), but the solubility is **not complete** because the structures differ. The FCC phase (α-brass) accommodates Zn up to ~39%, while HCP Zn (η-phase) accommodates little Cu — explaining the limited terminal solubilities.

---

## 3.6 Critical Cooling Rate & TTT Concepts

While not explicitly derivable, the conceptual framework:

$$\text{TTT (Time-Temperature-Transformation) Diagram}$$
- **Nose of curve:** Critical cooling rate (CCR) — fastest cooling that just avoids nose
- Above CCR → **Martensite** forms (diffusionless)
- Below CCR → **Pearlite/Bainite** (diffusion-controlled)

For a hardenable steel:

$$\text{CCR} = \frac{T_{\text{austenite}} - T_{\text{nose}}}{t_{\text{nose}}}$$

Hardenability = ability to form martensite at a given depth — measured by **Jominy End-Quench Test**.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define polymorphism and allotropy. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

**Polymorphism:** The ability of a solid material (element or compound) to exist in more than one crystal structure depending on temperature and/or pressure conditions.

**Allotropy:** A specific case of polymorphism that applies *only to pure elemental solids*, where the different crystal forms of the same element are termed allotropes.

**Examples:**
- **Polymorphism:** SiC (silicon carbide) exists in many crystal forms (3C, 4H, 6H polytypes).
- **Allotropy:** Iron exhibits BCC α-Fe (room temp), FCC γ-Fe (912–1394°C), BCC δ-Fe (1394–1538°C).

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**What is a solid solution? Distinguish between substitutional and interstitial solid solutions.**

**Model Answer:**

A **solid solution** is a homogeneous crystalline phase in which atoms of one element (solute) are incorporated into the crystal lattice of another element (solvent) without forming a new distinct phase.

| Feature | Substitutional | Interstitial |
|---|---|---|
| Location of solute | Replaces solvent atom at lattice site | Occupies void (interstice) between solvent atoms |
| Size of solute atom | Comparable to solvent atom (<15% size diff.) | Much smaller (e.g., C, N, H) |
| Examples | Cu-Ni, Cu-Zn (brass), Au-Cu | Fe-C (austenite), Fe-N, Pd-H |
| Solubility | Often limited by Hume-Rothery rules | Highly dependent on available voids |

---

## 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Module 1]**

**(a)** Derive the atomic packing factor (APF) for the **FCC** crystal structure. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the **Iron-Iron Carbide (Fe-Fe₃C) equilibrium diagram**. Label all critical temperatures, phases, and microconstituents. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a): APF of FCC

**Step 1:** Atoms per unit cell:
$$n = 8 \times \frac{1}{8} + 6 \times \frac{1}{2} = 4$$

**Step 2:** Atoms touch along face diagonal → $4r = a\sqrt{2}$, giving $a = 2\sqrt{2}\,r$.

**Step 3:** Volume of atoms in unit cell:
$$V_{\text{atoms}} = 4 \times \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 = \frac{16}{3}\pi r^3$$

**Step 4:** Volume of unit cell:
$$V_{\text{cell}} = a^3 = (2\sqrt{2}\,r)^3 = 16\sqrt{2}\,r^3$$

**Step 5:** APF calculation:
$$\text{APF} = \frac{V_{\text{atoms}}}{V_{\text{cell}}} = \frac{\frac{16}{3}\pi r^3}{16\sqrt{2}\,r^3} = \frac{\pi}{3\sqrt{2}} = \frac{\pi\sqrt{2}}{6}$$

$$\text{APF} \approx 0.740 \text{ or } 74.0\%$$

**Engineering significance:** This high packing factor explains why FCC metals (Cu, Al, Ni, Au) are generally **ductile and malleable** — closely packed planes can easily slide.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b): Fe-Fe₃C Equilibrium Diagram

**Diagram Description (text-based for student to sketch):**

The diagram has **X-axis = wt% C (0 to 6.67)** and **Y-axis = Temperature (°C, 0 to 1600)**.

**Key features to label:**

1. **Liquid (L)** region at top, above liquidus line.
2. **Peritectic reaction** at **1493°C, 0.16 wt% C:**
$$\text{L} + \delta\text{-Fe} \rightarrow \gamma\text{-Fe (austenite)}$$
3. **Eutectic reaction** at **1147°C, 4.30 wt% C:**
$$\text{L} \rightarrow \gamma\text{-Fe} + \text{Fe}_3\text{C} \quad (\text{forms Ledeburite})$$
4. **Eutectoid reaction** at **727°C, 0.77 wt% C:**
$$\gamma\text{-Fe} \rightarrow \alpha\text{-Fe} + \text{Fe}_3\text{C} \quad (\text{forms Pearlite})$$
5. **A₁ line** at 727°C (horizontal) — eutectoid temperature.
6. **A₃ line** (curving down from 912°C at pure Fe to 727°C at 0.77% C) — start of ferrite formation on cooling.
7. **Acm line** (curving down from 727°C at 2.14% C to ~6.67% at room temp) — cementite solubility limit in austenite.
8. **Solid solubility lines:**
   - Max C in γ-Fe (austenite) = **2.14% at 1147°C**
   - Max C in α-Fe (ferrite) = **0.022% at 727°C**

**Phase regions:**
- α (BCC ferrite): narrow region at left
- γ (FCC austenite): large central region
- Fe₃C (cementite): right boundary
- L (liquid): top

**Microconstituents to identify:**
- Pearlite (727°C, 0.77% C) — eutectoid
- Ledeburite (1147°C, 4.30% C) — eutectic
- Proeutectoid ferrite (left of eutectoid)
- Proeutectoid cementite (right of eutectoid)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE
**[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Supplementary]**

**(a)** What are **Hume-Rothery's rules**? Apply them to predict the solid solubility between **Copper (FCC)** and **Zinc (HCP)**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the following heat treatments with respect to a eutectoid steel:
   (i) Annealing
   (ii) Normalizing
   (iii) Hardening (Quenching)
   (iv) Tempering

For each, state the heating temperature, cooling medium, and resulting microstructure. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a): Hume-Rothery's Rules for Cu-Zn

**The Five Hume-Rothery Rules:**

1. **Atomic size factor:** Atomic radii of solute and solvent should differ by no more than 15%.
2. **Crystal structure:** Both elements should have the same crystal structure.
3. **Valency:** Both elements should have the same valence (or close valence).
4. **Electronegativity:** Should be similar to avoid compound formation.
5. **Chemical affinity:** Should not form intermediate compounds.

**Application to Cu (FCC, r = 0.128 nm, valency +2) and Zn (HCP, r = 0.133 nm, valency +2):**

| Rule | Analysis | Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Size | $\%\Delta r = \frac{0.005}{0.128} \times 100\% = 3.9\%$ | ✓ Pass |
| 2. Structure | Cu (FCC) ≠ Zn (HCP) | ✗ Fail |
| 3. Valency | Both typically +2 | ✓ Pass |
| 4. Electronegativity | Cu = 1.90, Zn = 1.65 | ✓ Close |
| 5. Compound formation | Ordered CuZn (β-brass) forms | ✗ Partial fail |

**Conclusion:** Cu and Zn exhibit **substantial but limited substitutional solid solubility**. FCC α-brass accommodates up to ~39% Zn; HCP η-phase accommodates little Cu. The differing crystal structures explain why **complete miscibility is not achieved** despite favorable size and valency.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b): Heat Treatments of Eutectoid Steel

| Treatment | Heating Range | Holding | Cooling | Resulting Microstructure |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Annealing** | Above A₃ (~750–800°C for eutectoid) | Until uniform | Furnace cool (slow) | **Coarse pearlite** — soft, ductile |
| **Normalizing** | Above Acm (~760°C) | Until uniform | **Still air** (faster than furnace) | **Fine pearlite** — harder than annealed, finer grain |
| **Hardening (Quench)** | Above Acm (~760°C) | Until austenitized | **Rapid quench in water/oil/polymer** | **Martensite** (BCT structure) — extremely hard, very brittle |
| **Tempering** | Below A₁ (150–650°C) | 1–2 hours | Air cool | **Tempered martensite** — hard but tough (Fe₃C particles dispersed in α-ferrite) |

**Key metallurgical observations:**
- Annealing → softest condition, ideal for machining.
- Normalizing → refines grain after hot working, slightly harder.
- Hardening alone → too brittle for practical use, hence **always followed by tempering**.
- Tempering temperature inversely proportional to hardness retained.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
>
> **Common mistakes students make that cost 2–4 marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing A₃, Acm, A₁ lines on the Fe-C diagram.** Memorize: A₁ = 727°C (eutectoid horizontal), A₃ = γ→α ferrite boundary (hypoeutectoid side), Acm = γ→Fe₃C boundary (hypereutectoid side).
>
> 2. **Quenching temperature errors:** Hypoeutectoid steel requires heating above A₃ (NOT Acm). Hypereutectoid steel requires heating above A₁ only — heating above Acm would dissolve all cementite and cause excessive retained austenite after quench.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to apply lever rule** in fraction calculations — KTU examiners explicitly allocate 2–3 marks for the lever rule setup.
>
> 4. **Mixing up "proeutectoid ferrite" with "eutectoid ferrite"** in microconstituent calculations.
>
> 5. **Nitriding vs Carburizing:** Don't write "nitriding is faster than carburizing" — actually, nitriding is **slower** but offers higher surface hardness without needing quenching.
>
> 6. **Pearlite composition wrong:** Many students write "50% ferrite + 50% cementite" — correct answer is **88% ferrite + 12% cementite**.
>
> 7. **HCP: stating c/a = 1.5 or 1.0 — wrong.** Ideal ratio is $\sqrt{8/3} \approx 1.633$.
>
> 8. **APF for SC = π/6 ≈ 0.524** — many students write 0.5 or π/4. Be precise.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1 (Materials & Manufacturing)**

### Crystallography Fundamentals
- [x] **Crystal** = long-range ordered solid; **Amorphous** = no long-range order.
- [x] **Space lattice** = mathematical array of points; **14 Bravais lattices** in 7 crystal systems.
- [x] **Unit cell** = smallest repeat unit; defined by $a, b, c, \alpha, \beta, \gamma$.
- [x] **BCC:** n=2, CN=8, APF=0.68, a=4r/√3.
- [x] **FCC:** n=4, CN=12, APF=0.74, a=2√2·r.
- [x] **HCP:** CN=12, APF=0.74, c/a=1.633, ABAB stacking.
- [x] **SC:** n=1, CN=6, APF=0.52 (rare in metals).

### Radius Ratio Rule
- [x] 0.155–0.225 → CN 3 (triangular)
- [x] 0.225–0.414 → CN 4 (tetrahedral)
- [x] 0.414–0.732 → CN 6 (octahedral)
- [x] 0.732–1.0 → CN 8 (cubic)

### Polymorphism vs Allotropy
- [x] Polymorphism = generic term; **Allotropy** = polymorphism in pure elements.
- [x] Fe has 3 allotropes (α, γ, δ). C has many (diamond, graphite, fullerene).

### Solid Solutions & Hume-Rothery
- [x] Substitutional (large atoms replace); Interstitial (small atoms in voids).
- [x] **5 Hume-Rothery Rules:** Size ≤15%, same structure, same valence, similar electronegativity, no compound formation.
- [x] Cu-Ni: **isomorphous** (complete solid solubility — both FCC, similar size).
- [x] Pb-Sn: **eutectic** system at 183°C, 61.9% Sn.

### Iron-Carbon Diagram (MOST CRITICAL)
- [x] **Eutectoid:** 727°C, 0.77% C → Pearlite (α + Fe₃C).
- [x] **Eutectic:** 1147°C, 4.30% C → Ledeburite (γ + Fe₃C).
- [x] **Max C in α:** 0.022% at 727°C.
- [x] **Max C in γ:** 2.14% at 1147°C.
- [x] **Cementite:** 6.67% C, hard/brittle Fe₃C.
- [x] **Pearlite composition:** 88% ferrite + 12% cementite.
- [x] **Hypoeutectoid** < 0.77% C; **Hypereutectoid** 0.77%–2.14% C.

### Heat Treatments — Steel
- [x] **Annealing** = heat above A₃, furnace cool → coarse pearlite.
- [x] **Normalizing** = heat above A₃, air cool → fine pearlite.
- [x] **Hardening** = heat above A₃, water/oil quench → martensite (BCT).
- [x] **Spheroidizing** = heat just below A₁, long hold → globular Fe₃C (softest).
- [x] **Tempering** = reheat martensite below A₁ → tempered martensite (tough + hard).

### Surface Hardening
- [x] **No change methods:** Flame, Induction, Laser, Electron Beam.
- [x] **Change methods:** Carburizing (C, 850–950°C, requires quench), Nitriding (N, 500–550°C, no quench).
- [x] Nitriding gives higher hardness (~1000 HV) but needs special steels (Al, Cr, Mo content).

### Critical Formula Recap
- [x] APF (FCC) = π/(3√2) ≈ 0.74
- [x] APF (BCC) = π√3/8 ≈ 0.68
- [x] APF (SC) = π/6 ≈ 0.52
- [x] Density ρ = nM/(a³N_A)
- [x] Lever rule: W₁ = (C₂ − C₀)/(C₂ − C₁)

### Last-Minute Memory Tricks
- [x] **"BCC has body, FCC has faces, SC has only sides"**
- [x] **"FCC & HCP are tied at 74% packing — only stacking differs"**
- [x] **"Iron's alphabet soup: α (BCC) → γ (FCC) → δ (BCC) — like the Iron Triangle"**
- [x] **"Pearlite is platy like a fingerprint; Bainite is feathery; Martensite is needle-like"**
- [x] **"Temper martensite to make it tough — not hard, but useful"**
- [x] **"Carburize above A₁ and quench; Nitride below A₁ and don't quench"**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT303 - MATERIALS AND MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Applications of Engineering Materials & Manufacturing Processes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

In the context of **PCRUT303 – Materials and Manufacturing Processes**, the term **"Applications"** refers to the systematic study of *where, why, and how* engineering materials (metals, alloys, polymers, ceramics, and composites) and primary manufacturing processes (casting, forming, joining, machining, and powder metallurgy) are deployed across real-world industries. Selection is driven by the interplay of **mechanical properties**, **physical properties**, **chemical resistance**, **cost**, **availability**, and **manufacturability** for a given service condition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Applications* is the branch of materials engineering that maps the mechanical, thermal, electrical, and chemical behavior of a material/process to a specific functional requirement — load-bearing capacity, wear resistance, corrosion protection, thermal insulation, electrical conduction, or aesthetic finishing — in sectors such as aerospace, automotive, marine, biomedical, energy, and structural civil engineering.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a doctor prescribing medicine. The doctor does not randomly select a drug; instead, he/she matches the **symptoms (service conditions)** with the **drug's effect (material properties)**. Similarly, an engineer does not pick aluminum or steel arbitrarily — they prescribe a material based on the **"disease" of the application**:

- A bridge leg that must carry huge compressive loads → **High compressive strength** → *Cast iron or structural steel*.
- An aircraft skin that must be **light + strong** → *Aluminum alloys or carbon-fiber composites*.
- A chemical tank that must resist **acid corrosion** → *Stainless steel or PTFE-lined carbon steel*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:**
> The right material in the wrong application = failure. The wrong material in the right application = economic loss. The objective of this topic is to teach *systematic selection logic*, not just memorize lists.

### Fundamental Performance Metrics (KTU Standard)

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ultimate Tensile Strength | UTS | **MPa** | Maximum stress before fracture |
| Yield Strength | $\sigma_y$ | **MPa** | Onset of plastic deformation |
| Hardness | HV / HB / HRC | **kgf/mm² or Rockwell number** | Resistance to indentation/wear |
| Density | $\rho$ | **g/cm³** | Mass per unit volume |
| Modulus of Elasticity | E | **GPa** | Stiffness under elastic load |
| Thermal Conductivity | k | **W/m·K** | Heat transfer rate |
| Corrosion Rate | CR | **mm/year (mpy)** | Material degradation in service |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always quote material properties with their **standard test condition** (e.g., "as-cast", "heat-treated to T6", "annealed at 650 °C"). KTU examiners frequently deduct marks when units or conditions are missing.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The selection of a material for any application follows a **4-stage decision funnel**:

### Stage 1 — Functional Requirement Analysis
Identify the **dominant service demand**:
- **Mechanical**: Static load, dynamic load, impact, fatigue, creep.
- **Thermal**: High temperature (creep regime), thermal shock, cryogenic.
- **Chemical**: Oxidation, aqueous corrosion, acidic/alkaline attack.
- **Electrical**: Conductor, insulator, semiconductor.
- **Special**: Wear, biocompatibility, optical transparency, magnetic.

### Stage 2 — Property Translation
Translate the service demand into **target properties**:

| Service Demand | Target Property | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| Load-bearing structural member | High $\sigma_y$ + high E | $\sigma_y \geq 250$ MPa |
| Lightweight mobility (auto/aero) | Low $\rho$ + high $\sigma_y / \rho$ | Specific strength $\geq 200$ kNm/kg |
| High-temperature turbine | Creep resistance, oxidation resistance | T service $\geq 800$ °C |
| Marine / chemical plant | Corrosion resistance in Cl⁻ / H₂SO₄ | CR $\leq 0.1$ mm/yr |
| Cutting tool | Hot hardness, wear resistance | Hardness $\geq 60$ HRC |
| Electrical transmission | Low resistivity | $\rho \leq 1.7 \times 10^{-8}$ Ω·m |
| Biomedical implant | Biocompatibility, fatigue strength | Non-toxic + $\sigma_f \geq 500$ MPa |

### Stage 3 — Material Family Selection
Match the target property to the appropriate material family:

- **Ferrous** → When strength, stiffness, and cost dominate.
- **Non-ferrous** → When weight, corrosion, or conductivity dominates.
- **Polymers** → When chemical inertness, low density, or electrical insulation dominates.
- **Ceramics** → When high temperature, hardness, or wear dominates.
- **Composites** → When a combination (e.g., light + strong + stiff) is needed.

### Stage 4 — Process Selection
Choose a manufacturing route based on:
- **Geometry complexity** → Casting / Powder Metallurgy / Polymer molding.
- **Strength requirement** → Forging / Rolling / Heat treatment.
- **Dimensional accuracy** → Machining / Precision casting.
- **Production volume** → Mass production → Stamping / Die casting / Injection molding.

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Application Context |
|---|---|---|---|
| Specific Strength | $SS = \dfrac{\sigma_{UTS}}{\rho}$ | $\sigma_{UTS}$ in MPa, $\rho$ in g/cm³ | Lightweight design (aerospace) |
| Specific Stiffness | $SM = \dfrac{E}{\rho}$ | E in GPa, $\rho$ in g/cm³ | Aerospace stiffness selection |
| Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number | $PREN = \%Cr + 3.3 \times \%Mo + 16 \times \%N$ | Composition in wt% | Stainless steel grade selection |
| Corrosion Rate (weight loss) | $CR = \dfrac{K \cdot W}{A \cdot T \cdot D}$ | mm/year | Material durability ranking |
| Safety Factor | $SF = \dfrac{\sigma_{yield}}{\sigma_{working}}$ | Dimensionless | Design reliability check |
| Creep Rate (Norton-Bailey) | $\dot{\varepsilon} = A \cdot \sigma^{n} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q_c}{RT}\right)$ | s⁻¹, Pa, J/mol, K | High-temperature service life |
| Resistivity | $\rho = \dfrac{R \cdot A}{L}$ | Ω·m | Conductor material selection |
| Thermal Stress | $\sigma_{th} = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Pa, /K, K | Thermal shock resistance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Anti-Truncation Note:** The Norton-Bailey creep equation uses $A$ (material constant), $n$ (stress exponent, typically 3–8), $Q_c$ (activation energy for creep), $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) (universal gas constant), and $T$ (absolute temperature in K). Examiners often test whether students can identify each term — write the full names during derivations.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace Industry**: Uses the **specific strength** chart to select between Al-7075, Ti-6Al-4V, and CFRP. A 1% weight reduction in a commercial aircraft saves roughly 100,000 liters of fuel per year.
- **Biomedical Implants**: Uses **biocompatibility + fatigue** to select Ti-6Al-4V (bone screws), Co-Cr-Mo (hip joints), and UHMWPE (acetabular cup).
- **Power Plants**: Uses **creep strength + oxidation resistance** to select Inconel 718 (gas turbines), T91 steel (boiler tubes).
- **Electronics**: Uses **resistivity + cost** to select between copper (high conductivity, expensive) and aluminum (lower conductivity, lighter, cheaper).

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 3.1 — Specific Strength for Material Selection

The **specific strength** is derived from the fundamental definition of strength-to-weight ratio. We begin by defining the engineering stress:

$$\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A}$$

where $F$ is the applied force in newtons (N) and $A$ is the cross-sectional area in m².

The mass of a uniform bar of volume $V$ is:

$$m = \rho \cdot V$$

For a bar of unit length $L$ and area $A$, the volume becomes $V = A \cdot L$. Therefore:

$$m = \rho \cdot A \cdot L$$

The strength carried per unit mass (specific strength) is:

$$SS = \dfrac{\sigma}{m/L} = \dfrac{F/A}{\rho \cdot A \cdot L / L \cdot 1/A} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\rho}$$

Hence, the **specific strength** is rigorously expressed as:

$$SS = \dfrac{\sigma_{UTS}}{\rho}$$

with units of $\dfrac{\text{MPa}}{\text{g/cm}^3}$ or equivalently $\dfrac{\text{N·m}}{\text{kg}}$.

**Interpretation:** A higher $SS$ value means the material can carry more load per kilogram of its own weight — making it ideal for aircraft, rockets, and high-speed rotating components.

### Derivation 3.2 — Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number (PREN)

The PREN is derived empirically from the observation that chromium, molybdenum, and nitrogen each contribute to pitting corrosion resistance in chloride environments. The empirical coefficients (1, 3.3, and 16) were fitted from extensive experimental data on austenitic and duplex stainless steels:

$$PREN = \%Cr + k_1 \cdot \%Mo + k_2 \cdot \%N$$

with $k_1 = 3.3$ and $k_2 = 16$ for austenitic stainless steels, and $k_2 = 30$ for duplex stainless steels.

**Step-by-step**:
1. Express the chromium contribution directly: $C_{Cr} = \%Cr$.
2. Express the molybdenum contribution with its empirical coefficient: $C_{Mo} = 3.3 \cdot \%Mo$.
3. Express the nitrogen contribution: $C_{N} = 16 \cdot \%N$.
4. Sum the three contributions:

$$PREN = C_{Cr} + C_{Mo} + C_{N} = \%Cr + 3.3 \cdot \%Mo + 16 \cdot \%N$$

**Selection rule (KTU standard):**
- $PREN < 18$ → Mild steel / low-alloy steel (NOT suitable for chloride service).
- $18 \leq PREN < 22$ → Standard austenitic SS (304).
- $22 \leq PREN < 28$ → Marine-grade SS (316, with $\sim 2\%$ Mo).
- $PREN \geq 32$ → Super-duplex SS / superaustenitic (e.g., 254 SMO, AL-6XN).

### Derivation 3.3 — Creep Life (Larson-Miller Parameter)

For high-temperature components, predicting the rupture life $t_r$ (in hours) at a given stress $\sigma$ and temperature $T$ (in K) uses the **Larson-Miller Parameter (LMP)**:

$$LMP = T \cdot \left[\log(t_r) + C\right]$$

where $C \approx 20$ is the Larson-Miller constant for most engineering alloys.

**Derivation**:
From the steady-state creep rate equation:

$$\dot{\varepsilon}_s = A \cdot \sigma^{n} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q_c}{RT}\right)$$

Integrating $\dot{\varepsilon}_s = \dfrac{\varepsilon_r}{t_r}$ over the rupture strain $\varepsilon_r$:

$$t_r = \dfrac{\varepsilon_r}{A \cdot \sigma^{n}} \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{Q_c}{RT}\right)$$

Taking logarithm (base 10):

$$\log(t_r) = \log\!\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon_r}{A \cdot \sigma^{n}}\right) + \dfrac{Q_c}{2.303 \cdot R \cdot T}$$

Multiplying by $T$:

$$T \cdot \log(t_r) = T \cdot \log\!\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon_r}{A \cdot \sigma^{n}}\right) + \dfrac{Q_c}{2.303 \cdot R}$$

For a fixed stress $\sigma$, the second term on the right is approximately constant. Define:

$$C = \log\!\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon_r}{A \cdot \sigma^{n}}\right)$$

and combine constants to obtain the **Larson-Miller parameter**:

$$LMP = T \cdot \left[\log(t_r) + C\right]$$

**Utility:** A single master curve of LMP vs. $\sigma$ is generated experimentally. Once known, the rupture life at any new combination of $T$ and $\sigma$ can be predicted without running a long-duration test.

### Analytical Matrix — Material Selection for Common Applications

| Application Sector | Dominant Requirement | Recommended Material | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Aircraft fuselage skin | High SS, fatigue | **Al 2024-T3** | $\rho = 2.77$ g/cm³, $\sigma_y = 345$ MPa |
| Jet engine turbine blade | Creep + oxidation | **Inconel 718 (Ni-base)** | Service T up to 700 °C, $\sigma_y = 1100$ MPa |
| Bridge girder | Cost + load | **Structural steel (A36, A572)** | $\sigma_y = 250$ MPa, weldable, cheap |
| Marine propeller | Corrosion + cavitation | **Ni-Al bronze (Cu-9Al-4Ni-4Fe)** | PREN $> 30$, cavitation-resistant |
| Nuclear pressure vessel | Radiation + pressure | **AISI 316 SS / SA 508 Cl.3** | Low Co, high toughness |
| Cutting tool bit | Hot hardness | **WC-Co cemented carbide** | Hardness $\geq 1500$ HV at 600 °C |
| Artificial hip joint | Biocompatibility + wear | **Ti-6Al-4V / Co-Cr-Mo** | Non-toxic, $\sigma_f = 900$ MPa |
| Domestic water pipe | Corrosion + cost | **Copper / CPVC / PPR** | No rust, easy joining |
| High-voltage conductor | Conductivity + density | **Al (AAC) / ACSR** | $\rho = 2.65 \times 10^{-8}$ Ω·m, light |
| Rocket nozzle | Thermal shock + ablation | **C/SiC composite / Tungsten** | T $\geq 3000$ °C |
| Cookware coating | Non-stick + heat | **PTFE (Teflon)** | T stable up to 260 °C |
| Bullet-proof vest | Energy absorption | **Kevlar (Aramid fiber) / UHMWPE** | High tenacity, low density |
| Furnace lining | Thermal insulation | **Alumina (Al₂O₃) brick / Ceramic fiber** | $k \leq 0.5$ W/m·K |
| Spark plug insulator | Dielectric + thermal | **Alumina (95% Al₂O₃)** | $\varepsilon_r = 9$, withstands 25 kV |
| PCB substrate | Insulating + rigid | **FR-4 (Epoxy + glass cloth)** | Dielectric, dimensionally stable |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 4.1 — Material Selection Logic Flow (Ashby-style funnel)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Define Service Requirements"] --> B["Translate to Target Properties"]
    B --> C["Apply Performance Indices SS, SM, PREN"]
    C --> D["Shortlist Material Families"]
    D --> E["Consider Manufacturing Constraints"]
    E --> F["Cost vs Performance Trade-off"]
    F --> G["Final Material and Process Choice"]
    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513
    style B fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#8B4513
    style C fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#4682B4
    style D fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400
    style E fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000
    style F fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082
    style G fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400
```

**Reading the diagram**: Every engineering application decision begins at the top with the *function* (what does the component do?), passes through *property translation* (strength, stiffness, corrosion?), applies **performance indices** (specific strength, PREN, etc.), shortlists material families, checks if the chosen material can be manufactured into the required shape, performs a cost trade-off, and finally arrives at the optimum material-process combination.

### Diagram 4.2 — Functional Architecture of Material Selection in Product Design

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph INPUT["SERVICE INPUTS"]
        I1["Mechanical Loads"]
        I2["Thermal Environment"]
        I3["Chemical Exposure"]
        I4["Electrical Demand"]
        I5["Cost Constraint"]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS["SELECTION ENGINE"]
        P1["Property Database Lookup"]
        P2["Performance Index Computation"]
        P3["Process Compatibility Check"]
        P4["Cost-Benefit Optimization"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["MATERIAL OUTPUT"]
        O1["Ferrous Family"]
        O2["Non-Ferrous Family"]
        O3["Polymer Family"]
        O4["Ceramic Family"]
        O5["Composite Family"]
    end

    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P1
    I3 --> P1
    I4 --> P1
    I5 --> P4
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> O1
    P4 --> O2
    P4 --> O3
    O1 --> P3
    O2 --> P3
    O3 --> P3
    O4 --> P3
    O5 --> P3
    style I1 fill:#FFE4E1
    style I2 fill:#FFE4E1
    style I3 fill:#FFE4E1
    style I4 fill:#FFE4E1
    style I5 fill:#FFE4E1
    style P1 fill:#B0E0E6
    style P2 fill:#B0E0E6
    style P3 fill:#B0E0E6
    style P4 fill:#B0E0E6
    style O1 fill:#90EE90
    style O2 fill:#90EE90
    style O3 fill:#90EE90
    style O4 fill:#90EE90
    style O5 fill:#90EE90
```

### Diagram 4.3 — Application Tree by Industry Sector

```mermaid
graph LR
    R["APPLICATIONS"] --> R1["Aerospace"]
    R --> R2["Automotive"]
    R --> R3["Marine"]
    R --> R4["Energy"]
    R --> R5["Biomedical"]
    R --> R6["Construction"]
    R --> R7["Electronics"]
    R --> R8["Defense"]

    R1 --> R1a["Al alloys for fuselage"]
    R1 --> R1b["Ti alloys for engine"]
    R1 --> R1c["CFRP for control surfaces"]

    R2 --> R2a["HSLA steel for body"]
    R2 --> R2b["Al alloys for wheels"]
    R2 --> R2c["Cast iron for block"]

    R3 --> R3a["Cu-Ni for piping"]
    R3 --> R3b["Ni-Al bronze propellers"]
    R3 --> R3c["FRP for boat hulls"]

    R4 --> R4a["Inconel for turbines"]
    R4 --> R4b["SA 508 for reactor vessel"]
    R4 --> R4c["Si for solar cells"]

    R5 --> R5a["Ti-6Al-4V implants"]
    R5 --> R5b["UHMWPE for joints"]
    R5 --> R5c["PMMA for lenses"]

    R6 --> R6a["Reinforced concrete"]
    R6 --> R6b["Structural steel"]
    R6 --> R6c["Glass facades"]

    R7 --> R7a["Cu for conductors"]
    R7 --> R7b["Si for semiconductors"]
    R7 --> R7c["FR-4 for PCB"]

    R8 --> R8a["Kevlar for vests"]
    R8 --> R8b["UHMWPE for helmets"]
    R8 --> R8c["W alloys for penetrators"]

    style R fill:#FFD700
    style R1 fill:#87CEEB
    style R2 fill:#FFA07A
    style R3 fill:#20B2AA
```
