```mermaid
    style R6 fill:#A9A9A9
    style R7 fill:#FFDAB9
    style R8 fill:#778899
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1] [3 Marks]**
*List any three applications of high-speed steel (HSS). State the property that justifies each application.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Cutting tools (drills, milling cutters, taps)** — Justification: HSS retains hardness up to 600 °C (red-hardness), enabling high cutting speeds without losing the cutting edge.
2. **Broaches and gear cutters** — Justification: High wear resistance and toughness enable machining of hard materials and intricate profiles.
3. **Woodworking saw blades and chisels** — Justification: Excellent combination of hardness (62–65 HRC) and toughness prevents brittle fracture under intermittent loads.

---

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2] [3 Marks]**
*Mention three engineering applications of Nylon 6,6 and justify each with one property.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Gears and bearings** — Justification: Self-lubricating, low coefficient of friction reduces wear.
2. **Electrical insulation (switch housings, cable ties)** — Justification: High dielectric strength and good electrical resistivity.
3. **Textile fibers (carpet, parachutes, tire cord)** — Justification: High tensile strength, abrasion resistance, and elasticity.

---

### Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Material Selection Justification:**
> Examiners at KTU strictly evaluate the **"property → application link"**. A common mistake students make is stating the material name and its application WITHOUT justifying the choice with a measurable property (e.g., "Tungsten is used in filaments because of its high melting point" is **INCOMPLETE**; the complete answer is "Tungsten is used in filaments because its melting point (3422 °C) allows it to operate at 2500 °C without melting, while its low vapor pressure at that temperature ensures long service life."). Always quote a numerical property with a unit and tie it to a service condition.

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Applications of Ferrous and Non-Ferrous Metals

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Expected Pattern]**

**(a)** Discuss, with property justifications, the applications of the following materials:
  *(i)* Stainless Steel 316 in marine hardware *(7 marks)*
  *(ii)* Titanium alloy Ti-6Al-4V in aerospace structural components *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A structural engineer must select between gray cast iron and ductile (nodular) cast iron for a machine tool bed weighing 1500 kg. Justify the better choice using a property-based comparison. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) (i) Stainless Steel 316 in Marine Hardware**
- Marine hardware (boat fittings, propeller shafts, deck fasteners) is exposed to chloride-rich seawater, which causes pitting and crevice corrosion in ordinary steels.
- SS 316 contains **16–18% Cr + 10–14% Ni + 2–3% Mo**. The Mo addition dramatically improves pitting resistance.
- Calculate PREN: $PREN = 17 + 3.3(2) + 16(0.02) = 17 + 6.6 + 0.32 \approx 23.92$.
- A PREN $\geq 23$ qualifies SS 316 as a **marine-grade** alloy, suitable for continuous seawater immersion.
- It also forms a passive Cr₂O₃ film that self-heals in oxygenated environments.
- Therefore, SS 316 is used for **marine fasteners, pump shafts, valve bodies, and seawater piping**.

**(a) (ii) Titanium Alloy Ti-6Al-4V in Aerospace Structural Components**
- Aerospace structures (wing spars, fuselage frames, landing gear) require high strength at minimum weight.
- Ti-6Al-4V has:
  - Density $\rho = 4.43$ g/cm³ (≈ 60% of steel).
  - Yield strength $\sigma_y \approx 880$ MPa (after heat treatment).
  - Specific strength $SS = 880 / 4.43 \approx 199$ kNm/kg.
  - Excellent fatigue strength (≥ 500 MPa at 10⁷ cycles).
  - Good corrosion resistance (passive TiO₂ film).
- Used in **aircraft engine compressor blades, helicopter rotor hubs, spacecraft pressure vessels, and rocket motor casings**.
- The temperature capability (up to ~315 °C) makes it ideal for **moderately hot aerospace zones** where aluminum loses strength.

**(b) Gray Cast Iron vs Ductile Cast Iron for Machine Tool Bed**

| Property | Gray Cast Iron | Ductile (Nodular) Cast Iron | Machine Bed Requirement |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tensile Strength | 150–400 MPa | 400–700 MPa | Bed must resist tensile cracks |
| Damping Capacity | **Very High** (graphite flakes dampen vibration) | Moderate (graphite nodules damp less) | High damping → cuts vibration → better surface finish on workpiece |
| Compressive Strength | 600–1000 MPa | High | Bed supports heavy workpiece |
| Cost | **Low** | 1.5–2× higher | Cost-effective preferred |
| Machinability | **Excellent** (graphite acts as chip-breaker) | Good | Easy to machine complex ribs |
| Brittleness | High (flakes act as stress concentrators) | Low (spheroidal graphite) | Bed needs toughness against impact loading |

**Conclusion:** Both are viable. However, for **precision machine tool beds (lathes, milling machines, grinding machines)** where **vibration damping is critical** for surface finish and **machinability** is critical for cost, **gray cast iron is the preferred choice**. If the bed is subjected to impact loads or requires higher tensile integrity (e.g., large press frames), **ductile iron is preferred**.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Applications of Polymers, Ceramics, and Composites

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Expected Pattern]**

**(a)** With suitable examples, explain the applications of:
  *(i)* PTFE (Teflon) in chemical and electrical industries *(7 marks)*
  *(ii)* Alumina (Al₂O₃) ceramics in cutting tools and insulators *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare the applications of **CFRP (Carbon Fiber Reinforced Polymer)** and **GFRP (Glass Fiber Reinforced Polymer)** in the aerospace and wind-energy sectors. Use specific strength and cost in your justification. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) (i) PTFE Applications — Chemical and Electrical Industries**
- **Chemical Industry:**
  - PTFE is used for **gaskets, valve seats, lining of chemical tanks, and pipe linings** because of its near-universal chemical inertness.
  - It resists attack from strong acids (H₂SO₄, HCl, HNO₃), strong alkalis (NaOH, KOH), and organic solvents.
  - Service temperature range: **–260 °C to +260 °C**, allowing cryogenic to high-temperature operation.
- **Electrical Industry:**
  - PTFE has an exceptionally **low dielectric constant ($\varepsilon_r \approx 2.1$)** and **very high dielectric strength (~60 kV/mm)**.
  - Used as **insulation in coaxial cables, hook-up wires, and printed circuit boards (PTFE-clad microwave substrates)**.
  - Its low loss tangent makes it the preferred dielectric for **high-frequency radar and aerospace RF systems**.

**(a) (ii) Alumina (Al₂O₃) Applications**
- Alumina ceramics come in purity grades from 85% to 99.9%. Higher purity → better mechanical and electrical properties.
- **Cutting Tools:**
  - Alumina-based ceramics (often reinforced with ZrO₂ or SiC whiskers) are used as **inserts for high-speed machining of cast iron and superalloys**.
  - Hardness $\geq 1800$ HV and hot hardness retention up to 1200 °C allows cutting speeds 2–3× higher than carbide.
- **Electrical Insulators:**
  - Spark plug insulators (95% Al₂O₃) withstand 25 kV at 400 °C.
  - Substrates for thick-film and thin-film hybrid circuits, feedthroughs for vacuum systems, and high-voltage bushings.
- Other applications: **biomedical hip liners, grinding media, ballistic armor tiles, furnace tubes**.

**(b) CFRP vs GFRP — Aerospace vs Wind Energy**

| Property | CFRP | GFRP |
|---|---|---|
| Density $\rho$ | 1.55–1.80 g/cm³ | 1.8–2.1 g/cm³ |
| Tensile Strength | 1500–3500 MPa | 1000–2500 MPa |
| Young's Modulus E | 130–230 GPa | 30–45 GPa |
| Specific Strength | $\geq 1500$ kNm/kg | $\leq 1200$ kNm/kg |
| Specific Stiffness | $\geq 100$ MNm/kg | $\leq 20$ MNm/kg |
| Cost (per kg) | **$30–$100** | **$3–$8** |

- **Aerospace sector:** CFRP dominates because every kg saved in an aircraft saves ~$3000/year in fuel. Examples: **Boeing 787 (50% CFRP by weight), Airbus A350 (53% CFRP)**.
- **Wind Energy sector:** Wind turbine blades (50–100 m long) are primarily made of **GFRP** because:
  - Cost dominates the blade manufacturing decision.
  - GFRP offers adequate stiffness and fatigue resistance for the service loads.
  - CFRP is used **only in critical spar caps** of very long blades where GFRP cannot provide sufficient stiffness.

**Conclusion:** **CFRP for aerospace** (where SS and SM outweigh cost), **GFRP for wind blades** (where cost and adequate performance dominate).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Applications Module 1**

- ✅ Applications are selected on **property-justification** — never state the use without the supporting property + numerical value.
- ✅ Always use **performance indices** ($SS = \sigma / \rho$, $SM = E / \rho$, $PREN$) when comparing materials — KTU examiners award extra marks for quantitative reasoning.
- ✅ **Ferrous metals** dominate when **strength + cost** matter; **non-ferrous** when **weight + corrosion + conductivity** dominate.
- ✅ **Stainless Steel 304** → General purpose (PREN ~ 18); **316** → Marine (PREN ~ 23); **Duplex 2205** → Chloride + strength (PREN ~ 35).
- ✅ **Al 2024** (high strength, fuselage), **Al 7075** (high fatigue, wing), **Al 6061** (extrudable, structural), **Al 1100** (chemical tanks).
- ✅ **Ti-6Al-4V** → Aerospace + biomedical (biocompatibility + high fatigue).
- ✅ **Gray cast iron** → Machine tool beds (damping), brake drums (wear); **Ductile iron** → Crankshafts, gears (toughness).
- ✅ **Polymers**: PTFE (chemical + electrical), Nylon 6,6 (gears + fibers), PMMA (transparent + lenses), UHMWPE (biomedical liners), Kevlar (ballistic), Epoxy + glass = FR-4 (PCB).
- ✅ **Ceramics**: Alumina (insulators + cutting tools + implants), SiC (grinding + furnace), ZrO₂ (thermal barrier + cutting), Si₃N₄ (engine components).
- ✅ **Composites**: CFRP (aerospace primary structure), GFRP (wind blades + boat hulls), MMC (Al-SiC for brake rotors).
- ✅ Always quote the **service temperature limit** for high-temperature alloys (e.g., Inconel 718 → 700 °C, Haynes 230 → 1100 °C).
- ✅ For corrosion: PREN, **pH of environment**, and **chloride concentration** are the three key inputs.
- ✅ For creep: Use **Larson-Miller Parameter** to predict long rupture life from short tests.
- ✅ Cost factors (relative): **CFRP > Ti > Inconel > Stainless Steel > Al > Mild Steel > Cast Iron > GFRP > Polymers > Concrete**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Tip:**
> Whenever a question asks *"Where is X used?"*, structure your answer in the form **"X is used in Y because of property Z (with numerical value), which satisfies the service condition W."** This single sentence pattern will earn you full marks in every material-application question across the KTU 2024 Scheme.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications of ferrous and non-ferrous alloys, steel (low, medium, high carbon steels), stainless steels, tool steels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT303 - MATERIALS AND MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Applications of ferrous and non-ferrous alloys, steel (low, medium, high carbon steels), stainless steels, tool steels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Ferrous Alloys — The Iron Backbone of Modern Engineering

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
Ferrous alloys are metallic materials whose principal constituent is **iron (Fe)**, typically combined with carbon (C) and various alloying elements such as manganese (Mn), chromium (Cr), nickel (Ni), molybdenum (Mo), silicon (Si), and vanadium (V). The family of ferrous alloys includes **carbon steels, alloy steels, stainless steels, tool steels, and cast irons**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Definition:** A ferrous alloy contains iron as the base metal (typically ≥ 50% by weight). The word *ferrous* comes from the Latin *ferrum*, meaning iron. All ferrous alloys are **magnetic** at room temperature (with the exception of certain austenitic stainless steels).

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Building Blocks" View

Imagine a **concrete mixture** used in construction:
- **Cement** (iron) is the main binder that holds everything together.
- **Sand and gravel** (carbon + alloying elements) are the fillers that change properties.
- **Water** (heat treatment) activates the chemistry and finalizes the strength.

Just as changing the sand-to-cement ratio in concrete dramatically alters its strength and workability, changing the **carbon content in steel** dramatically alters its hardness, ductility, and tensile strength. The **alloying elements act like specialty additives** (plasticizers, accelerators) that give the final material specific engineered properties.

## 1.2 Non-Ferrous Alloys — The Specialty Performers

**Formal Definition:**
Non-ferrous alloys are metallic materials whose base metal is **not iron**. Common non-ferrous alloy systems include **aluminum, copper, titanium, magnesium, nickel, zinc, and lead**, each combined with carefully chosen alloying elements to deliver properties that ferrous alloys cannot match (e.g., low density, corrosion resistance, electrical conductivity, non-magnetic behavior).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Non-ferrous alloys are essential when the application demands properties such as **lightweight construction (aerospace), corrosion resistance (marine), high electrical/thermal conductivity (electrical), or non-magnetic behavior (medical implants, electronics)**.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Specialist Surgeon" View

If ferrous alloys are **general physicians** who can treat most common ailments effectively, then non-ferrous alloys are **specialist surgeons** — each trained to handle a specific, demanding condition. You wouldn't use a cardiologist to set a bone, just as you wouldn't use a carbon steel (which rusts) to build a marine propeller or an aluminum engine block.

## 1.3 The Unified Material Selection Logic

All engineering materials, whether ferrous or non-ferrous, are selected based on four pillars:

| Pillar | Engineering Question | Example Property |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical Performance** | Will it carry the load? | Tensile strength, hardness |
| **Physical Behavior** | Will it survive the environment? | Density, conductivity, magnetism |
| **Chemical Stability** | Will it resist corrosion/oxidation? | Cr content, passivation |
| **Economic Feasibility** | Can we afford it at scale? | Cost per kg, machinability |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite Definition (frequently asked):**
> *"Ferrous alloys are iron-based alloys whose properties are primarily governed by carbon content (0.02% to 2.0%) and the presence of alloying elements like Mn, Cr, Ni, Mo."*
> Memorize this verbatim for 3-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Ferrous Alloys — The Complete Family Tree

The ferrous alloy universe is stratified by **carbon content** and **alloying additions**. Understanding this hierarchy is critical because **every 0.1% change in carbon shifts the application envelope significantly**.

### 2.1.1 Plain Carbon Steels (The Workhorses)

Plain carbon steels contain iron, carbon (≤ 2.0%), manganese (≤ 1.65%), silicon (≤ 0.6%), and trace impurities (S, P). They are classified by carbon content:

| Category | Carbon (% by wt.) | Microstructure | Key Property Profile |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Low Carbon Steel** | **< 0.30%** | Ferrite + small pearlite | Ductile, weldable, machinable, low strength |
| **Medium Carbon Steel** | **0.30% – 0.60%** | Ferrite + pearlite (balanced) | Good balance of strength and ductility, hardenable |
| **High Carbon Steel** | **0.60% – 1.0%** | Pearlite + proeutectoid cementite | Very hard, strong, low ductility, wear-resistant |
| **Ultra-High Carbon Steel** | 1.0% – 2.0% | Cementite network in pearlite matrix | Extremely hard, brittle, used for special tools |

### 2.1.2 Stainless Steels — The Corrosion Champions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Critical Definition:**
> Stainless steels are ferrous alloys containing a **minimum of 10.5% chromium** (Cr) by mass. The chromium forms a passive, self-healing **Cr₂O₃ (chromium oxide) layer** on the surface that prevents further oxidation and corrosion.

**Four Major Families of Stainless Steels:**

| Family | Microstructure | Cr (%) | Ni (%) | Key Traits | Representative Grade |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Austenitic** | FCC (γ-Fe) | 16–26 | 6–22 | Non-magnetic, excellent corrosion resistance, formable | **304, 316** |
| **Ferritic** | BCC (α-Fe) | 11–30 | < 1 | Magnetic, good corrosion resistance, cheap | **430, 409** |
| **Martensitic** | BCT (α'-Fe) | 11–18 | < 1 | Magnetic, hardenable, high strength | **410, 420, 440C** |
| **Duplex** | Mixed FCC + BCC | 21–26 | 4–8 | High strength + corrosion resistance, magnetic | **2205, 2507** |

### 2.1.3 Tool Steels — The Cutting Edge Performers

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Board Exam Favourite):** Tool steels are a special class of high-quality alloy steels designed specifically for **cutting, forming, stamping, and shaping** of other materials. They are characterized by **high hardness (≥ HRC 60), wear resistance, toughness, and the ability to retain hardness at elevated temperatures (red hardness)**.

**AISI Classification of Tool Steels:**

| AISI Code | Type | Quench Medium | Key Trait | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **W** | Water-Hardening | Water | Low cost, shallow hardening | Files, taps, drills, chisels |
| **S** | Shock-Resistant | Oil | High toughness, impact resistance | Jackhammer bits, punches, chisels |
| **O** | Oil-Hardening | Oil | Low distortion, good machinability | Dies, gauges, shear blades |
| **A** | Air-Hardening | Air | Minimal distortion, high dimensional stability | Thread gauges, forming dies |
| **D** | Cold-Work (High-C, High-Cr) | Air/Oil | High wear resistance, abrasion resistance | Drawing dies, blanking dies |
| **H** | Hot-Work | Air/Oil | Retains hardness at high temperatures (≤ 540°C) | Hot forging dies, die casting dies |
| **T** | High-Speed (Tungsten) | Oil/Air | Red hardness up to 620°C | Cutting tools, drills, milling cutters |
| **M** | High-Speed (Molybdenum) | Oil/Air | Cost-effective T-steel substitute | End mills, reamers, saw blades |
| **P** | Plastic-Mold | Air | High polishability, corrosion resistance | Injection molding dies |

## 2.2 Classification of Non-Ferrous Alloys

### 2.2.1 Aluminum Alloys — The Lightweight Champions

Aluminum alloys are designated by a **4-digit numerical code (e.g., 2024, 6061, 7075)**:

| Series | Alloying Element | Heat Treatable? | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1xxx** | Pure Al (≥ 99%) | No | Electrical conductors, chemical tanks |
| **2xxx** | Copper (Cu) | Yes | Aircraft structures (2024-T3) |
| **3xxx** | Manganese (Mn) | No | Beverage cans (3003), roofing sheets |
| **4xxx** | Silicon (Si) | Yes (some) | Welding wire, pistons (4032) |
| **5xxx** | Magnesium (Mg) | No | Marine hulls, pressure vessels (5083) |
| **6xxx** | Mg + Si | Yes | Structural extrusions (6061-T6), automotive |
| **7xxx** | Zinc (Zn) | Yes | Aircraft frames, high-stress parts (7075-T6) |
| **8xxx** | Other (Li, Fe) | Yes | Aerospace (Al-Li alloys), conductors |

### 2.2.2 Copper Alloys — The Conductivity Specialists

| Alloy Family | Composition | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Brass** (Cu + Zn) | Zn: 5–45% | Plumbing fittings, musical instruments, cartridges |
| **Bronze** (Cu + Sn) | Sn: 1–10% | Bearings, bushings, ship propellers |
| **Aluminum Bronze** | Cu + Al (5–12%) | Marine hardware, pump parts |
| **Beryllium Copper** | Cu + Be (0.5–2%) | Non-sparking tools, springs, electrical contacts |
| **Cupronickel** | Cu + Ni (10–30%) | Condenser tubes, marine applications, coinage |

### 2.2.3 Other Critical Non-Ferrous Systems

| Alloy System | Base Metal | Density (g/cm³) | Defining Trait | Primary Applications |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Titanium Alloys** | Ti (Grade 5: Ti-6Al-4V) | **4.5** | Highest strength-to-weight ratio, biocompatible | Aircraft engine parts, medical implants, chemical reactors |
| **Magnesium Alloys** | Mg (AZ91D, AM60) | **1.74** | Lightest structural metal | Steering wheels, laptop housings, helicopter gearboxes |
| **Nickel Alloys** | Ni (Inconel 718, Monel 400) | **8.9** | Retains strength at > 650°C, corrosion resistant | Jet engine turbine blades, rocket nozzles, nuclear reactors |
| **Zinc Alloys** | Zn (Zamak 3, 5) | **7.1** | Excellent die casting, low cost | Die-cast hardware, automotive trim, toys |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Property Correlations

> [!NOTE]
> These formulas are **frequently tested** in KTU exams — both in numerical problems and theory derivations. Memorize the units and the carbon content boundaries.

| Formula / Correlation | Expression | Units | Application Context |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tensile Strength from Carbon Content** | $\sigma_{UTS} \approx 350 + 1000 \times (\%C)$ for hot-rolled C-steel | MPa | Estimating UTS from composition (valid up to 0.6% C) |
| **Hardness from Carbon Content** | $HB \approx 80 + 250 \times (\%C)$ (empirical, plain C-steel) | BHN | Quick hardness estimation in production |
| **Hardness-Tensile Strength Link** | $\sigma_{UTS} \approx 3.45 \times HB$ | MPa | Universal conversion across steels |
| **Density of Steel (Rule of Mixtures)** | $\rho_{steel} \approx 7.85 + \Delta\rho_{alloy}$ | g/cm³ | Ferrous alloy design |
| **Carbon Equivalent (IIW Formula)** | $CE = \%C + \frac{\%Mn}{6} + \frac{\%Cr+\%Mo+\%V}{5} + \frac{\%Ni+\%Cu}{15}$ | % | Weldability assessment |
| **Specific Strength** | $\sigma_{UTS}/\rho$ | kN·m/kg | Material selection for aerospace |
| **Strengthening Contribution (Rule)** | $\Delta\sigma_{alloy} \approx \sum k_i \cdot (\%X_i)^{n_i}$ | MPa | Total alloy strengthening |
| **Cr Equivalent (Stainless Steels)** | $Cr_{eq} = \%Cr + 1.5 \times \%Si + \%Mo$ | % | Determines ferrite vs. austenite phase balance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Weldability Rule (Board Exam Favourite):** If **CE < 0.40%**, the steel is generally **weldable without preheating**. If **CE > 0.60%**, **preheating and post-weld heat treatment (PWHT) are mandatory** to avoid hydrogen-induced cracking in the heat-affected zone (HAZ).

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Steel Classification — Engineering Logic

The classification by carbon content is **not arbitrary**. It is driven by **microstructural evolution** on the Fe–Fe₃C (iron–cementite) equilibrium phase diagram:

- **Below 0.022% C**: Pure iron / ferrite region — too soft for structural use, only ferrite.
- **0.022% – 0.76% C (Hypoeutectoid)**: Proeutectoid ferrite + pearlite. Strength increases monotonically with carbon.
- **0.76% C (Eutectoid Point)**: 100% pearlite — the strongest "as-cooled" plain carbon microstructure.
- **0.76% – 2.14% C (Hypereutectoid)**: Proeutectoid cementite network + pearlite. Cementite at grain boundaries **embrittles** the steel beyond ~1.0% C.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Students often think higher carbon always means stronger steel. **This is false beyond ~1.0% C** because the brittle cementite network at grain boundaries causes catastrophic fracture under impact loading.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Tensile Strength from Carbon Content in Plain Carbon Steel

We will derive the empirical correlation that is frequently tested in KTU board exams.

### Step 1 — Define the Underlying Physics

Tensile strength in plain carbon steel comes primarily from two microstructural contributions:

1. **Ferrite matrix strength** ($\sigma_{ferrite}$) — the soft, ductile BCC iron matrix.
2. **Pearlite contribution** ($\sigma_{pearlite}$) — the lamellar mixture of ferrite + cementite, which scales with the **volume fraction of pearlite**.

The **volume fraction of pearlite** in a hypoeutectoid steel (carbon content $< 0.76\%$) is given by the **Lever Rule** applied to the Fe–Fe₃C diagram at room temperature:

$$ V_{pearlite} = \frac{\%C - 0.022}{0.76 - 0.022} = \frac{\%C - 0.022}{0.738} $$

### Step 2 — Apply the Rule of Mixtures

The strength follows a linear rule of mixtures between ferrite and pearlite:

$$ \sigma_{UTS} = \sigma_{ferrite} \cdot (1 - V_{pearlite}) + \sigma_{pearlite} \cdot V_{pearlite} $$

### Step 3 — Substitute Known Baseline Values

From experimental data on pure ferrite and fully pearlitic steel:

- $\sigma_{ferrite} \approx 280 \text{ MPa}$
- $\sigma_{pearlite} \approx 980 \text{ MPa}$

Substituting:

$$ \sigma_{UTS} = 280 \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\%C - 0.022}{0.738}\right) + 980 \cdot \left(\frac{\%C - 0.022}{0.738}\right) $$

### Step 4 — Expand and Simplify

$$ \sigma_{UTS} = 280 - 280 \cdot \frac{\%C - 0.022}{0.738} + 980 \cdot \frac{\%C - 0.022}{0.738} $$

$$ \sigma_{UTS} = 280 + \frac{(\%C - 0.022)(980 - 280)}{0.738} $$

$$ \sigma_{UTS} = 280 + \frac{(\%C - 0.022) \cdot 700}{0.738} $$

### Step 5 — Final Empirical Form

$$ \sigma_{UTS} \approx 280 + 950 \cdot (\%C - 0.022) $$

For practical engineering use (rounding the intercept and coefficient to memorable values):

$$ \boxed{\sigma_{UTS} \approx 300 + 1000 \cdot (\%C) \text{ MPa}} $$

This is the **rule-of-thumb equation** that KTU examiners expect students to recall for short calculations.

### Step 6 — Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A hot-rolled low carbon steel contains **0.25% C**. Estimate its ultimate tensile strength.

**Solution:**

$$ \sigma_{UTS} = 300 + 1000 \times 0.25 = 300 + 250 = 550 \text{ MPa} $$

The predicted UTS ≈ **550 MPa**, which agrees well with published data for AISI 1025 hot-rolled steel (typical UTS ≈ 510–560 MPa). ✓

## 3.2 Derivation: Weldability Carbon Equivalent (IIW Formula)

### Step 1 — Engineering Need

Weldability quantifies the susceptibility of a steel to **cold cracking** in the heat-affected zone (HAZ) during welding. Higher carbon and alloying content → **higher hardenability** → **harder martensite in HAZ** → **higher cracking risk**.

### Step 2 — Empirical Composition Index

The **International Institute of Welding (IIW)** proposed that each alloying element contributes proportionally to hardenability, with carbon having the strongest effect. The empirical formula is:

$$ CE_{IIW} = \%C + \frac{\%Mn}{6} + \frac{\%Cr + \%Mo + \%V}{5} + \frac{\%Ni + \%Cu}{15} $$

### Step 3 — Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A pressure vessel steel has composition: **C = 0.18%, Mn = 1.20%, Cr = 0.30%, Mo = 0.15%, V = 0.04%, Ni = 0.20%, Cu = 0.15%**. Calculate the IIW carbon equivalent and comment on weldability.

**Solution:**

$$ CE = 0.18 + \frac{1.20}{6} + \frac{0.30 + 0.15 + 0.04}{5} + \frac{0.20 + 0.15}{15} $$

$$ CE = 0.18 + 0.200 + \frac{0.49}{5} + \frac{0.35}{15} $$

$$ CE = 0.18 + 0.200 + 0.098 + 0.0233 $$

$$ CE = 0.501\% $$

**Interpretation:**
- $CE < 0.40\%$ → Excellent weldability (no preheat needed).
- $0.40\% < CE < 0.60\%$ → **Preheating recommended** to avoid HAZ cracking. *(Our case: 0.501%)*
- $CE > 0.60\%$ → Strict preheat and PWHT mandatory.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout:** Students often forget the **+1.5×Si correction** if the problem states **Pcm (critical metal parameter)** instead of IIW CE. Always read the problem statement carefully to determine which formula applies.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Property vs. Application Cross-Reference

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **highest-yield revision table** for KTU Module 2. Practice drawing this from memory before exams.

| Material Class | Density (g/cm³) | UTS Range (MPa) | Hardness | Corrosion Resistance | Cost Factor | Flagship Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Low Carbon Steel (1018)** | **7.87** | 400–550 | 70–80 HRB | Low | 1× (baseline) | Car body panels, structural beams, wire |
| **Medium Carbon Steel (1045)** | **7.85** | 565–700 | 80–95 HRB | Low | 1.2× | Crankshafts, gears, axles, couplings |
| **High Carbon Steel (1080)** | **7.85** | 770–1100 | 25–40 HRC | Low | 1.4× | Springs, blades, railway rails, cutting tools |
| **Austenitic SS (304)** | **8.0** | 515–620 | 70–90 HRB | **Excellent** | 4× | Kitchen sinks, chemical tanks, architectural |
| **Martensitic SS (440C)** | **7.7** | 760–1970 | 58–62 HRC | Moderate | 5× | Ball bearings, surgical instruments, knife blades |
| **Duplex SS (2205)** | **7.8** | 620–950 | 28–32 HRC | **Very High** | 6× | Offshore platforms, heat exchangers, desalination |
| **Tool Steel (H13, hot-work)** | **7.8** | 1500–1900 | 50–55 HRC | Moderate | 8× | Die-casting dies, hot forging dies, extrusion dies |
| **Tool Steel (M2, high-speed)** | **8.2** | 2000–2800 | 60–65 HRC | Moderate | 10× | Drills, taps, milling cutters, saw blades |
| **Al Alloy 6061-T6** | **2.70** | 310 | 95 HRB | High (oxide) | 3× | Aircraft fuselage, bicycle frames, structural extrusions |
| **Al Alloy 7075-T6** | **2.81** | 570 | 150 HB | High | 5× | Aircraft wing spars, military-grade parts |
| **Ti Alloy Ti-6Al-4V** | **4.43** | 950–1100 | 32 HRC | **Outstanding** | 25× | Jet engine compressor blades, orthopedic implants |
| **Inconel 718 (Ni-base)** | **8.19** | 1240–1430 | 36–44 HRC | **Outstanding** | 30× | Gas turbine disks, rocket engine components, nuclear |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Complete Ferrous Alloy Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[FERR ALLOYS BASE IRON] --> B[PLAIN CARBON STEELS]
    A --> C[ALLOY STEELS]
    A --> D[STAINLESS STEELS]
    A --> E[TOOL STEELS]
    A --> F[CAST IRONS]

    B --> B1[LOW CARBON LT 0.30PCT]
    B --> B2[MED CARBON 0.30 TO 0.60PCT]
    B --> B3[HIGH CARBON 0.60 TO 1.0PCT]
    B --> B4[ULTRA HIGH CARBON 1.0 TO 2.0PCT]

    B1 --> B1A[STRUCTURAL BEAMS]
    B1 --> B1B[AUTOMOTIVE PANELS]
    B1 --> B1C[WIRE ROPES]

    B2 --> B2A[CRANKSHAFTS]
    B2 --> B2B[GEARS AND AXLES]
    B2 --> B2C[RAILWAY WHEELS]

    B3 --> B3A[SPRINGS]
    B3 --> B3B[CUTTING BLADES]
    B3 --> B3C[RAILWAY RAILS]

    C --> C1[LOW ALLOY LT 5PCT ALLOYING]
    C --> C2[HSLA HIGH STRENGTH LOW ALLOY]
```


> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip for Exams:** When drawing phase/microstructure diagrams in the exam, always label **phases, compositions, and temperatures** on the axes. Examiners award partial marks for **correct axis labels** even if the curves are not perfectly drawn.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q: Differentiate between ferrous and non-ferrous alloys. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

**Ferrous Alloys:**
- Metallic alloys whose **principal element is iron (Fe)**.
- Generally **magnetic**, high density (~7.85 g/cm³), prone to oxidation/rusting unless alloyed.
- Properties strongly influenced by **carbon content** (0.02% – 2.0%).
- Examples: **Mild steel (low carbon steel)**, **stainless steel (304)**, **cast iron**, **tool steel (M2)**.

**Non-Ferrous Alloys:**
- Metallic alloys whose **base metal is not iron** — typically Al, Cu, Ti, Ni, Mg, Zn.
- Generally **non-magnetic** (with exceptions), low density, superior corrosion resistance, good conductivity.
- Properties tailored by **alloying additions** specific to each base metal.
- Examples: **Aluminum alloy 6061-T6**, **Titanium Ti-6Al-4V**, **Brass (Cu-Zn)**, **Inconel 718**.

*(Note: The differentiation must emphasize iron content, magnetism, and corrosion behavior. The above covers the three required attributes.)*

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q: What is stainless steel? List the four major families of stainless steels with one typical application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Stainless steel is a **ferrous alloy containing a minimum of 10.5% chromium (Cr) by mass**, which forms a thin, adherent, self-healing **chromium oxide (Cr₂O₃) passive film** on the surface that prevents further corrosion.

**Four Major Families:**

| Family | Microstructure | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Austenitic (e.g., 304, 316)** | FCC (γ-Fe), non-magnetic | Kitchen sinks, chemical tanks |
| **Ferritic (e.g., 430, 409)** | BCC (α-Fe), magnetic | Automotive exhaust systems |
| **Martensitic (e.g., 410, 440C)** | BCT (α'-Fe), magnetic, hardenable | Surgical instruments, knife blades |
| **Duplex (e.g., 2205)** | Mixed FCC + BCC, magnetic | Offshore oil platforms, heat exchangers |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper Style]**

**Q (a)** With the help of a labeled diagram, explain the classification of tool steels according to the AISI system. List the **quench medium** and **two representative applications** for each class. **(7 marks)**

**Q (b)** A plain carbon steel contains **0.45% C**. Estimate its **ultimate tensile strength** and **Brinell hardness** using the empirical correlation. State two typical applications of medium carbon steel. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer for Q (a) — 7 Marks

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Tool steels are **high-quality alloy steels** engineered specifically for **cutting, forming, and shaping operations** on other materials. They exhibit **high hardness (HRC ≥ 60), wear resistance, toughness, and dimensional stability** during heat treatment.

**AISI Classification Table (5 marks):**

| AISI Code | Type | Quench Medium | Applications |
|---|---|---|---|
| **W** | Water-Hardening | Water | Files, chisels, low-cost drills |
| **S** | Shock-Resistant | Oil | Jackhammer bits, punches, pneumatic tools |
| **O** | Oil-Hardening | Oil | Threading dies, gauges, shear blades |
| **A** | Air-Hardening | Air | Forming dies, blanking dies, precision punches |
| **D** | Cold-Work Die Steel (High-C, High-Cr) | Air | Drawing dies, long-run stamping dies |
| **H** | Hot-Work Tool Steel | Air | Die-casting dies, hot-forging dies, extrusion mandrels |
| **T** | Tungsten High-Speed Steel | Oil/Air | Cutting tools, milling cutters, lathe tools |
| **M** | Molybdenum High-Speed Steel | Oil/Air | Drills, taps, reamers, end mills |
| **P** | Plastic Mold Steel | Air | Injection molding cavities, die-casting dies |

**Concluding Statement (1 mark):**
The choice of tool steel grade depends on the **operating temperature, impact loading, dimensional tolerance, and production volume**. High-speed steels (T and M series) are preferred for **high-temperature cutting** because of their **red hardness** property.

---

### Model Answer for Q (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Identify the Steel Category (1 mark):**
With C = 0.45%, the steel falls in the **medium carbon steel** category (0.30% – 0.60% C).

**Step 2 — Calculate Tensile Strength (3 marks):**

Using the empirical rule:

$$ \sigma_{UTS} = 300 + 1000 \times (\%C) \text{ MPa} $$

$$ \sigma_{UTS} = 300 + 1000 \times 0.45 $$

$$ \sigma_{UTS} = 300 + 450 = 750 \text{ MPa} $$

**Step 3 — Calculate Brinell Hardness (2 marks):**

Using the empirical rule:

$$ HB = 80 + 250 \times (\%C) $$

$$ HB = 80 + 250 \times 0.45 $$

$$ HB = 80 + 112.5 = 192.5 \text{ BHN} $$

**Step 4 — Applications of Medium Carbon Steel (1 mark):**
- **Crankshafts, axles, and gears** in automotive and machinery applications.
- **Railway wheels, couplings, and connecting rods.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> Students frequently **forget to state the units** (MPa for UTS, BHN for hardness) and lose 0.5–1 mark. Additionally, applying the formula **outside its valid range** (> 0.60% C) is a common error — the formula **does not hold for hypereutectoid steels**.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)

**Q (a)** Explain the **classification of stainless steels** into austenitic, ferritic, martensitic, and duplex families. Compare their **microstructure, magnetic behavior, corrosion resistance, and typical applications** in a tabular format. **(7 marks)**

**Q (b)** A low carbon steel plate (AISI 1020) is selected for a structural I-beam application. Justify this selection by listing **three mechanical properties**, **one corrosion limitation**, and **one alternative material** that could be used if the application demanded **higher strength-to-weight ratio**. **(7 marks)**

*(Detailed model answers follow the same pattern as Question A; students should produce full tabular comparisons and justified selections.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — High-Density Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist and revise 30 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

### ✅ Ferrous Alloys — Must-Know Facts
- [ ] Ferrous alloys = **iron-based**, generally **magnetic**, prone to rust.
- [ ] Carbon in steel is the **single most important strengthening element** (up to ~0.76%).
- [ ] **Low C (< 0.30%)** → ductile, weldable, structural. Examples: 1018, 1020.
- [ ] **Medium C (0.30–0.60%)** → balanced strength-ductility, hardenable. Examples: 1045, 4140.
- [ ] **High C (0.60–1.0%)** → hard, wear-resistant, springs/rails. Examples: 1080, 1095.
- [ ] **Stainless steel** = ≥ 10.5% Cr; forms passive Cr₂O₃ layer.
- [ ] Four stainless families: **Austenitic (FCC, non-mag), Ferritic (BCC, mag), Martensitic (BCT, hardenable), Duplex (mixed FCC+BCC)**.
- [ ] **Tool steels** classified by AISI: **W, S, O, A, D, H, T, M, P** — memorize quench medium and key trait for each.
- [ ] **High-speed steels (T & M)** retain hardness at red heat (≥ 600°C).

### ✅ Non-Ferrous Alloys — Must-Know Facts
- [ ] **Aluminum alloys** — 4-digit designation; **6xxx (Mg+Si)** and **7xxx (Zn)** are the workhorses.
- [ ] **6061-T6** → structural extrusions; **7075-T6** → aircraft structures.
- [ ] **Copper alloys**: Brass (Cu+Zn), Bronze (Cu+Sn), Aluminum bronze (Cu+Al), Cupronickel (Cu+Ni).
- [ ] **Titanium Ti-6Al-4V** (Grade 5) → aerospace + biomedical.
- [ ] **Magnesium alloys** → lightest structural metals; AZ91D, AM60.
- [ ] **Nickel superalloys (Inconel, Monel)** → high-temperature, corrosion-resistant.
- [ ] Specific strength: **Titanium > Aluminum > Steel > Cast Iron**.

### ✅ Formulas to Memorize (Last-Minute Revision)
- [ ] $\sigma_{UTS} \approx 300 + 1000 \times (\%C)$ MPa (plain C-steel, hot-rolled).
- [ ] $HB \approx 80 + 250 \times (\%C)$.
- [ ] $\sigma_{UTS} \approx 3.45 \times HB$ MPa.
- [ ] $CE_{IIW} = \%C + \frac{\%Mn}{6} + \frac{\%Cr+\%Mo+\%V}{5} + \frac{\%Ni+\%Cu}{15}$.
- [ ] Weldability rule: **CE < 0.40% = safe**, **CE > 0.60% = preheat mandatory**.

### ✅ High-Yield Comparison Statements (For Theory Answers)
- [ ] Austenitic SS = **best corrosion resistance, non-magnetic, not hardenable by heat treatment**.
- [ ] Martensitic SS = **hardenable, magnetic, moderate corrosion resistance**.
- [ ] Duplex SS = **highest strength among SS families + excellent corrosion resistance**.
- [ ] High-speed steel (M2) = **red hardness up to 620°C**, used for cutting tools operating at high speeds.
- [ ] Hot-work tool steel (H13) = **resists thermal fatigue** in die-casting and forging operations.

### ✅ Common Exam Pitfalls to Avoid
- [ ] ❌ Don't confuse **stainless steel "304" (austenitic)** with **tool steel "A4"** (air-hardening cold-work).
- [ ] ❌ Don't claim **higher carbon = always better** — brittleness rises beyond 1.0% C.
- [ ] ❌ Don't forget **units** (MPa, HRC, BHN, g/cm³).
- [ ] ❌ Don't mix up **brass (Cu+Zn)** with **bronze (Cu+Sn)**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cast iron (gray, white, ductile), copper and its alloys, aluminium and its alloys, magnesium and alloys
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT303 - MATERIALS AND MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cast iron (gray, white, ductile), copper and its alloys, aluminium and its alloys, magnesium and alloys

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Engineering Alloys? — Foundational Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Alloys** are metallic mixtures composed of two or more elements, where at least one is a metal, engineered to achieve a specific combination of mechanical, thermal, chemical, and electrical properties unattainable by a pure metal. For KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2, the focus is on **Ferrous Cast Irons** and **Non-Ferrous Light/Heavy Alloys** of copper, aluminium, and magnesium.

In manufacturing, material selection is governed by the **strength-to-weight ratio**, **corrosion resistance**, **machinability**, and **castability** of the alloy. The four alloy families discussed here represent approximately **80%** of all metals used in cast and machined components in mechanical and production engineering.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe" Viewpoint

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of pure metal as *plain flour* and alloying elements as *ingredients (sugar, yeast, butter, salt)* added to a *baking recipe*. Just as different ingredient combinations produce bread, cake, or biscuits with distinct textures, adding carbon to iron produces brittle white cast iron, while adding magnesium to iron produces ductile cast iron. The base metal is the same — only the "recipe" changes.

## 1.3 Cast Irons — Family Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cast Iron** is a group of **Fe–C–Si alloys** containing **> 2.14% Carbon** and typically **1–3% Silicon**. Carbon content (in KTU standard notation) is expressed in **weight percent (wt %)**. The unique feature of cast iron is that most of its carbon exists as **graphite** or **cementite (Fe₃C)** rather than dissolved in ferrite.

**The Four Standard Types (KTU 2024 Module 2 Focus):**

| Family | Carbon Form | Colour of Fracture | Key Property |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Gray Cast Iron** | Graphite flakes | Gray | Damping, machinability |
| **White Cast Iron** | Cementite (Fe₃C) | White | Hardness, abrasion resistance |
| **Ductile (Nodular) Cast Iron** | Spheroidal graphite | Silver-gray | Ductility + strength |
| **Malleable Cast Iron** | Temper graphite (irregular) | Dull gray | Toughness |

**Physical Constants and Standard Metrics:**

- Density of cast iron: $\rho = \mathbf{7.1 \text{ to } 7.3 \text{ g/cm}^3}$
- Melting range: $\mathbf{1150\text{–}1300\,^\circ C}$ (significantly lower than steel at **1500 °C**)
- Eutectic point of Fe–C system: **4.3% C at 1147 °C**
- Eutectoid point: **0.77% C at 727 °C**

## 1.4 Copper and Its Alloys — Foundational View

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Copper (Cu)** is a **face-centered cubic (FCC)** metal with atomic number **29**, density **8.96 g/cm³**, and electrical conductivity second only to silver. Copper alloys are classified into **Brasses** (Cu + Zn) and **Bronzes** (Cu + Sn, Al, Si, Be, etc.).

## 1.5 Aluminium and Its Alloys — Foundational View

> [!NOTE]
> **Aluminium (Al)** is an **FCC** metal with atomic number **13**, density **2.70 g/cm³** — approximately **one-third** the density of steel. Pure Al has a tensile strength of only **~90 MPa**, but alloying can raise this to **> 600 MPa** in heat-treated grades.

## 1.6 Magnesium and Its Alloys — Foundational View

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Magnesium (Mg)** is a **hexagonal close-packed (HCP)** metal — atomic number **12**, density **1.74 g/cm³**. It is the **lightest structural metal** known, approximately **35% lighter than aluminium** and **77% lighter than steel**.

---

## 1.7 Visual Block Diagram — Material Family Tree

```
                    ENGINEERING ALLOYS
                          |
        +-----------------+-----------------+
        |                                   |
   FERROUS                              NON-FERROUS
   (Fe-based)                            (Cu, Al, Mg)
        |                                   |
   CAST IRONS                    +----------+----------+----------+
   (C > 2.14%)                   |          |          |          |
        |                   COPPER     ALUMINIUM   MAGNESIUM    OTHERS
   +----+----+              |          |          |          (Ti, Zn, Ni)
   |    |    |          +---+---+   +---+---+    +--+--+
GRAY WHITE DUCTILE    BRASS BRONZE WROUGHT CAST  Mg-Al  Mg-Zn
```
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Gray Cast Iron — Theoretical Breakdown

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is it called "gray"?** The dark color arises from **graphite flakes** dispersed throughout a ferrite/pearlite matrix. When the material fractures, it does so along these flakes, leaving a gray surface.

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

- Step 1: Molten Fe–C–Si alloy is cooled **slowly** under conditions that allow carbon to precipitate as **graphite** rather than cementite.
- Step 2: Silicon acts as a **graphitizer** (stabilizes graphite) — at **> 1% Si**, Fe₃C is destabilized.
- Step 3: Graphite flakes act as **internal stress concentrators** — they blunt crack tips, giving excellent **damping capacity** (10–20× better than steel).
- Step 4: However, flakes act as **2D notches** → low tensile strength (**150–400 MPa**) but high **compressive strength** (**600–1000 MPa**).
- Step 5: Flakes improve **machinability** by promoting **chip breaking**.

**Mechanical Properties (Typical Gray Cast Iron Grades):**

| Grade | Tensile Strength (MPa) | Hardness (BHN) | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **FG 150** | 150 | 130–180 | Light-duty housings |
| **FG 200** | 200 | 145–180 | Pump bodies |
| **FG 260** | 260 | 160–220 | Cylinder blocks, machine beds |
| **FG 350** | 350 | 200–270 | High-strength gears |

## 2.2 White Cast Iron — Theoretical Breakdown

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is it called "white"?** Rapid cooling (chill casting) suppresses graphite formation. Carbon remains combined as **iron carbide (cementite, Fe₃C)**, producing a hard, white, crystalline fracture surface.

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

- Step 1: Low silicon content (**< 0.5%**) or rapid cooling suppresses graphitization.
- Step 2: Carbon remains in combined form as Fe₃C — extremely hard (**> 500 BHN**).
- Step 3: Hardness makes white cast iron **unmachinable by conventional cutting tools** — usually ground only.
- Step 4: Excellent **abrasion resistance** → used in slurry pumps, crusher liners, grinding balls.
- Step 5: **Brittle** — low impact resistance — used where wear dominates, not shock.

**Key Reactions in Fe–C System:**

$$L \xrightarrow{1147\,^\circ C} \gamma (\text{Austenite}) + Fe_3C$$

$$Fe_3C \xrightarrow{\text{Heat (malleabilizing)}} \alpha (\text{Ferrite}) + \text{Temper Graphite}$$

## 2.3 Ductile (Nodular/Spheroidal Graphite) Cast Iron

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Innovation:** Addition of **Magnesium (Mg)** or **Cerium (Ce)** to molten iron causes graphite to form as **spheroids** rather than flakes. Spheroids do **not** act as stress concentrators like flakes — hence high ductility (**> 18% elongation**).

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

- Step 1: Molten Fe–C–Si is treated with **0.02–0.05% Mg** (inoculant).
- Step 2: Mg neutralizes sulfur and oxygen that would otherwise stabilize flake graphite.
- Step 3: Carbon precipitates as **spheroidal graphite** (nodules) during solidification.
- Step 4: Spherical shape minimizes the surface-area-to-volume ratio — low stress concentration.
- Step 5: Tensile strength rises to **350–700 MPa** with elongation up to **25%**.

**Ductile Iron Grade Equivalents (KTU Reference):**

| KTU/ISO Grade | Tensile (MPa) | Yield (MPa) | Elongation (%) | Matrix |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **FCD 400** | 400 | 250 | 18 | Ferrite |
| **FCD 500** | 500 | 320 | 10 | Ferrite + Pearlite |
| **FCD 600** | 600 | 380 | 6 | Pearlite + Ferrite |
| **FCD 800** | 800 | 500 | 3 | Pearlite (Tempered) |

## 2.4 Copper and Its Alloys — Deep Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> **Pure Copper Properties (KTU High-Yield):**
> - Electrical conductivity = **5.96 × 10⁷ S/m** (100% IACS — International Annealed Copper Standard)
> - Thermal conductivity = **401 W/m·K**
> - Density = **8.96 g/cm³**
> - Crystal structure = **FCC**
> - Melting point = **1085 °C**

### 2.4.1 Brasses (Cu–Zn Alloys)

**Composition Range:**

- **Alpha (α) Brass:** 0–35% Zn — single-phase FCC, **cold workable**.
- **Alpha-Beta (α+β) Brass:** 35–45% Zn — two-phase, **hot workable** only.
- **Beta (β) Brass:** 45–50% Zn — BCC, hard, brittle.

**Key Brass Types for KTU:**

| Alloy | Composition | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Cartridge Brass (70/30)** | 70% Cu, 30% Zn | Cartridge cases, deep drawing |
| **Muntz Metal (60/40)** | 60% Cu, 40% Zn | Condenser tubes, marine fittings |
| **Naval Brass** | 60% Cu, 39% Zn, 1% Sn | Marine hardware, propeller shafts |
| **Admiralty Brass** | 69% Cu, 30% Zn, 1% Sn | Heat exchangers |

### 2.4.2 Bronzes (Cu–Sn, Cu–Al, Cu–Si, Cu–Be)

| Alloy | Composition | Property | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tin Bronze** | 88% Cu, 12% Sn | Hard, corrosion resistant | Bearings, gears |
| **Aluminium Bronze** | 90% Cu, 10% Al | High strength, seawater resistance | Marine propellers |
| **Silicon Bronze** | 96% Cu, 3% Si, 1% Mn | High strength, weldable | Bolts, fasteners |
| **Beryllium Copper** | 98% Cu, 2% Be | Springy, hardenable | Springs, non-sparking tools |

## 2.5 Aluminium and Its Alloys — Deep Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aluminium Designation System (AA — The Aluminum Association):**
> A 4-digit code is used:
> - **First digit** = principal alloying element
> - **Second digit** = modification
> - **Last two digits** = specific alloy

**Wrought Alloy Series (KTU High-Yield):**

| Series | Alloying Element | Heat-Treatable? | Example | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1xxx** | Pure Al (≥ 99%) | No | 1050 | Electrical conductors |
| **2xxx** | Cu | **Yes** | 2024 (Al-Cu) | Aircraft structures |
| **3xxx** | Mn | No | 3003 | Cookware, cans |
| **5xxx** | Mg | No | 5052 | Marine, pressure vessels |
| **6xxx** | Mg + Si | **Yes** | 6061 | Structural extrusions |
| **7xxx** | Zn | **Yes** | 7075 | Aircraft frames, defense |
| **8xxx** | Li / Other | **Yes** | 8090 | Aerospace (low density) |

> [!NOTE]
> **2xxx, 6xxx, 7xxx** are the principal **heat-treatable** aluminium alloys — strengthened by **precipitation hardening** (T6 treatment: solution + quench + artificial aging).

## 2.6 Magnesium and Its Alloys — Deep Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is Mg's HCP structure a manufacturing concern?** The HCP lattice has only **3 slip systems** (vs 12 for FCC Al), making cold deformation nearly impossible. Mg alloys are therefore **cast, extruded, or forged hot** (typically at **200–400 °C**).

**Common Mg Alloy Systems (KTU Reference):**

| Alloy | Composition | Key Feature | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **AZ91** | Mg-9%Al-1%Zn | Most used, high strength/weight | Auto gearbox housings |
| **AM60** | Mg-6%Al-0.3%Mn | Better ductility | Steering wheels |
| **ZK60** | Mg-6%Zn-0.5%Zr | Highest strength, age-hardenable | Aerospace brackets |
| **WE43** | Mg-4%Y-3%RE | High temperature stability | Jet engine gearbox |

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all key engineering formulas** and **physical metrics** required for Module 2 problem-solving in the KTU University Examination.

| # | Parameter | Formula / Value | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Density of Gray Cast Iron | $\rho_{CI} = 7.1\text{–}7.3$ | $\text{g/cm}^3$ | Mass calculation |
| 2 | Density of Al | $\rho_{Al} = 2.70$ | $\text{g/cm}^3$ | Weight savings |
| 3 | Density of Mg | $\rho_{Mg} = 1.74$ | $\text{g/cm}^3$ | Aerospace weight |
| 4 | Density of Cu | $\rho_{Cu} = 8.96$ | $\text{g/cm}^3$ | Electrical components |
| 5 | **Weight %** → **At %** | $C_{at} = \dfrac{C_{wt}/A_i}{\sum_j C_{wt,j}/A_j} \times 100$ | at% | Phase diagram work |
| 6 | **Brinell Hardness** | $HB = \dfrac{2F}{\pi D \left(D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}\right)}$ | kgf/mm² | White CI hardness |
| 7 | **Tensile Stress** | $\sigma = F / A_0$ | MPa | All alloys |
| 8 | **% Elongation** | $\%EL = \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$ | % | Ductility |
| 9 | **Specific Strength** | $S_{sp} = \sigma_{UTS} / \rho$ | $\text{kN·m/kg}$ | Al/Mg selection |
| 10 | **Eutectic Composition Fe-C** | $4.3\% \, C$ at $1147\,^\circ C$ | wt% | Cast iron range |
| 11 | **Eutectoid Composition** | $0.77\% \, C$ at $727\,^\circ C$ | wt% | Steel boundary |
| 12 | **IACS Standard** | $\sigma_{Cu} = 5.96 \times 10^7$ | S/m | Cu conductivity |
| 13 | **Al 7075 UTS** | $572$ | MPa | Aerospace alloy |
| 14 | **Mg AZ91 UTS** | $230$ | MPa | Auto applications |
| 15 | **Damping Capacity Ratio** | $\psi_{gray}/\psi_{steel} \approx 10\text{–}20$ | ratio | Machine tool beds |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Aerospace and automotive industries use the **specific strength** formula (Row 9) to compare Al-7075 ($\approx 212$ kN·m/kg) versus Mg-AZ91 ($\approx 132$ kN·m/kg) versus steel ($\approx 63$ kN·m/kg). This single equation drives **aircraft wing selection** and **EV chassis design**.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Conversion of Wt% Carbon to At% Carbon in Cast Iron

**Problem Context (KTU Model Problem Style):**

A gray cast iron contains **3.4 wt% C, 2.0 wt% Si, and 94.6 wt% Fe**. Convert this composition to **atomic percent (at%)** to correctly position the alloy on the **Fe–C equilibrium phase diagram**.

### Step 1 — Identify Atomic Masses

We use standard KTU-handbook atomic weights:

$$A_{Fe} = 55.85 \, \text{g/mol}, \quad A_C = 12.01 \, \text{g/mol}, \quad A_{Si} = 28.09 \, \text{g/mol}$$

### Step 2 — Convert Wt% to Moles (per 100 g of alloy)

For 100 g of alloy:

$$n_{Fe} = \frac{94.6}{55.85} = 1.694 \, \text{mol}$$

$$n_C = \frac{3.4}{12.01} = 0.2831 \, \text{mol}$$

$$n_{Si} = \frac{2.0}{28.09} = 0.0712 \, \text{mol}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Total Moles

$$n_{tot} = 1.694 + 0.2831 + 0.0712 = 2.0483 \, \text{mol}$$

### Step 4 — Compute At%

$$C_{Fe}^{at} = \frac{1.694}{2.0483} \times 100 = 82.70\%$$

$$C_C^{at} = \frac{0.2831}{2.0483} \times 100 = 13.82\%$$

$$C_{Si}^{at} = \frac{0.0712}{2.0483} \times 100 = 3.48\%$$

### Step 5 — Verification

$$82.70 + 13.82 + 3.48 = 100.00\% \, \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The wt% C (3.4%) and at% C (13.82%) differ because carbon is much lighter than iron. For phase diagram work — especially locating the **eutectic point (4.3 wt% C)** — **at% must be used on the x-axis** in fundamental treatments, but **wt% is the industry standard**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Specific Strength Comparison for Aerospace Material Selection

**Problem Context (KTU Model Problem Style):**

A drone designer must choose between **Al 7075-T6** and **Mg AZ91** for a wing spar. Compute the **specific strength** of each and recommend the optimum.

### Given Data

$$\sigma_{UTS,Al7075} = 572 \, \text{MPa}, \quad \rho_{Al7075} = 2.81 \, \text{g/cm}^3$$

$$\sigma_{UTS,AZ91} = 230 \, \text{MPa}, \quad \rho_{AZ91} = 1.83 \, \text{g/cm}^3$$

### Step 1 — Define Specific Strength

$$S_{sp} = \frac{\sigma_{UTS}}{\rho}$$

### Step 2 — Compute for Al 7075-T6

$$S_{sp,Al} = \frac{572}{2.81} = 203.6 \, \text{kN·m/kg}$$

### Step 3 — Compute for Mg AZ91

$$S_{sp,Mg} = \frac{230}{1.83} = 125.7 \, \text{kN·m/kg}$$

### Step 4 — Compare and Conclude

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{S_{sp,Al}}{S_{sp,Mg}} = \frac{203.6}{125.7} = 1.62$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Logic:** Al 7075 has **1.62× the specific strength** of Mg AZ91. **Recommendation:** For a high-stress wing spar, **Al 7075 is preferred** despite being heavier per spar. Mg AZ91 is preferred for **non-structural housings** where minimum weight dominates and stresses are low.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Brinell Hardness Calculation for White Cast Iron Liner

**Problem Context (KTU Model Problem Style):**

A white cast iron crusher liner is tested with a **10 mm diameter tungsten carbide ball** under a load of **3000 kgf**. The indentation diameter is **2.8 mm**. Calculate the Brinell Hardness Number.

### Step 1 — Recall the Standard Formula

$$BHN = \frac{2F}{\pi D \left(D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}\right)}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Values

$$D = 10 \, \text{mm}, \quad d = 2.8 \, \text{mm}, \quad F = 3000 \, \text{kgf}$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate Inner Term

$$D^2 - d^2 = 10^2 - 2.8^2 = 100 - 7.84 = 92.16$$

$$\sqrt{92.16} = 9.600$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate Denominator

$$D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2} = 10 - 9.600 = 0.400 \, \text{mm}$$

$$\pi \times 0.400 = 1.2566$$

### Step 5 — Compute BHN

$$BHN = \frac{2 \times 3000}{1.2566} = \frac{6000}{1.2566} = 4775 \, \text{kgf/mm}^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Note:** Typical BHN for white cast iron is **400–700 BHN**. The computed **4775 kgf/mm²** should be reported in **SI units (MPa)** by multiplying by **9.81**, giving **~468 GPa** equivalent — confirming this is a **hard, abrasion-resistant** alloy suitable for crusher liners.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Why Each Alloy Fits Its Application

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is a **KTU High-Yield Table** for direct use in viva, theory exams, and design questions.

| Application | Material Selected | Property Exploited | Property Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Machine tool bed | **Gray CI** | Damping capacity | 10–20× steel |
| Crusher liner | **White CI** | Hardness, abrasion resistance | 500+ BHN |
| Crank-shaft | **Ductile CI** | Strength + ductility | 600 MPa, 6% EL |
| Marine propeller | **Al-Bronze (Cu-Al)** | Seawater corrosion | — |
| Aircraft wing | **Al 7075** | Specific strength | 204 kN·m/kg |
| EV chassis | **Al 6061-T6** | Weldability + strength | 310 MPa |
| Gearbox housing | **Mg AZ91** | Minimum weight | 1.83 g/cm³ |
| Heat exchanger tube | **Admiralty Brass** | Thermal conductivity | 110 W/m·K |
| Beryllium spring | **Cu-Be alloy** | Age hardening | 1200 MPa UTS |
| Bullet casing | **Cartridge Brass** | Deep drawability | 30% Zn |

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Why is gray cast iron called "gray"? Mention one industrial use.

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Gray cast iron is called "gray" because its fracture surface appears **gray in color** due to the presence of **graphite flakes** in its microstructure. The graphite is formed because of the **graphitizing effect of silicon** (typically 1–3%) during slow cooling from the melt. **Industrial use:** Engine cylinder blocks, machine tool beds, and pump housings — applications requiring **high damping capacity** and **good machinability**.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] What is the role of magnesium in ductile (nodular) cast iron?

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Magnesium is added in **small quantities (0.02–0.05%)** to molten iron as a **nodulizer/inoculant**. Its role is to **neutralize the surface-active elements** (sulfur and oxygen) that otherwise stabilize flake graphite. This allows the carbon to precipitate as **spheroidal graphite nodules** during solidification, giving the iron **high ductility (up to 25% elongation)** and **tensile strength (350–700 MPa)**. The spheroidal shape minimizes stress concentration compared to sharp flakes.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Question (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks

**Q.A (a)** [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022] Compare **gray, white, and ductile cast iron** based on (i) carbon form, (ii) tensile strength, (iii) ductility, and (iv) one typical application.

**Q.A (b)** [7 Marks] Explain the **brass-copper alloy system** with reference to (i) alpha and beta phase formation, (ii) effect of zinc content on properties, and (iii) industrial applications of cartridge brass and Muntz metal.

---

### Model Solution to Q.A (a) — 7 Marks

**Comparison Table (Full 4 marks):**

| Property | Gray CI | White CI | Ductile CI |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Carbon Form** | Graphite flakes | Cementite (Fe₃C) | Spheroidal graphite |
| **Tensile Strength (MPa)** | 150–400 | 200–400 (brittle) | 350–700 |
| **Elongation (%)** | < 1 | ≈ 0 | 6–25 |
| **Hardness (BHN)** | 130–270 | 400–700 | 140–300 |
| **Damping Capacity** | Excellent (10–20× steel) | Low | Moderate |
| **Machinability** | Excellent | Very poor (grinding only) | Good |
| **Typical Application** | Cylinder block, machine bed | Crusher liner, grinding balls | Crankshaft, gear blank |

**Concluding Statement (3 marks):**
Gray CI is selected where **damping and machinability** dominate (machine tool beds). White CI is used where **wear resistance** is critical (crusher liners). Ductile CI bridges the gap between cast iron and steel — used for **safety-critical components like crankshafts, steering knuckles, and differential housings** in automobiles.

---

### Model Solution to Q.A (b) — 7 Marks

**(i) Alpha and Beta Phase Formation (3 marks):**
Brass is a binary **Cu–Zn** alloy. Up to **~35 wt% Zn**, the alloy is a **single-phase alpha (α) solid solution** with the **FCC crystal structure** of copper. Between **~35–45 wt% Zn**, the **beta (β) phase** — a **BCC ordered structure (CsCl-type)** — coexists with α. Above **~45 wt% Zn**, the alloy is fully β-phase but becomes **brittle** at room temperature.

**(ii) Effect of Zinc Content on Properties (2 marks):**
As Zn% increases:
- Strength and hardness increase up to **~35% Zn** (solid-solution strengthening).
- Beyond 35% Zn, the brittle β-phase appears, and **cold workability decreases**.
- α-brass is **ductile and cold-workable**; α+β brass is **hot-workable only**.

**(iii) Industrial Applications (2 marks):**
- **Cartridge Brass (70% Cu, 30% Zn):** Single-phase α-brass — used in **ammunition cartridge cases, deep-drawn shells, and musical instruments** because of its excellent cold-formability and strength.
- **Muntz Metal (60% Cu, 40% Zn):** Two-phase α+β brass — used in **marine condenser tubes, ship sheathing, and architectural trim** because it is hot-workable and corrosion resistant in seawater.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative)

**Q.B (a)** [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam - May 2023] With a suitable sketch, explain the **Aluminium-Copper phase diagram** and describe **precipitation hardening** in **Al-2024 alloy**.

**Q.B (b)** [7 Marks] State the **composition, properties, and two applications** of (i) AZ91 and (ii) Beryllium Copper.

---

### Model Solution to Q.B (a) — 7 Marks

**Al-Cu Phase Diagram (4 marks):**
The Al-Cu system is a **eutectic phase diagram** with the following key features:
- Maximum solid solubility of Cu in Al (FCC α-phase): **5.65 wt% Cu at 548 °C** (eutectic temperature).
- Eutectic composition: **33 wt% Cu at 548 °C** — forms α + θ (CuAl₂) mixture.
- Solubility decreases sharply with temperature, dropping to **~0.5 wt% at room temperature** — this is the **thermodynamic basis of age hardening**.

**Precipitation Hardening (T6 Treatment) in Al-2024 (3 marks):**

1. **Solution Treatment:** Heat alloy to **~495 °C** for several hours — Cu dissolves into Al matrix, forming a **single-phase α solid solution**.
2. **Quenching:** Rapidly cool in water — Cu atoms are **trapped in supersaturated solid solution (SSSS)** at room temperature.
3. **Aging:** Hold at **~190 °C** for 8–12 hours — fine, coherent **θ′ precipitates (CuAl₂)** form, which impede dislocation motion, increasing yield strength from **~100 MPa (as-quenched) to ~345 MPa (T6 condition)**.

---

### Model Solution to Q.B (b) — 7 Marks

**(i) AZ91 — Magnesium Alloy (3.5 marks):**
- **Composition:** Mg – 9% Al – 1% Zn – 0.2% Mn.
- **Properties:** Density **1.83 g/cm³** (lightest structural die-cast alloy); UTS **230 MPa**; good castability; excellent damping; moderate corrosion resistance.
- **Applications:** (1) **Automotive gearbox and clutch housings** (e.g., VW, GM, Tata vehicles), (2) **Laptop and camera body casings** where weight is critical.

**(ii) Beryllium Copper (Cu-Be) (3.5 marks):**
- **Composition:** Cu – 2% Be – 0.5% (Co or Ni).
- **Properties:** After heat treatment (solution + age), achieves **UTS up to 1200 MPa**; excellent electrical conductivity (**22% IACS**); non-magnetic; non-sparking; high fatigue strength.
- **Applications:** (1) **Springs, bellows, and electrical contacts** in aerospace, (2) **Non-sparking tools** for oil/gas and explosive environments (ATEX-certified).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Zones in KTU 2024 Module 2:**
> 1. **Forgetting Si in cast iron:** Many students write only "Fe-C" but **silicon (1–3%) is the graphitizer** that distinguishes gray CI from white CI. Loss: **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Wrong phase in brass:** Students frequently state β-brass is FCC. **Correction:** β is **BCC ordered (CsCl-type)**, α is FCC. Loss: **1 mark**.
> 3. **HCP confusion in Mg:** Writing that Mg "can be cold-rolled easily". **Correction:** Mg has only 3 slip systems → **cold work is impossible**; must be **hot-worked at 200–400 °C**. Loss: **2 marks**.
> 4. **Al-Cu solubility direction:** Writing that solubility **increases** with decreasing temperature. **Correction:** It **decreases** — this is the **driving force** for age hardening. Loss: **2 marks**.
> 5. **Units in BHN:** Reporting BHN in kgf/mm² without converting to MPa or GPa. **Correction:** Standard SI reporting is **MPa** (multiply kgf/mm² by 9.81). Loss: **1 mark**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2, Materials Section:**

**Cast Irons (Ferrous Alloys):**
- Cast iron = **Fe + >2.14% C + 1–3% Si**.
- **Gray CI:** Graphite flakes → high damping, low tensile strength, **FG grades** (FG150 to FG350).
- **White CI:** Cementite Fe₃C → hard, brittle, abrasion-resistant, **BHN 400–700**.
- **Ductile CI:** Spheroidal graphite (Mg-treated) → strength + ductility, **FCD grades** (FCD400 to FCD800).
- **Eutectic point:** 4.3% C at 1147 °C; **Eutectoid:** 0.77% C at 727 °C.

**Copper Alloys (Non-Ferrous):**
- Pure Cu = **100% IACS, 8.96 g/cm³, FCC**.
- **Brasses** = Cu-Zn; **α < 35% Zn, α+β 35–45% Zn, β > 45% Zn**.
- **Bronzes** = Cu + Sn, Al, Si, or Be; higher strength and corrosion resistance.
- **Beryllium Cu** = age-hardenable to **1200 MPa UTS**.

**Aluminium Alloys:**
- **FCC, ρ = 2.70 g/cm³**; pure Al UTS ≈ 90 MPa.
- **2xxx, 6xxx, 7xxx** are **heat-treatable** (precipitation hardening).
- **Al 7075-T6:** 572 MPa UTS — **highest strength wrought Al alloy**.
- **6061-T6:** 310 MPa UTS — most widely used **structural Al alloy**.

**Magnesium Alloys:**
- **HCP, ρ = 1.74 g/cm³** — **lightest structural metal**.
- Cold work impossible; **hot work at 200–400 °C**.
- **AZ91:** most used Mg alloy — die-cast gearbox housings.
- **ZK60:** highest strength Mg alloy — aerospace brackets.
- **Specific strength** governs aerospace selection, not absolute strength.

**Universal Formulas to Memorize:**
- $\rho$ ranges (CI 7.1, Cu 8.96, Al 2.70, Mg 1.74) — all in **g/cm³**.
- $S_{sp} = \sigma / \rho$ — drives aerospace alloy choice.
- Wt% ↔ At% conversion formula — required for phase-diagram problems.
- Brinell Hardness formula — for white CI and bearing bronzes.

---
