topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT303 - MATERIALS AND MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Primary processes: Casting - sand casting, moulds, types of moulding sand, types of patterns, pattern materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Primary Manufacturing Process?

> [!NOTE]
> **Primary Manufacturing Processes** are those that convert raw materials (ingots, billets, powders, or molten metal) directly into a semi-finished or finished product of a desired shape. **Casting** is the most ancient and widely used primary shaping process, in which a molten metal is poured into a cavity (mould) of the desired shape, allowed to solidify, and then removed.

Among all primary processes, **Casting** offers the unique advantage of producing **intricate shapes, internal cavities, and very large components** that would be impossible or uneconomical to make by forging or machining.

## 1.2 Sand Casting — The Workhorse of Foundries

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sand Casting** is a casting process in which a **specially prepared refractory sand mixture** is compacted around a pattern (a replica of the final part) to form a mould cavity. Molten metal is poured into this cavity, allowed to solidify, and the mould is broken away to retrieve the casting. It accounts for over **60%** of all metal castings produced globally.

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Cookie Cutter" of Foundries

Imagine you want to make a shaped chocolate at home. You would:
1. Take a metal cookie-cutter (the **pattern**).
2. Press it into soft dough/sand (the **moulding sand**) to make a cavity.
3. Remove the cutter carefully.
4. Pour molten chocolate (the **molten metal**) into the cavity.
5. Wait for it to solidify in the refrigerator.
6. Break the sand away to reveal your chocolate shape.

Sand casting works exactly on this principle — except at temperatures above **1000 °C**, with engineering-grade precision, and for components weighing from a few grams to several tons (e.g., turbine housings, engine blocks, machine tool beds).

## 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Mould** | The refractory container that holds molten metal in the desired shape |
| **Pattern** | A replica (slightly oversized) of the final part used to create the mould cavity |
| **Core** | A sand shape placed inside the mould to form internal cavities or hollow sections |
| **Cope** | The **upper half** of the mould (flask) |
| **Drag** | The **lower half** of the mould (flask) |
| **Parting Line** | The interface where the cope meets the drag |
| **Pouring Basin / Sprue** | Channels through which molten metal enters the mould cavity |
| **Riser** | An extra reservoir of molten metal that feeds the casting during solidification to compensate for shrinkage |

## 1.4 Scope of This Module

The KTU 2024 syllabus for Module 3 specifically focuses on:
- The **sand casting process sequence**
- **Types of moulds** (green sand, dry sand, loam, shell, etc.)
- **Types of moulding sand** and their constituents
- **Pattern types** and **pattern materials**

These form the foundation for advanced casting topics (die casting, investment casting, etc.) covered in higher semesters.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Sand Casting Process — Step-by-Step Logic

The sand casting process follows a strict, sequential procedure. Mastering these steps is essential for both theory and lab viva questions.

### Step-by-Step Operations

1. **Pattern Preparation** — A pattern, slightly larger than the final part (to account for shrinkage), is made from wood, metal, or plastic.
2. **Flask Assembly** — A flask (a rigid frame) is placed on a moulding board. The drag (lower half) is positioned first.
3. **Sand Compaction (Drag Side)** — Moulding sand is rammed around the pattern placed on the board. The drag is inverted.
4. **Sand Compaction (Cope Side)** — The cope is placed over the drag. The pattern half is placed, the **sprue pin** and **riser pin** are positioned, and sand is rammed.
5. **Pattern Removal** — The cope is lifted off; the pattern is withdrawn carefully (with **rapping** to slightly enlarge the cavity and prevent damage).
6. **Core Placement** (if needed) — Cores are positioned in the core prints to form internal cavities.
7. **Mould Closure** — The cope is placed back on the drag and clamped.
8. **Pouring** — Molten metal is poured through the sprue at a controlled temperature.
9. **Solidification & Cooling** — The metal solidifies, fed by the riser.
10. **Shakeout** — The mould is broken, sand is removed, and the casting is extracted.
11. **Finishing** — Sprues, risers, and gates are cut off, and the surface is cleaned.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "slightly larger" patterns?** — Metals shrink on cooling (e.g., **cast iron ≈ 1%**, **aluminum ≈ 1.3–1.5%**, **steel ≈ 2%**). The pattern is oversized by the **shrinkage allowance** to ensure the final casting matches the desired dimensions.

---

## 2.2 Types of Moulds

Moulds are classified based on the type of sand, the binder used, and the production scale.

### 2.2.1 Based on Sand Condition

| Mould Type | Description | Application |
|------------|-------------|-------------|
| **Green Sand Mould** | Uses **moist (4–6% water)** clay-bonded sand; "green" refers to the moist state, not the colour | Most common; 90%+ of ferrous and non-ferrous castings |
| **Dry Sand Mould** | Green sand mould dried in an oven at **200–300 °C** before pouring | Large castings; better strength and surface finish |
| **Loam Mould** | Sand mixed with clay and water, applied over a brick framework | Very large castings (e.g., flywheels, lathe beds) |

### 2.2.2 Based on Binder System

| Mould Type | Binder | Key Feature |
|------------|--------|-------------|
| **Green Sand** | Bentonite clay + water | Moisture-activated; reusable |
| **Dry Sand** | Clay + water (oven-dried) | Higher strength, fewer defects |
| **Chemically Bonded (No-Bake)** | Resin (Furan, Phenolic) + Catalyst | Excellent dimensional accuracy, used for heavy castings |
| **Shell Mould (Croning Process)** | Phenolic resin-coated sand | Two-part mould, thin shell, high precision |
| **CO₂ Sand Mould** | Sodium silicate + CO₂ gas | Hardens rapidly; popular in core-making |
| **Investment Mould (Lost Wax)** | Wax pattern + ceramic slurry | Highest accuracy (aerospace, jewellery) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** Students often confuse "green" with the colour. **Green sand is moist, not coloured**. The moisture activates the bentonite clay, which acts as the binder.

---

## 2.3 Types of Moulding Sand

Moulding sand is the heart of any sand casting operation. It must possess specific engineering properties to produce a sound casting.

### 2.3.1 Constituents of Moulding Sand

| Constituent | Function | Typical Proportion |
|-------------|----------|--------------------|
| **Silica Sand (SiO₂)** | Refractory grains; withstands high temperatures | **85–95%** |
| **Clay (Bentonite)** | Binder; provides green/dry strength | **5–10%** |
| **Water** | Activates the clay binder | **4–6%** (for green sand) |
| **Additives** (coal dust, dextrin, silica flour) | Improves surface finish, reduces metal penetration | **2–5%** |

### 2.3.2 Types of Moulding Sand (Classification)

1. **Natural Sand** — Found in natural deposits containing clay and sand in suitable proportions. Used for small-scale, non-critical work.
2. **Synthetic Sand** — Artificially prepared by mixing silica sand, clay, and water in controlled proportions. **Used in 95%+ of modern foundries.**
3. **Green Sand** — Moist, clay-bonded sand with **4–6% moisture**. Most widely used.
4. **Dry Sand** — Same as green sand but oven-dried to remove moisture.
5. **Loam Sand** — A mixture of sand, clay, and water (≈ 50% clay); used for large castings.
6. **Facing Sand** — A fine sand layer applied directly against the pattern to give a smooth surface finish to the casting.
7. **Backing Sand (Filler Sand)** — Coarser, less expensive sand used to fill the bulk of the mould behind the facing sand. Reusable.
8. **Core Sand** — Special sand (often oil-bonded or resin-bonded) used to make cores that form internal cavities.
9. **Parting Sand** — Fine, dry sand (often silica flour) sprinkled on the parting line to prevent cope and drag from sticking together.
10. **System Sand** — Sand used in mechanized foundries with a centralized sand plant; recirculated and reconditioned.

### 2.3.3 Required Properties of Moulding Sand

| Property | Definition | Why It Matters |
|----------|------------|----------------|
| **Refractoriness** | Ability to withstand high temperatures without fusion | Prevents the sand from melting and fusing with the casting |
| **Permeability** | Ability to allow gases and steam to escape through the mould | Prevents gas porosity in castings |
| **Cohesiveness / Green Strength** | Ability of sand grains to stick together in the moist state | Mould must retain its shape during pouring |
| **Dry Strength** | Strength of mould after drying | Critical for dry sand moulds |
| **Flowability** | Ability of sand to pack tightly around the pattern | Determines surface finish and dimensional accuracy |
| **Collapsibility** | Ability of sand to collapse after solidification | Prevents hot tearing and cracking as the casting cools |
| **Adhesiveness** | Ability to retain its shape against the moulding box wall | Mould integrity during handling |

---

## 2.4 Types of Patterns

The pattern is the "tool" that creates the cavity. Its type directly affects moulding time, cost, and accuracy.

### 2.4.1 Classification Based on Construction

| Pattern Type | Description | Use Case |
|--------------|-------------|----------|
| **Single Piece (Solid) Pattern** | One-piece replica; moulded in the drag only; pattern is destroyed or has to be withdrawn carefully | Very simple shapes; small-batch production |
| **Two-Piece (Split) Pattern** | Most common; split along the parting line; moulded in both cope and drag | General-purpose castings (flanges, gears) |
| **Multi-Piece Pattern** | Three or more pieces; used when parting line is complex | Cylindrical or highly contoured parts |
| **Match Plate Pattern** | Both halves of the pattern are mounted on opposite sides of a single plate (match plate) | High-production mechanized foundries |
| **Follow Board Pattern** | Pattern mounted permanently on a wooden board (follow board) that has the same shape as the parting surface | Single-piece patterns with irregular parting surfaces |
| **Sweep Pattern** | A wooden template (sweep) is rotated or swept around a pivot to form the mould cavity | Large, symmetrical (axially rotated) castings like pulleys, gear blanks |
| **Skeleton Pattern** | A wooden framework covered with boards; used only for very large castings | Lathe beds, large machine tool frames |
| **Loose Piece Pattern** | Has small projections (loose pieces) that stay in the sand when the main pattern is withdrawn | Patterns with deep recesses, undercuts |
| **Cope and Drag Pattern** | Two separate patterns — one for the cope and one for the drag | Used when the parting line lies along the central plane |

### 2.4.2 Classification Based on Use

- **Master Pattern** — A high-precision pattern used to create match plates or core boxes (not used directly for moulding).
- **Production Pattern** — Used for actual moulding in production; usually durable.
- **Gate Pattern** — A pattern that includes the gating system (sprues, runners, gates) integrated into it.
- **Skeleton Pattern** — As above.

---

## 2.5 Pattern Materials

The choice of pattern material depends on **production volume**, **dimensional accuracy**, and **expected service life**.

| Material | Life (Number of Castings) | Cost | Application |
|----------|---------------------------|------|-------------|
| **Wood (Pine, Mahogany)** | **100–1000** | Low | Small-batch; large patterns (easy to carve) |
| **Metal — Cast Iron / Steel** | **> 10,000** (up to 100,000) | High | High-production; precision castings |
| **Metal — Aluminum / Brass** | **5,000–20,000** | Medium-High | Medium-production; light patterns |
| **Plaster (Gypsum, POP)** | 50–500 | Low | Prototype patterns; master patterns for low-melting alloys |
| **Plastics (Epoxy, Polyester, Phenolic)** | **1,000–10,000** | Medium | Medium-production; good surface finish |
| **Wax (for Investment Casting)** | Single-use (melted out) | Low | Investment / lost-wax casting |
| **Rubber (Polyurethane, Silicone)** | 100–1000 | Medium | Master patterns for limited production |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Pearl:** The pattern material selection follows the rule — **Wood for low-volume + large size; Metal for high-volume + small/medium size; Plastics for medium-volume + intricate shapes.**

---

## 2.6 Allowances on Patterns

Patterns are not made exactly to the drawing size. The following allowances are provided:

| Allowance | Purpose | Typical Value |
|-----------|---------|---------------|
| **Shrinkage Allowance** | Compensates for metal contraction during cooling | Cast iron: 1%, Steel: 2%, Aluminum: 1.3%, Brass: 1.5% |
| **Machining Allowance** | Extra material left on surfaces to be machined | 1.5–6 mm depending on size |
| **Draft (Taper) Allowance** | Taper on vertical walls to allow pattern withdrawal | Wooden: 1°–2°; Metal: 0.5°–1° |
| **Distortion / Camber Allowance** | Pre-distorts the pattern to counter casting distortion | Based on experience |
| **Rapping Allowance** | Extra size to allow for sand particles disturbed during rapping | 0.5–2 mm |
| **Shake Allowance** | Extra size to allow pattern to be shaken out | Applied as an additional draft |

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Description / Use |
|-------------------|------------|-------------------|
| **Solidification Time (Chvorinov's Rule)** | $t = B \cdot \left(\dfrac{V}{A}\right)^{n}$ | $t$ = solidification time, $V$ = volume, $A$ = surface area, $B$ = mould constant, $n \approx 2$ |
| **Cooling Rate** | $\dot{T} = \dfrac{T_{\text{pour}} - T_{\text{mould}}}{t}$ | Determines grain structure |
| **Total Shrinkage (Volumetric)** | $S_v = \dfrac{V_{\text{initial}} - V_{\text{final}}}{V_{\text{final}}} \times 100\,\%$ | Used to design riser size |
| **Shrinkage (Linear)** | $S_l = \dfrac{L_{\text{initial}} - L_{\text{final}}}{L_{\text{final}}} \times 100\,\%$ | Used to oversize the pattern |
| **Riser Size Modulus** | $M_r = \dfrac{V_r}{A_r}$ | Must be **$\geq 1.2 \times M_c$** (casting modulus) |
| **Permeability Number** | $P = \dfrac{V \cdot h}{p \cdot A \cdot t}$ | $V$ = air volume, $h$ = sand height, $p$ = pressure, $A$ = area, $t$ = time |
| **Grain Fineness Number (GFN)** | $\text{GFN} = \dfrac{\sum (n_i \times d_i)}{\sum n_i}$ | $n_i$ = % sand retained, $d_i$ = sieve size (Tyler scale) |
| **Moisture Content** | $M\% = \dfrac{W_{\text{wet}} - W_{\text{dry}}}{W_{\text{dry}}} \times 100$ | Critical control parameter in green sand |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Rule for Riser Design:** A riser must solidify **after** the casting. By Chvorinov's rule, this means the riser must have a **higher V/A ratio (modulus)** than the casting.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Chvorinov's Rule for Solidification Time

Chvorinov's rule is the **single most important equation** in casting theory. It is derived from Fourier's heat conduction equation applied to a cooling casting.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Consider a casting of volume $V$ and surface area $A$ cooling in a sand mould of initial temperature $T_0$. The molten metal is poured at $T_p$.

**Assumptions:**
1. The mould is a semi-infinite heat sink.
2. Heat transfer is dominated by **conduction** through the mould-casting interface.
3. The casting solidifies at a constant temperature $T_m$ (pure metal approximation).
4. The Biot number is small — uniform temperature inside the casting.

### 3.1.2 Energy Balance

The rate of heat release from the casting as it solidifies equals the rate of heat absorbed by the mould.

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{casting}} = \rho V \cdot L \cdot \dfrac{d f_s}{dt}
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{mould}} = h A (T_m - T_0)
$$

where:
- $\rho$ = density of metal
- $L$ = latent heat of fusion
- $f_s$ = fraction solidified
- $h$ = convective heat transfer coefficient at mould interface

### 3.1.3 Equating the Two Sides

$$
\rho V L \dfrac{df_s}{dt} = h A (T_m - T_0)
$$

The fraction solidified increases linearly with time for a constant-rate process, so:

$$
f_s = \dfrac{t}{t_{\text{total}}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\rho V L \dfrac{1}{t} = h A (T_m - T_0)
$$

Solving for $t$:

$$
t = \dfrac{\rho V L}{h A (T_m - T_0)}
$$

### 3.1.4 Deriving the V/A Relationship

Rearranging the energy balance:

$$
t = \underbrace{\left[\dfrac{\rho L}{h (T_m - T_0)}\right]}_{\text{constant } B} \cdot \dfrac{V}{A}
$$

This gives $n = 1$. However, **empirically**, Chvorinov found $n = 2$ due to non-uniform heat extraction and the parabolic nature of mould temperature rise.

$$
t = B \cdot \left(\dfrac{V}{A}\right)^{2}
$$

In **general form**:

$$
\boxed{\,t = B \cdot \left(\dfrac{V}{A}\right)^{n}\,}
$$

where $n \approx 2$ (empirically) for most castings and $B$ is the **mould constant** depending on metal and mould material.

---

### 3.1.5 Worked Numerical Example

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Style Problem]**
> A cast iron cube of side **50 mm** is cast in a green sand mould. The mould constant is **B = 600 s/mm²**. Using Chvorinov's rule with $n = 2$, find the solidification time.

**Given:**
- Side of cube, $a = 50$ mm
- $B = 600$ s/mm²
- $n = 2$

**Step 1:** Calculate volume.

$$
V = a^3 = (50)^3 = 125{,}000 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate surface area.

$$
A = 6 a^2 = 6 \times (50)^2 = 15{,}000 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate V/A ratio (also called the **modulus**).

$$
\dfrac{V}{A} = \dfrac{125{,}000}{15{,}000} = 8.333 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply Chvorinov's rule.

$$
t = B \cdot \left(\dfrac{V}{A}\right)^2 = 600 \times (8.333)^2
$$

$$
(8.333)^2 = 69.44
$$

$$
t = 600 \times 69.44 = 41{,}664 \text{ s} \approx 11.57 \text{ hours}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** A common mistake is forgetting to square the V/A ratio. Always remember: **modulus squared**, not modulus. For a more realistic answer, multiply the modulus by an effective shape factor (Chvorinov's shape factor).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Riser Sizing Using Chvorinov's Rule

### 3.2.1 Objective

Design a spherical riser for a steel casting such that the riser solidifies **after** the casting.

### 3.2.2 Condition

For the riser to feed the casting:

$$
t_{\text{riser}} \geq t_{\text{casting}}
$$

$$
B_r \left(\dfrac{V_r}{A_r}\right)^{2} \geq B_c \left(\dfrac{V_c}{A_c}\right)^{2}
$$

Assuming the same mould constant ($B_r \approx B_c$):

$$
\dfrac{V_r}{A_r} \geq \dfrac{V_c}{A_c}
$$

A **safety factor of 1.2** is applied:

$$
\boxed{\,\dfrac{V_r}{A_r} \geq 1.2 \times \dfrac{V_c}{A_c}\,}
$$

### 3.2.3 Example: Spherical Riser for a Cylindrical Casting

**Given:**
- Casting: cylinder of diameter $D_c = 100$ mm, length $L_c = 200$ mm
- Safety factor $K = 1.2$
- Assume the same mould constant

**Step 1:** Compute casting volume and area.

$$
V_c = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D_c^2 L_c = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (100)^2 (200) = 1{,}570{,}796 \text{ mm}^3
$$

$$
A_c = \pi D_c L_c + 2 \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} D_c^2 = \pi(100)(200) + \dfrac{\pi}{2}(100)^2
$$

$$
A_c = 62{,}832 + 15{,}708 = 78{,}540 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 2:** Compute casting modulus.

$$
M_c = \dfrac{V_c}{A_c} = \dfrac{1{,}570{,}796}{78{,}540} = 20 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute required riser modulus.

$$
M_r \geq 1.2 \times M_c = 1.2 \times 20 = 24 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute riser diameter (sphere: $V/A = D/6$).

For a sphere:

$$
\dfrac{V_r}{A_r} = \dfrac{\dfrac{\pi}{6} D_r^3}{\pi D_r^2} = \dfrac{D_r}{6}
$$

So:

$$
\dfrac{D_r}{6} = 24 \implies D_r = 144 \text{ mm}
$$

**Answer:** Use a **spherical riser of diameter 144 mm** to ensure proper feeding of the cylindrical casting.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Shrinkage Allowance on Pattern

### 3.3.1 Problem

A pattern is to be made for a **cast iron flange** of nominal length **300 mm**. The shrinkage allowance for cast iron is **1%**. Calculate the pattern length.

### 3.3.2 Formula

$$
L_{\text{pattern}} = L_{\text{casting}} \times (1 + S)
$$

where $S$ = shrinkage fraction.

### 3.3.3 Calculation

$$
L_{\text{pattern}} = 300 \times (1 + 0.01) = 300 \times 1.01
$$

$$
\boxed{L_{\text{pattern}} = 303 \text{ mm}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Pattern Selection Decision Chart

| Production Volume | Pattern Size | Required Accuracy | Recommended Pattern Material |
|-------------------|--------------|-------------------|------------------------------|
| **< 50 pieces** | Large | Low | **Wood** |
| **50–500 pieces** | Medium | Medium | **Wood / Plastic** |
| **500–5,000 pieces** | Medium-Small | High | **Plastic / Aluminum** |
| **> 5,000 pieces** | Small | Very High | **Cast iron / Steel** |

| Casting Geometry | Recommended Pattern Type |
|------------------|--------------------------|
| Simple flat shape | **Single piece (solid)** |
| General 3D shape with simple parting | **Two-piece (split)** |
| Complex parting line | **Multi-piece** or **Match-plate** |
| Symmetric about an axis (large) | **Sweep pattern** |
| Deep undercuts | **Loose piece pattern** |
| Very large (lathe bed) | **Skeleton pattern** |

| Sand Type Required | Application |
|--------------------|-------------|
| **Facing sand** | Layer next to pattern → smooth finish |
| **Backing sand** | Bulk fill behind facing sand |
| **Core sand** | Inside cores → must be highly permeable |
| **Parting sand** | Between cope and drag → prevents sticking |
| **System sand** | Mechanized foundries → recirculated |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Sand Casting Process Flow

The following Mermaid flowchart maps every operation in a sand casting foundry, including inputs, processes, outputs, and feedback loops.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Raw Materials Silica Sand Bentonite Clay Water Additives] --> A2[Sand Preparation Muller Mill Mix Blend]
    A2 --> A3{Is Sand Quality OK?}
    A3 -- No --> A2
    A3 -- Yes --> A4[Flask Assembly Drag Placed on Board]
    B1[Pattern Material Selection Wood Metal Plastic] --> B2[Pattern Fabrication]
    B2 --> B3[Pattern Inspection Shrinkage Draft Machining Allowances]
    B3 --> A4
    A4 --> C1[Place Pattern in Drag]
    C1 --> C2[Ramming Moulding Sand Facing Sand Layer Then Backing Sand]
    C2 --> C3[Roll Over Drag Flask]
    C3 --> C4[Place Cope on Drag]
    C4 --> C5[Insert Sprue Pin Riser Pin]
    C5 --> C6[Ramming Cope Side]
    C6 --> C7[Withdraw Pattern Carefully with Rapping]
    C7 --> C8{Need Internal Cavity?}
    C8 -- Yes --> C9[Place Core in Core Prints]
    C8 -- No --> C10[Close Mould and Clamp]
    C9 --> C10
    C10 --> D1[Melting Furnace Cupola Induction Arc]
    D1 --> D2[Pour Molten Metal at Controlled Temperature]
    D2 --> D3[Solidification Riser Feeds Casting]
    D3 --> D4[Cooling in Mould]
    D4 --> E1[Shakeout Break Mould Remove Casting]
    E1 --> E2[Fettling Cut Off Sprue Riser Gates]
    E2 --> E3[Heat Treatment if Required]
    E3 --> E4[Inspection Dimensional NDT]
    E4 --> F1{Defects Found?}
    F1 -- Yes --> F2[Rework or Reject]
    F1 -- No --> F3[Final Cast Component]
    E1 --> A2
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**
**Define sand casting. List any four constituents of moulding sand and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Sand Casting** is a casting process in which a specially prepared refractory sand mixture is compacted around a pattern to form a mould cavity. Molten metal is poured into this cavity, allowed to solidify, and the mould is broken to retrieve the casting.

**Constituents of moulding sand:**

| Constituent | Function |
|-------------|----------|
| **Silica Sand (SiO₂)** | Provides refractoriness and is the bulk grain material (85–95%) |
| **Bentonite Clay** | Acts as a binder; provides green strength and dry strength (5–10%) |
| **Water** | Activates the bentonite clay; provides cohesiveness (4–6% for green sand) |
| **Coal Dust / Additives** | Improves surface finish and reduces metal penetration |

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**
**Differentiate between green sand mould and dry sand mould.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Green Sand Mould | Dry Sand Mould |
|-----------|------------------|----------------|
| **Moisture Content** | 4–6% water retained | Oven-dried to <1% moisture |
| **Strength** | Lower green strength | Higher dry strength |
| **Surface Finish** | Moderate | Better, due to dry sand surface |
| **Permeability** | Higher (moisture aids venting) | Lower; requires more venting |
| **Application** | Most general-purpose castings; small to medium | Large castings; complex shapes |
| **Production Time** | Shorter (no drying required) | Longer (requires oven drying) |
| **Cost** | Lower | Higher due to drying |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **step-by-step procedure of sand casting**. List the various types of moulds. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the different **types of moulding sand** and their specific applications in foundry practice. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**The Sand Casting Procedure — 7 Key Steps:**

1. **Pattern and Flask Preparation:** A pattern (slightly oversized for shrinkage) is placed on a moulding board. The **drag** (lower flask) is positioned.

2. **Sand Ramming (Drag Side):** **Facing sand** is sifted around the pattern first, followed by **backing sand**. The sand is rammed uniformly to ensure compaction.

3. **Pattern Inversion and Cope Placement:** The drag is inverted and placed on the board. The **cope** (upper flask) is positioned, and the other half of the pattern (if split) is placed. Sprue pin and riser pin are inserted.

4. **Sand Ramming (Cope Side):** Facing sand and backing sand are rammed into the cope. The sand is vented with vent rods.

5. **Pattern Withdrawal and Core Setting:** The cope is lifted, and the pattern is withdrawn carefully with **rapping** (slight vibration). Cores are placed in core prints if internal cavities are required.

6. **Mould Closure and Pouring:** The cope is placed on the drag and clamped. Molten metal is poured through the pouring basin at the correct pouring temperature.

7. **Solidification, Cooling, and Shakeout:** After complete solidification and cooling, the mould is broken. The casting is cleaned, and the sprue, riser, and gates are cut off.

**Types of Moulds:**

- **Green Sand Mould** — Most common; moist clay-bonded sand.
- **Dry Sand Mould** — Green sand oven-dried before pouring.
- **Loam Mould** — Sand + clay + water over a brick framework for very large castings.
- **Shell Mould** — Resin-coated sand forming a thin shell (Croning process).
- **Chemically Bonded Mould** — Resin + catalyst (no-bake); used for heavy castings.
- **CO₂ Mould** — Sodium silicate + CO₂ gas hardening.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Types of Moulding Sand and Their Applications:**

| Type | Composition | Application |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Natural Sand** | Found naturally with clay and silica in suitable ratio | Small foundries; non-critical castings |
| **Synthetic Sand** | Artificially mixed silica + clay + water | Modern mechanized foundries (95%+ use) |
| **Green Sand** | 4–6% moisture, clay-bonded | General purpose, small to large castings |
| **Dry Sand** | Same as green sand, oven-dried | Large castings, better surface finish |
| **Loam Sand** | Sand + clay + water (≈ 50% clay) | Very large castings (lathe beds, flywheels) |
| **Facing Sand** | High-quality, fine grain, clay-bonded | Layer next to pattern for surface finish |
| **Backing Sand** | Coarse, less expensive | Bulk fill behind facing; reusable |
| **Core Sand** | Oil or resin-bonded sand | Forms internal cavities in castings |
| **Parting Sand** | Dry silica flour | Sprinkled on parting line to prevent sticking |
| **System Sand** | Mechanically reconditioned sand | Used in centralized mechanized foundries |

---

### **Question B: [Alternative Choice for Module 3 Internal Selection]**

**(a)** Classify the various **types of patterns** used in sand casting with neat diagrams. Discuss the factors affecting the **selection of pattern materials**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A cast iron cube of side **75 mm** is cast in a green sand mould. The mould constant is **B = 580 s/mm²**. Using Chvorinov's rule (with $n = 2$), calculate the solidification time. If a spherical riser is to be designed such that it solidifies after the casting with a safety factor of **1.25**, calculate the required **riser diameter**. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Types of Patterns (with sketches described):**

| Pattern Type | Description | Sketch Description |
|--------------|-------------|--------------------|
| **Single Piece (Solid)** | One-piece; moulded in drag only; pattern destroyed or rapped out | Solid rectangular block |
| **Two-Piece (Split)** | Most common; split at parting line; one half in cope, one in drag | Block split horizontally into upper and lower halves |
| **Multi-Piece** | Three or more pieces; for complex parting lines | Cylindrical shape in 3 or 4 segments |
| **Match Plate** | Both halves mounted on a single match plate | Two halves bolted to a central plate |
| **Follow Board** | Pattern attached to a shaped wooden follow board | Pattern glued to a contoured board matching parting surface |
| **Sweep Pattern** | A template rotated or swept around a pivot | Sweep arm rotating about a central pivot |
| **Skeleton Pattern** | Wooden framework with boards; for very large castings | Skeletal frame with surface boards |
| **Loose Piece Pattern** | Has small projections that remain in sand | Block with small separate inserts |

**Factors Affecting Pattern Material Selection:**

1. **Production Volume** — Wood for low-volume (< 100), Metal for high-volume (> 5,000).
2. **Size of Pattern** — Large patterns use wood (light, easy to shape); small patterns use metal.
3. **Dimensional Accuracy Required** — Metal patterns give higher accuracy than wood.
4. **Surface Finish Required** — Metal patterns give smoother surfaces.
5. **Complexity of Shape** — Wood and plastic are easier to carve for complex shapes.
6. **Frequency of Use (Durability)** — High-frequency use demands metal or durable plastic.
7. **Cost of Pattern** — Wood is cheapest; metal is most expensive.
8. **Type of Moulding Process** — Match plate and shell moulds require metal patterns.
9. **Moisture Resistance** — Patterns for green sand should resist moisture (metals better).
10. **Ease of Repair and Modification** — Wood is easier to repair than metal.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- Side of cube: $a = 75$ mm
- Mould constant: $B = 580$ s/mm²
- $n = 2$
- Safety factor: $K = 1.25$
- Shape factor: Cube → Chvorinov's effective shape factor = 1.0 (cubic approximation)

**Step 1 — Compute Casting Volume:**

$$
V_c = a^3 = (75)^3 = 421{,}875 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Surface Area:**

$$
A_c = 6a^2 = 6 \times (75)^2 = 33{,}750 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Modulus (V/A):**

$$
M_c = \dfrac{V_c}{A_c} = \dfrac{421{,}875}{33{,}750} = 12.5 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Chvorinov's Rule:**

$$
t_c = B \cdot (M_c)^2 = 580 \times (12.5)^2 = 580 \times 156.25
$$

$$
t_c = 90{,}625 \text{ s} \approx 25.17 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 5 — Required Riser Modulus (with safety factor 1.25):**

$$
M_r = K \times M_c = 1.25 \times 12.5 = 15.625 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute Riser Diameter (Spherical Riser: $M_r = D_r / 6$):**

$$
\dfrac{D_r}{6} = 15.625 \implies D_r = 15.625 \times 6 = 93.75 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{D_r \approx 94 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Answer:** Solidification time ≈ **25.17 hours**, and spherical riser diameter ≈ **94 mm**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Chvorinov's Rule Squared Modulus:** Many students write $t = B \cdot (V/A)$ instead of $t = B \cdot (V/A)^2$. The squared form is **empirically proven and compulsory** unless specifically stated otherwise.
> 2. **Modulus Formula Confusion:** Modulus is $V/A$, **not** $A/V$. Writing the inverse will lead to incorrect riser sizing.
> 3. **Green Sand ≠ Green Colour:** Don't write "green coloured sand" in definitions. It refers to the **moist state** of the sand.
> 4. **Pattern Material Selection:** Don't say "metal is best for everything." Wood is preferred for large patterns due to **ease of shaping and low cost**.
> 5. **Riser Design Condition:** The riser must solidify **AFTER** the casting. Translating this correctly requires $M_r \geq K \cdot M_c$, not $M_c \geq M_r$.
> 6. **Missing Allowances:** A pattern without listing **shrinkage, machining, draft, and rapping allowances** is incomplete — these are mandatory discussion points.
> 7. **Unit Consistency:** Always express $V$ in mm³, $A$ in mm², modulus in mm, and $t$ in seconds. KTU valuators deduct marks for mixed SI units.
> 8. **Confusing Pattern Type with Material:** A *wooden two-piece pattern* is a **type + material** combination. Don't confuse the two in classifications.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] **Casting** is the oldest primary shaping process; **sand casting** is the most common variant.
- [x] **Green sand mould** uses 4–6% moisture in clay-bonded sand; "green" means **moist, not coloured**.
- [x] **Moulding sand constituents:** Silica (85–95%), Bentonite (5–10%), Water (4–6%), Additives (2–5%).
- [x] **Essential properties** of moulding sand: Refractoriness, Permeability, Green Strength, Dry Strength, Flowability, Collapsibility, Adhesiveness.
- [x] **Pattern types** to remember: Single piece, Two-piece (split), Multi-piece, Match plate, Follow board, Sweep, Skeleton, Loose piece.
- [x] **Pattern materials** ranked by life: Wood (< 1000) → Plastics (1000–10,000) → Aluminum/Brass (5000–20,000) → Cast Iron/Steel (> 10,000).
- [x] **Pattern allowances** include Shrinkage, Machining, Draft, Distortion, Rapping, Shake.
- [x] **Chvorinov's Rule:** $t = B \cdot (V/A)^n$ with $n = 2$.
- [x] **Modulus of a casting** = $V/A$; for a sphere, $M = D/6$; for a cube of side $a$, $M = a/6$.
- [x] **Riser design rule:** $M_r \geq 1.2 \cdot M_c$ (safety factor 1.2 standard; KTU sometimes uses 1.25).
- [x] **Facing sand** is used next to the pattern; **Backing sand** fills the rest; **Core sand** forms internal cavities.
- [x] **Dry sand mould** is green sand oven-dried at 200–300 °C for large castings.
- [x] **Shell mould (Croning process)** uses resin-coated sand for thin-shell, high-accuracy moulds.
- [x] **Draft allowance:** Wooden patterns need 1°–2°; metal patterns need 0.5°–1° for easy withdrawal.
- [x] **Shrinkage values:** Cast iron ≈ 1%, Steel ≈ 2%, Aluminum ≈ 1.3–1.5%, Brass ≈ 1.5%.
- [x] **Sweep pattern** is best for large axially symmetric castings like pulleys and gear blanks.
- [x] **Skeleton pattern** is used for very large castings such as lathe beds and machine tool frames.

### Three "Must-Remember" Equations for the KTU Exam

$$
\boxed{t = B \cdot \left(\dfrac{V}{A}\right)^2} \quad \text{— Chvorinov's Rule (Solidification Time)}
$$

$$
\boxed{M_{\text{riser}} \geq K \cdot M_{\text{casting}}} \quad \text{— Riser Design Condition}
$$

$$
\boxed{L_{\text{pattern}} = L_{\text{casting}} \cdot (1 + S)} \quad \text{— Shrinkage Allowance}
$$
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Forging methods: Precision forging, coining, heading, piercing, die design, die materials and lubrication, forging machines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT303 - MATERIALS AND MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Forging methods: Precision forging, coining, heading, piercing, die design, die materials and lubrication, forging machines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Forging — The Foundation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forging** is a primary metal forming process in which controlled compressive forces (impact or steady pressure) are applied to a heated (or cold) metal workpiece (billet, bar, or ingot) to plastically deform it into a desired geometry, while preserving the continuous fibrous grain flow of the metal.

**Fibre flow alignment** with the part geometry is the single most important metallurgical advantage of forging — it gives superior fatigue strength, fracture toughness, and impact resistance compared to casting or machining.

> [!NOTE]
> **Workpiece:** The starting metal piece in forging is called a **billet** (small cross-section) or an **ingot** (large cross-section). The preheated metal is also termed the **slug** or **blank** in closed-die operations.

---

## 1.2 Family of Forging Methods — A Bird's-Eye View

| Method | One-line Definition | Typical Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **Precision Forging** | Near-net-shape forging producing parts with minimal post-machining allowance (≤ 0.25 mm). | Like using a perfectly cut cookie-cutter on rolled dough — minimal trimming. |
| **Coining** | A cold forging / sizing process that produces extremely fine surface detail and dimensional accuracy by squeezing in a closed die. | Like stamping a coin on metal — high pressure, perfect imprint. |
| **Heading** | Forging the **end** of a bar/billet to enlarge its cross-section (e.g., bolt heads, rivets, screws). | Like squishing the end of a pencil eraser to make a flat top. |
| **Piercing** | Forcing a punch through a heated billet to create a hole (without removing material). | Like pushing a straw through a soft ball of clay to make a hole. |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Forging as a Sculptural Process

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition:** Imagine you have a ball of warm clay (the heated billet). If you press it between two wooden boards (dies), the clay flows outward and takes the shape of the boards. That is **forging**. The boards can be flat (open-die), or shaped like a mould (closed-die), or even have a peg sticking out (piercing). The fibre-like strands in the clay represent the **grain flow** in the metal — and these strands *never break*, they only *reorient* with the shape. This is why forged wrenches are stronger than cast wrenches.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Forging Operations

> [!NOTE]
> KTU requires a strict classification. The forging process tree under the **2024 Scheme** syllabus is:

- **By Temperature**
   - **Hot Forging:** Above recrystallization temperature (typically 0.6–0.8 of the melting point, in **°C** or **K**). Easier deformation, lower force.
   - **Warm Forging:** Between cold and hot ranges (room temp to recrystallization).
   - **Cold Forging:** At or near room temperature. Higher accuracy, better surface finish, higher force.
- **By Type of Die**
   - **Open-Die Forging:** Workpiece deforms between flat/contoured dies with material free to flow sideways.
   - **Closed-Die (Impression-Die) Forging:** Material is fully constrained within the die cavity (flash may form).
- **By Equipment**
   - Hammer forging (impact).
   - Press forging (steady pressure) — hydraulic or mechanical.

---

## 1.5 Precision Forging — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Precision Forging** (also called **Near-Net-Shape Forging**) is a forging process that uses precisely machined dies, controlled temperature, and often multiple stages (preform → finish) to produce components with very tight dimensional tolerances and a near-finished surface, thus reducing or eliminating subsequent machining.

**Key metrics of precision forging:**
- Tolerance: **± 0.1 mm to ± 0.25 mm** (compared to ± 0.5–1.0 mm in conventional forging).
- Surface roughness: **Ra 1–3 µm** (versus Ra 6–12 µm in conventional forging).
- Draft angle: often **0°–1°** (versus 3°–7° in conventional forging).
- Machining allowance: **≤ 0.25 mm** per face.

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — Precision Forging vs. Conventional Forging:** Conventional forging is like baking a rough cake and then icing it smoothly. Precision forging is like baking the cake *already* in the shape of the final product — the icing step is almost gone.

**Applications:** Bevel gears, connecting rods, turbine blades, automotive constant-velocity (CV) joints, surgical implants, aerospace structural brackets.

---

## 1.6 Coining — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coining** is a *cold* closed-die forging process in which a workpiece is placed in a die cavity and squeezed under extremely high pressure so that every fine detail of the die surface is reproduced on the metal surface. The deformation is primarily a *sizing* operation — the cavity is fully filled and the metal is plastically compressed to a precisely defined thickness.

**Distinguishing features of coining:**
- Performed **cold** (room temperature, occasionally warm).
- Pressure: **1500 MPa to 2500 MPa** (very high).
- Material flow: negligible; deformation is mainly *thinning* of the slug.
- Used for: minting coins, producing medals, decorative items, jewellery, precision flat surfaces, embossing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Coining"?** The process takes its name from the **minting of coins** — the very first industrial application of the closed-die squeezing process, dating back over **2000 years** to ancient Lydia (modern-day Turkey, ~600 BC).

---

## 1.7 Heading — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heading** is a forging process in which the **end** of a bar, rod, or wire is upset (enlarged in cross-section) to form a head or a localised bulge. The axis of the workpiece is usually aligned with the direction of the applied force.

**Variants of heading:**
- **Upset heading:** End is simply enlarged (e.g., bolt heads).
- **Die heading (closed-die):** The upset metal is shaped inside a die cavity (e.g., hex-head bolts).
- **Cold heading vs. Hot heading:** Cold heading is dominant in mass production of fasteners.
- **Extrusion heading:** Material is also pushed along the axis during heading (e.g., socket-head cap screws).

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — Heading:** Hold a stick of warm toffee at one end and push the free end against a hard wall. The end that hits the wall bulges — that bulge is the "head." A bolt is essentially a headed cylinder, and most production bolts are made by **cold heading** at rates of **100–300 pieces per minute** on multi-station cold headers.

**Production rates & applications:** Bolts, screws, rivets, nails, valve stems, stud ends, axle stubs, spark-plug terminals.

---

## 1.8 Piercing — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Piercing** is a forging operation in which a punch is forced through a heated, fully enclosed billet (inside a die cavity) to create a **through-hole or blind cavity** *without removing any material* (the slug is *displaced*, not cut). The material flows around the punch to fill the rest of the die.

**Key difference from machining a hole:**
- In machining, material is removed as chips.
- In piercing, the slug is *split* into two halves of reduced thickness, or a central slug is punched out as a *rejected* piece.

> [!NOTE]
> **Commonly confused terms:** Piercing is often called **"hot extrusion piercing"** in tube manufacturing (e.g., the Mannesmann process for seamless tubes). In closed-die forging, piercing is used to make holes in flanges, hubs, gears, and rings.

**Piercing force formula (introductory):**

$$
F_{pierce} = \sigma_f \cdot A_{punch}
$$

where $\sigma_f$ is the **flow stress** of the billet at forging temperature and $A_{punch}$ is the cross-sectional area of the punch.

---

## 1.9 Forging Machines — Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Forging machines deliver the **force/energy** needed to deform the metal. The two broad categories are:

| Category | Energy Source | Force Characteristic | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hammer Forging Machines** | Gravity + steam/air/hydraulic | **Impact** (high peak force, short duration) | Open-die, closed-die hot forging |
| **Press Forging Machines** | Hydraulic or mechanical | **Sustained** (constant force, longer stroke) | Closed-die, precision, cold/warm forging |

**Sub-classification of hammers:**
- **Drop hammers** (board, belt-lift)
- **Steam/air hammers** (single-acting, double-acting)
- **Counterblow hammers** (energy-efficient)
- **Hydraulic hammers**

**Sub-classification of presses:**
- **Mechanical presses** (crank, eccentric, knuckle-joint — high speed, fixed stroke)
- **Hydraulic presses** (variable speed, variable force — preferred for precision and large parts)

---

## 1.10 The Holistic Picture

> [!TIP]
> **Bringing it all together:** A typical *forged connecting rod* begins as a heated bar, is **upset/headed** at one end in a heading machine, then placed in a **closed-die forging press** where it is **coined** to final thickness and **pierced** for the big-end and small-end bolt holes. The final part is a **precision forging** that needs only light grinding at the parting line. The dies are made of **H11 / H13 hot-work tool steel**, lubricated with a **graphite-water suspension**, and the press is a **screw-type mechanical press** rated at **10,000 kN** (≈ 1000 tonnes).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Precision Forging — Operational Logic

> [!NOTE]
> Precision forging is *not* a single machine setting — it is a **process philosophy** that integrates die accuracy, temperature control, lubrication, and multi-stage preforming.

### Step-by-step logic:

1. **Billet preparation:** Cut to precise weight (±1%) to control flash and final dimensions.
2. **Controlled heating:** Induction heating is preferred (rapid, scale-free) — target ±10 °C accuracy.
3. **Multi-stage deformation:** Use **preform dies** (rough shape) → **finish dies** (final shape). The number of stages can be **2 to 5**.
4. **Lubrication:** Apply graphite-based or MoS₂-based lubricant; this prevents die wear and aids material flow.
5. **Trimming:** Cold or hot trimming of flash (rotary trim press or band saw).
6. **Inspection:** Coordinate Measuring Machine (CMM) for ±0.05 mm verification.

### Why precision forging works:

> [!TIP]
> The "**Why**" — Three pillars enable precision:
> 1. **Tight temperature window** → predictable flow stress, less scale, less dimensional drift.
> 2. **Preform → finish stages** → reduces local strain in a single hit, preventing die fracture and overloading the press.
> 3. **Smooth die surfaces (Ra < 0.2 µm)** + **graphite lubricant** → material flows uniformly; surface finish transfers from die to part.

---

## 2.2 Coining — Operational Logic

### Step-by-step logic:

1. **Slug preparation:** Cold slug is precisely blanked (volume-controlled) and annealed if necessary.
2. **Cleaning:** Acid pickling or shot blasting to remove scale/oxides.
3. **Lubrication:** Light mineral oil or stearate-based film.
4. **Placing in die cavity:** The slug is positioned between upper and lower coining dies.
5. **Application of pressure:** The dies close, squeezing the slug to die-cavity thickness.
6. **Ejection:** Part is ejected by knockout pins; trimmed if flash present.

### Pressure requirement (coining):

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The required coining pressure is essentially equal to the **flow stress of the material** in its strain-hardened state, multiplied by the projected area:

$$
P_{coin} = K \cdot \varepsilon^n \cdot A_{proj}
$$

where:
- $K$ = strength coefficient (MPa)
- $\varepsilon$ = true strain during coining
- $n$ = strain-hardening exponent
- $A_{proj}$ = projected area of the part (mm²)

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering reality check:** For a typical coining operation on mild steel slug ($\sigma_f \approx 700$ MPa at 30% cold reduction) over an area of 100 mm², the coining force is **~70 kN** (≈ 7 tonnes) — which is why coining presses are rated in the **1000–10,000 kN** range.

### Coining vs. Stamping — a key KTU distinction:

| Feature | Coining | Stamping (Blanking) |
|---|---|---|
| Material flow | Continuous plastic flow | Shear separation |
| Force level | Very high (≥ σf) | Moderate (shear strength) |
| Burr | None | Yes (≈ 5–10% thickness) |
| Tolerance | ±0.01 mm | ±0.05 mm |
| Application | Coins, medals | Sheet metal parts |

---

## 2.3 Heading — Operational Logic

### Step-by-step logic:

1. **Wire/rod feed:** A precise length of wire is fed into the heading machine (cold-header).
2. **Cut-off:** Shear cuts the wire to slug length.
3. **Upsetting:** A heading punch strikes the end of the slug, upsetting it into the die cavity.
4. **Extrusion (if needed):** A second punch pushes material along the axis to form the shank.
5. **Trimming & thread rolling:** Flash is removed (if any), threads are rolled (not cut) onto the shank.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of thumb for heading:** The length-to-diameter ratio of the unsupported slug must be limited to prevent **buckling**:

$$
\frac{L_0}{D_0} \leq 2.5 \text{ to } 3.0
$$

If exceeded, the slug folds (forms a "cold shut" or lap defect). Workarounds include **conical-point punches**, **multiple-blow heading**, or **die-heading** with side support.

### Heading force:

$$
F_{head} = K_f \cdot \sigma_{f} \cdot A_{proj}
$$

where $K_f$ is the **forging shape factor** (typically 1.5–3.0 for heading, due to friction and redundant work).

---

## 2.4 Piercing — Operational Logic

### Step-by-step logic:

1. **Billet preform:** A slug is upset into a short cylinder (L/D ≈ 0.5–1.0) to ensure the punch does not wander.
2. **Piercing punch entry:** The heated punch enters the slug at low velocity to avoid deflection.
3. **Material displacement:** Metal flows radially outward to fill the die cavity; the central slug is extruded into a clearance hole or a "pip" cavity.
4. **Punch withdrawal:** The punch is withdrawn, leaving a clean hole.

> [!NOTE]
> **Piercing vs. Punching:**
> - **Piercing (forging):** No material is removed; the slug is plastically displaced. Used in closed-die hot forging.
> - **Punching (sheet metal):** Material is sheared and removed as a scrap slug. Used in blanking/piercing of sheet metal.

### Piercing force:

$$
F_{pierce} = \sigma_{f,hot} \cdot A_{punch} \cdot (1 + \mu \cdot \frac{D}{h})
$$

where:
- $\sigma_{f,hot}$ = flow stress at forging temperature (MPa)
- $A_{punch}$ = punch cross-section (mm²)
- $\mu$ = friction coefficient at the punch-billet interface
- $D$ = punch diameter
- $h$ = billet height/thickness

The $(1 + \mu D/h)$ term accounts for the **friction hill** effect.

---

## 2.5 Die Design — Comprehensive Theory

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A forging die is a precision tool, typically made of **hot-work tool steel** (H11, H13, H21) or **carbide inserts** for high-volume production. The design integrates the cavity geometry, parting line, draft angles, flash gutter, and ejector system.

### 2.5.1 Parting Line (PL)

> [!NOTE]
> The **parting line** is the line where the upper and lower die halves meet. It should be chosen to:
> - Maximize die life (avoid high PL in regions of high metal flow).
> - Minimize flash.
> - Allow easy forging and trimming.
> - Enable proper metal flow (avoid trapping).

### 2.5.2 Draft Angle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **draft angle** ($\alpha$) is a slight taper on die walls that allows the forged part to be ejected without scoring the die. Typical values:

| Process | Draft Angle (α) |
|---|---|
| Conventional hot forging | 3° – 7° |
| Precision hot forging | 1° – 3° |
| Cold forging / coining | 0° – 1° |

### 2.5.3 Flash Gutter (Flash Land)

> [!NOTE]
> A small gap left intentionally at the parting line. The **flash gutter** has:
> - **Land (bridge)** — thin restriction (h ≈ 0.5–3 mm) that builds up back-pressure.
> - **Well** — outer region where excess flash spreads.
> This **flash gutter is the "pressure regulator"** of closed-die forging.

### 2.5.4 Flash Ratio Design Rule:

$$
\text{Flash ratio} = \frac{W}{T} \approx 4 \text{ to } 6
$$

where $W$ = width of flash gutter, $T$ = thickness of flash land. This ratio balances back-pressure with forging load.

### 2.5.5 Die Block Design

- **Web thickness** ≥ 1.5 × cavity depth.
- **Shrinkage allowance** (steel cools & shrinks ~1.5%).
- **Bolt holes, ejector pin holes, vent holes** at strategic positions.

---

## 2.6 Die Materials — Deep Dive

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Die material selection is governed by **operating temperature**, **impact loading**, **wear**, and **production volume**.

| Die Material | Hardness (HRC) | Max Working Temp (°C) | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **H11** (5% Cr hot-work steel) | 48–54 | 595 | Hot forging of Al, Mg |
| **H13** (5% Cr + Mo + V) | 48–54 | 595 | **Most common hot-forging die steel** |
| **H21** (tungsten hot-work) | 45–55 | 650 | Severe hot forging of steel |
| **H26 / H41** | 50–58 | 700+ | High-temperature die casting |
| **D2 / D3** (cold-work) | 58–62 | < 200 | Cold heading, coining |
| **A2 / A6** (air-hardening) | 58–62 | < 200 | Cold forging, blanking |
| **M2 / M4** (high-speed steel) | 60–65 | < 600 | High-wear die inserts |
| **Tungsten Carbide (WC-Co)** | 70–80 HRA | 800 | Precision die inserts |
| **Inconel 718 (nickel superalloy)** | 40–45 HRC | 980 | Forging titanium/aerospace alloys |

> [!TIP]
> **Recall trick (KTU-favourite):** "**H13 is to forging what SS304 is to kitchen sinks**" — it is the *default* die material for 90% of hot-forging applications.

### Die Manufacturing Steps:

1. **Rough machining** of die block (CNC milling).
2. **Heat treatment:** Harden + temper to specified HRC.
3. **Die sinking (EDM):** Electric Discharge Machining for complex cavities.
4. **Hand polishing & engraving.**
5. **Try-out** in a forging press; corrections made.
6. **Production release** after sample approval.

---

## 2.7 Forging Lubrication — Deep Dive

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The forging lubricant must **(a)** prevent die-workpiece sticking, **(b)** reduce wear, **(c)** act as a thermal barrier, and **(d)** control heat transfer.

### Lubricant families:

| Lubricant Type | Carrier | Operating Temp (°C) | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Graphite-water suspension** | Water (evaporates) | 200–600 | Hot forging (most common) |
| **Graphite-oil suspension** | Mineral oil | 100–400 | Warm forging |
| **MoS₂ (molybdenum disulfide)** | Oil/grease | 200–400 | Cold forging, heading |
| **Boric acid + water** | Water | 500–800 | High-T forging of steel |
| **Phosphate coating + soap** | Dry film | < 200 | Cold heading (mandatory) |
| **Glass-based lubricants** | Dry film | 700–1200 | Titanium, superalloy forging |
| **Wax-based emulsions** | Water | 200–400 | Aluminium forging |

### Application methods:

- **Swab / brush** (manual, low volume).
- **Spray** (atomized air, high volume).
- **Dip coating** (parts dipped in lubricant bath).
- **Phosphate + soap coating** (chemical conversion coating — industry standard for cold heading).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why graphite is king in hot forging:** Graphite has a **layered hexagonal lattice** that shears easily under load, providing a low coefficient of friction (μ ≈ 0.05–0.1). The water carrier evaporates on contact with the hot die, leaving a uniform graphite film.

---

## 2.8 Forging Machines — Detailed Classification

### 2.8.1 Hammer Forging Machines

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Hammers deliver **kinetic energy** ($E = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$) to the workpiece. The impact lasts only **5–30 milliseconds**, generating a high strain rate of **10²–10³ s⁻¹**.

| Hammer Type | Energy Source | Blowing Force (kN) | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Board hammer** | Lifting board + gravity | 5–50 | Small blacksmith work |
| **Belt-lift hammer** | Belt + gravity | 50–500 | Small-scale hot forging |
| **Steam hammer (single-acting)** | Steam raises ram, gravity drops | 200–2000 | General forging |
| **Steam hammer (double-acting)** | Steam drives ram down | 1000–8000 | Heavy forging |
| **Air hammer** | Compressed air | 100–500 | Light-medium work |
| **Counterblow hammer** | Two rams move towards each other | 1000–25,000 | **Energy efficient**, large parts |
| **Hydraulic hammer** | Hydraulic fluid | 5000–100,000 | Modern high-tonnage forging |

### 2.8.2 Press Forging Machines

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Presses deliver **sustained force** (slower, controlled, longer stroke). Two main types:

#### (a) Mechanical Presses

- **Crank press** — most common; stroke fixed by crank radius.
- **Eccentric press** — similar to crank but with eccentric shaft.
- **Knuckle-joint press** — high mechanical advantage at bottom of stroke (ideal for coining).
- **Screw press** — flywheel drives screw; very high force at end of stroke (used in **precision forging**).
- **Forging energy:** Stored in flywheel ($E = \frac{1}{2}I\omega^2$).

#### (b) Hydraulic Presses

- **Direct-acting hydraulic press** — piston driven by hydraulic fluid.
- **Indirect (accumulator) press** — uses nitrogen/oil accumulator for high-speed response.
- **Force range:** 1000 kN to 750,000 kN (largest presses in the world are **80,000-tonne** hydraulic forging presses used for nuclear reactor components).

### 2.8.3 Comparison Table

| Feature | Hammer | Mechanical Press | Hydraulic Press |
|---|---|---|---|
| Force type | Impact | Sustained (variable) | Sustained (controlled) |
| Max force | 25,000 kN | 30,000 kN | 750,000 kN |
| Stroke | Short | Fixed | Variable |
| Speed | 60–120 blows/min | 30–80 strokes/min | 5–20 strokes/min |
| Energy | Kinetic | Flywheel | Hydraulic |
| Best for | Open die, hot die | Closed die, coining | Large closed die, extrusion |
| Vibration | High | Medium | Low |

---

## 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The following table collects **every formula** a KTU 2024 student must memorize for Module 3.

| # | Formula | Variables | Units | Where Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = K_f \cdot \sigma_f \cdot A$ | $K_f$ = shape factor; $\sigma_f$ = flow stress; $A$ = projected area | $K_f$ (—), $\sigma_f$ (MPa), $A$ (mm²), $F$ (N) | General forging load |
| 2 | $\sigma_f = K \cdot \varepsilon^n$ | $K$ = strength coefficient; $\varepsilon$ = true strain; $n$ = hardening exponent | $K$ (MPa); $\varepsilon$ (—); $n$ (—) | Cold forging flow stress |
| 3 | $\varepsilon = \ln \left( \frac{h_0}{h_f} \right)$ | $h_0$ = initial thickness; $h_f$ = final thickness | mm | True strain in forging |
| 4 | $F_{coin} = \sigma_f \cdot A_{proj}$ | $\sigma_f$ at strain-hardened state; $A_{proj}$ = projected area | MPa, mm², N | Coining |
| 5 | $F_{pierce} = \sigma_{f,hot} \cdot A_{punch} \cdot (1 + \mu \frac{D}{h})$ | friction hill factor | MPa, mm², N | Piercing |
| 6 | $F_{head} = K_f \cdot \sigma_f \cdot A_{proj}$ | $K_f = 1.5–3.0$ | N | Heading |
| 7 | $\text{Flash ratio} = W/T = 4–6$ | $W$ = gutter width; $T$ = land thickness | mm | Die design |
| 8 | $\alpha_{draft} = 0°–7°$ (process-dependent) | — | degrees | Die design |
| 9 | $E_{hammer} = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$ | $m$ = ram mass; $v$ = ram velocity | kg, m/s, J | Hammer energy |
| 10 | $E_{flywheel} = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$ | $I$ = moment of inertia; $\omega$ = angular velocity | kg·m², rad/s, J | Mechanical press energy |
| 11 | $P_{press} = \frac{F \cdot v}{A_{ram}}$ | $F$ = force; $v$ = ram velocity; $A_{ram}$ = ram area | Pa | Press power |
| 12 | $L_0/D_0 \leq 2.5–3.0$ | slug length/diameter | mm | Heading buckling limit |
| 13 | $V_{slug} = V_{final} + V_{flash}$ | slug volume balance | mm³ | Closed-die design |
| 14 | $D_{cavity,hot} = D_{part,cold} \cdot (1 + \delta_{shrink})$ | $\delta_{shrink} \approx 0.015$ for steel | mm | Die cavity dimensioning |
| 15 | $\dot{\varepsilon} = \frac{v}{h}$ | $v$ = ram speed; $h$ = workpiece height | s⁻¹ | Strain rate |

---

## 2.10 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Automotive:** ~70% of a car's structural strength components (crankshafts, connecting rods, steering arms, gears) are forged.
> - **Aerospace:** Turbine disks, compressor blades, and landing-gear beams are precision-forged from Inconel/Ti-6Al-4V.
> - **Defence:** Gun barrels, tank tracks, missile casings.
> - **General engineering:** Wrenches, valves, fittings, fasteners (every bolt is a cold-headed forging).
> - **Currency:** Every coin in circulation is coined.

The combination of **forging method + die design + die material + lubricant + machine** is what determines whether a factory can produce a defect-free, dimensionally-accurate, cost-effective part — and this is precisely the design problem KTU examiners test in Part B questions.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section provides **derivation-level** depth for the most important KTU 2024 Module 3 derivations: **(a) forging load for upsetting, (b) friction hill in forging, (c) coining pressure, (d) heading force with shape factor, (e) piercing force, (f) hammer energy**.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Upsetting (Open-Die Forging) Load

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Setup:** A cylindrical slug of initial height $h_0$ and initial diameter $D_0$ is compressed between two flat, parallel dies. Friction at the die-slug interface is governed by Coulomb's law: $\tau = \mu \cdot p$, where $p$ is the local contact pressure.

**Step 1:** Volume constancy (incompressible plastic deformation):

$$
A_0 \cdot h_0 = A_f \cdot h_f
$$

**Step 2:** True strain (axial direction) is defined as:

$$
\varepsilon = \ln \left( \frac{h_0}{h_f} \right)
$$

**Step 3:** Equilibrium of a thin slice of the slug at height $z$ (radius $r$, thickness $dz$). The force balance on the slice under axial stress $\sigma_z$:

$$
\text{Net axial force} = \sigma_z \cdot \pi r^2 \Big\vert_{z+dz} - \sigma_z \cdot \pi r^2 \Big\vert_{z}
$$

plus the friction shear from the die contact acting inward:

$$
\tau \cdot (2\pi r \cdot dz) = \mu p \cdot 2 \pi r \cdot dz
$$

**Step 4:** Using the **Tresca yield criterion** for axisymmetric compression: $\sigma_z - \sigma_r = Y$ (where $Y$ is the uniaxial yield stress ≈ flow stress $\sigma_f$). Therefore $\sigma_r = \sigma_z - Y$.

**Step 5:** Substituting into equilibrium (radial equilibrium $\frac{d\sigma_r}{dr} = 0$ and using $\sigma_r = -p$ at the die surface for sticking friction):

$$
\frac{d\sigma_z}{dz} = \frac{2 \mu p}{r}
$$

**Step 6:** Recognising $p \approx -\sigma_r = Y - \sigma_z$ and integrating across the height:

$$
\sigma_z = Y \cdot \left( 1 + \frac{\mu r}{3 h} \right) \text{ (for sticking friction)}
$$

For **sliding friction** (low $\mu$ or good lubrication):

$$
\sigma_z = Y \cdot \left( 1 + \frac{2 \mu r}{3 h} \right)
$$

**Step 7:** Total upsetting force is the average axial stress times the projected area ($A = \pi r^2$):

$$
F = Y \cdot A \cdot \left( 1 + \frac{\mu r}{3 h} \right)
$$

Or in KTU's standard notation, with $K_f = 1 + \frac{\mu r}{3 h}$ as the **shape factor**:

$$
F = K_f \cdot \sigma_f \cdot A
$$

**Final boxed result (KTU 2024)**:

$$
\boxed{F = \sigma_f \cdot A \cdot \left( 1 + \frac{\mu r}{3 h} \right)}
$$

**Engineering check:** If $\mu = 0.1$, $r = 25$ mm, $h = 50$ mm, then $K_f = 1 + 0.1 \cdot 25 / (3 \cdot 50) = 1.0167$ — friction raises the required force by **~1.7%** (lubrication matters more for thin discs).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Friction Hill in Upsetting

> [!NOTE]
> The **friction hill** is the distribution of axial stress across the radius during upsetting. It rises sharply at the periphery due to friction at the die contact.

**Step 1:** From the equilibrium solution above, at any radius $r$:

$$
\sigma_z(r) = \sigma_f \cdot e^{\frac{2 \mu (R - r)}{h}}
$$

where $R$ is the slug radius. This is the **friction hill equation**.

**Step 2:** Expand as Taylor series for small exponent:

$$
\sigma_z(r) \approx \sigma_f \cdot \left[ 1 + \frac{2 \mu (R - r)}{h} \right]
$$

**Step 3:** Maximum stress at the periphery ($r = 0$):

$$
\sigma_{z,max} = \sigma_f \cdot e^{\frac{2 \mu R}{h}}
$$

**Step 4:** The average axial stress is:

$$
\bar{\sigma_z} = \sigma_f \cdot \frac{h}{2 \mu R} \left( e^{\frac{2 \mu R}{h}} - 1 \right)
$$

**Step 5:** Total upsetting force:

$$
F = \bar{\sigma_z} \cdot A = \sigma_f \cdot \pi R^2 \cdot \frac{h}{2 \mu R} \left( e^{\frac{2 \mu R}{h}} - 1 \right)
$$

**Final boxed result (KTU 2024)**:

$$
\boxed{F = \frac{\pi R^2 \sigma_f h}{2 \mu R} \left( e^{\frac{2 \mu R}{h}} - 1 \right) = \frac{\pi R h \sigma_f}{2 \mu} \left( e^{\frac{2 \mu R}{h}} - 1 \right)}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Quick numerical sanity check:** For a steel slug with $R = 25$ mm, $h = 50$ mm, $\sigma_f = 400$ MPa, $\mu = 0.1$:
> Exponent = $2 \cdot 0.1 \cdot 25 / 50 = 0.1$
> $e^{0.1} - 1 = 0.1052$
> $F = (\pi \cdot 25 \cdot 50 \cdot 400 \cdot 0.1052) / (2 \cdot 0.1)$
> $F \approx 8.27 \times 10^6$ N = **8270 kN** ≈ 827 tonnes.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Coining Pressure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Coining is closed-die squeezing to a fixed die-cavity thickness. The metal is constrained, so the *pressure* required is essentially the flow stress of the material at the imposed strain.

**Step 1:** The cold-flow stress at strain $\varepsilon$:

$$
\sigma_f = K \cdot \varepsilon^n
$$

**Step 2:** The true strain in coining (thickness reduction):

$$
\varepsilon = \ln \left( \frac{h_0}{h_f} \right)
$$

**Step 3:** The coining force is the average pressure over the projected area:

$$
F = \sigma_f \cdot A_{proj} = K \cdot \left[ \ln \left( \frac{h_0}{h_f} \right) \right]^n \cdot A_{proj}
$$

**Step 4:** Substituting typical values (e.g., copper with $K = 315$ MPa, $n = 0.54$, $h_0 = 5$ mm, $h_f = 3.6$ mm, $A_{proj} = 200$ mm²):

$$
\varepsilon = \ln(5/3.6) = 0.329
$$

$$
\sigma_f = 315 \cdot 0.329^{0.54} = 315 \cdot 0.563 = 177 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
F = 177 \cdot 200 = 35{,}400 \text{ N} \approx 35.4 \text{ kN}
$$

**Final boxed result (KTU 2024)**:

$$
\boxed{F_{coin} = K \cdot \left[ \ln \left( \frac{h_0}{h_f} \right) \right]^n \cdot A_{proj}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Heading Force with Shape Factor

> [!NOTE]
> Heading is an upsetting operation at the end of a bar. The shape factor accounts for the **constrained flow** at the unsupported end and the **barrel distortion**.

**Step 1:** The shape factor for a headed cylindrical slug:

$$
K_f = 1 + \frac{0.4 \mu (D_0/d_0 - 1)}{h_0/d_0}
$$

where $D_0$ = head diameter, $d_0$ = shank diameter, $h_0$ = head height.

**Step 2:** Heading force:

$$
F_{head} = K_f \cdot \sigma_f \cdot A_{head} = K_f \cdot \sigma_f \cdot \frac{\pi D_0^2}{4}
$$

**Step 3:** Substituting typical values ($D_0 = 20$ mm, $d_0 = 10$ mm, $h_0 = 12$ mm, $\mu = 0.08$, $\sigma_f = 500$ MPa):

$$
K_f = 1 + \frac{0.4 \cdot 0.08 \cdot (2 - 1)}{1.2} = 1 + 0.0267 = 1.0267
$$

$$
A_{head} = \frac{\pi \cdot 20^2}{4} = 314.16 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
F_{head} = 1.0267 \cdot 500 \cdot 314.16 = 161{,}260 \text{ N} \approx 161 \text{ kN}
$$

**Final boxed result (KTU 2024)**:

$$
\boxed{F_{head} = \left[ 1 + \frac{0.4 \mu (D_0/d_0 - 1)}{h_0/d_0} \right] \cdot \sigma_f \cdot \frac{\pi D_0^2}{4}}
$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Piercing Force with Friction Hill

> [!NOTE]
> In piercing, the punch penetrates the slug. Friction acts on the punch walls and on the die-billet interface.

**Step 1:** Basic piercing force (no friction):

$$
F_{0} = \sigma_{f,hot} \cdot A_{punch} = \sigma_{f,hot} \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}
$$

**Step 2:** Add friction correction for the **punch-billet interface** (area $\pi D h$):

$$
F_{friction} = \mu \cdot p \cdot \pi D h
$$

where $p$ is the normal contact pressure $\approx \sigma_{f,hot}$.

**Step 3:** Total piercing force:

$$
F_{pierce} = \sigma_{f,hot} \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} + \mu \cdot \sigma_{f,hot} \cdot \pi D h
$$

**Step 4:** Factor out $\sigma_{f,hot} \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$:

$$
F_{pierce} = \sigma_{f,hot} \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot \left( 1 + \frac{4 \mu h}{D} \right)
$$

**Step 5:** Numerical check ($D = 30$ mm, $h = 25$ mm, $\sigma_{f,hot} = 200$ MPa at 1100°C, $\mu = 0.2$):

$$
F_{pierce} = 200 \cdot \frac{\pi \cdot 900}{4} \cdot \left( 1 + \frac{4 \cdot 0.2 \cdot 25}{30} \right)
$$

$$
F_{pierce} = 200 \cdot 706.86 \cdot (1 + 0.667) = 141{,}372 \cdot 1.667 = 235{,}617 \text{ N} \approx 236 \text{ kN}
$$

**Final boxed result (KTU 2024)**:

$$
\boxed{F_{pierce} = \sigma_{f,hot} \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot \left( 1 + \frac{4 \mu h}{D} \right)}
$$

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Hammer Energy and Ram Velocity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A hammer's ability to deform a workpiece depends on its **kinetic energy** at the moment of impact. The energy is converted into plastic deformation work plus elastic strain in the machine frame.

**Step 1:** Energy balance for a single blow:

$$
E_{ram} = E_{plastic} + E_{elastic} + E_{friction} + E_{sound,heat}
$$

**Step 2:** The plastic deformation work for an upset from $h_0$ to $h_f$:

$$
E_{plastic} = \bar{\sigma_f} \cdot V \cdot \varepsilon
$$

where $V$ = slug volume, $\varepsilon$ = true strain, $\bar{\sigma_f}$ = average flow stress.

**Step 3:** For a falling ram, the kinetic energy is from gravity drop:

$$
E_{ram} = m \cdot g \cdot H
$$

where $H$ = drop height.

**Step 4:** Equating (ignoring losses):

$$
m \cdot g \cdot H = \bar{\sigma_f} \cdot V \cdot \ln \left( \frac{h_0}{h_f} \right)
$$

**Step 5:** Velocity of the ram at impact:

$$
v = \sqrt{2 g H}
$$

**Step 6:** For a 1000 kg ram with $H = 2$ m:
$v = \sqrt{2 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 2} = 6.26$ m/s
$E_{ram} = 1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 2 = 19{,}620$ J ≈ **19.6 kJ** per blow.

**Final boxed result (KTU 2024)**:

$$
\boxed{E_{ram} = m g H = \frac{1}{2} m v^2}
$$

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Forging Method

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix helps decide **which forging method + die material + lubricant + machine** to use for a given component.

| Part Type | Recommended Method | Die Material | Lubricant | Forging Machine |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Coin / medal | Coining | D2 / carbide | Light oil | Knuckle-joint press |
| Bolt head | Cold heading | M2 / carbide | Phosphate + soap | Cold header |
| Connecting rod | Precision forging (closed die) | H13 | Graphite-water | Screw press / hydraulic press |
| Crankshaft | Hot forging + rolling | H13 / H21 | Graphite-water | Hammers + forging rolls |
| Gear blank | Closed-die hot forging | H13 | Graphite-water | Mechanical press |
| Tube with hole | Piercing | H11 / H13 | Glass lubricant | Hydraulic press (Mannesmann) |
| Aircraft structural | Precision forging (isothermal) | Inconel dies | Glass lubricant | Hydraulic press in vacuum |
| Hand wrench | Open-die forging + finishing | H11 | Graphite-oil | Hammer |

---

## 3.8 Numerical Worked Examples for KTU Board Exam Practice

> [!TIP]
> Three KTU-flavoured numerical problems with full solutions.

### Example 3.8.1 — Hot Forging Load

**Problem:** A steel cylindrical slug of 50 mm diameter and 75 mm height is hot forged at 1200 °C between flat dies. Flow stress at this temperature is 150 MPa, friction coefficient is 0.15. Find the maximum forging load at 30% height reduction.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Final height: $h_f = h_0 (1 - 0.30) = 75 \cdot 0.70 = 52.5$ mm.
Step 2: True strain: $\varepsilon = \ln(75 / 52.5) = \ln(1.4286) = 0.357$.
Step 3: Average slug radius remains ≈ 25 mm (volume constancy: $r_f = r_0 \sqrt{h_0/h_f} = 25 \cdot \sqrt{1.4286} = 29.9$ mm; use original radius as approximation for shape factor).
Step 4: Shape factor with sticking friction:

$$
K_f = 1 + \frac{\mu r_0}{3 h} = 1 + \frac{0.15 \cdot 25}{3 \cdot 52.5} = 1 + 0.0238 = 1.0238
$$

Step 5: Forging load:

$$
F = K_f \cdot \sigma_f \cdot A = 1.0238 \cdot 150 \cdot \frac{\pi \cdot 50^2}{4}
$$

$$
F = 1.0238 \cdot 150 \cdot 1963.5 = 301{,}587 \text{ N} \approx 301.6 \text{ kN}
$$

**Answer: F ≈ 302 kN** (≈ 30.7 tonnes force).

### Example 3.8.2 — Coining Pressure

**Problem:** A brass disc (K = 450 MPa, n = 0.45) is coined from thickness 4 mm to 3 mm in a die of 25 mm diameter. Find the required coining force.

**Solution:**

Step 1: True strain: $\varepsilon = \ln(4/3) = 0.2877$.
Step 2: Flow stress: $\sigma_f = 450 \cdot 0.2877^{0.45} = 450 \cdot 0.572 = 257.5$ MPa.
Step 3: Projected area: $A = \pi \cdot 12.5^2 = 490.87$ mm².
Step 4: Coining force:

$$
F = 257.5 \cdot 490.87 = 126{,}399 \text{ N} \approx 126.4 \text{ kN}
$$

**Answer: F ≈ 126.4 kN** (≈ 12.9 tonnes).

### Example 3.8.3 — Heading Force

**Problem:** A bolt head is to be cold-headed on a 12 mm shank to a head diameter of 22 mm and head height of 9 mm. Material is medium-carbon steel with flow stress 600 MPa and μ = 0.08. Find the heading force.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Head area: $A = \pi \cdot 22^2 / 4 = 380.13$ mm².
Step 2: Shape factor:

$$
K_f = 1 + \frac{0.4 \cdot 0.08 \cdot (22/12 - 1)}{9/12} = 1 + \frac{0.4 \cdot 0.08 \cdot 0.833}{0.75}
$$

$$
K_f = 1 + 0.0356 = 1.0356
$$

Step 3: Heading force:

$$
F = 1.0356 \cdot 600 \cdot 380.13 = 236{,}240 \text{ N} \approx 236.2 \text{ kN}
$$

**Answer: F ≈ 236 kN** (≈ 24 tonnes).
