# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions are modelled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme** End Semester Examination (ESE) pattern. Mark distribution: **Part A = 3 marks each (short answer)**, **Part B = 14 marks each (full descriptive + numerical)** with **internal choice**. Bloom's taxonomy cognitive levels (Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyse, Evaluate) are mapped to each question.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper)]

> Differentiate between **open-die forging** and **closed-die forging**. Mention two typical components produced by each method.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Open-Die Forging | Closed-Die Forging |
|---|---|---|
| Die shape | Flat or simple contour | Matches final part geometry |
| Material flow | Free lateral flow | Constrained within cavity |
| Tolerance | ±1–3 mm | ±0.1–0.5 mm |
| Flash | None | Yes (controlled) |
| Production rate | Low | High |
| Typical parts | Shafts, rings, discs | Gears, connecting rods, bolts |
| Equipment | Hammers, large hydraulic presses | Mechanical/hydraulic presses |

**Marking guide:** 2 marks for clear differentiation in tabular form; 1 mark for typical components.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]

> List the **major functions of forging lubricants** and name the most commonly used lubricant in **hot forging**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Functions of forging lubricant:**
1. Reduce friction between die and workpiece (prevents sticking and scoring).
2. Act as a thermal barrier — protect die from rapid heat loss/cycling.
3. Insulate workpiece from die chilling (helps maintain forging temperature).
4. Reduce die wear (extends die life by 2–5×).
5. Improve surface finish of the forged part.
6. Aid in metal flow (especially in deep cavities).

**Most common hot-forging lubricant:** **Graphite suspended in water** (typical ratio 1:5 to 1:10 graphite:water by mass; applied by spray or swab).

**Marking guide:** 2 marks for any four functions; 1 mark for naming the lubricant.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Forging Method Questions:**
> - Students frequently **omit the friction hill / shape factor** when computing forging load — losing 2–3 marks instantly. Always state whether friction is *sticking* or *sliding* and include the appropriate correction.
> - When asked to "compare" methods, students often write paragraphs without a **table** — this is a 1–2 mark penalty in 2024 Scheme. Use tables.
> - Die-design questions require both **dimensional** (flash ratio, draft, web thickness) and **material** (H11/H13) aspects — missing one loses ~5 marks.
> - In coining questions, students confuse the *flow stress at the cold-worked state* with the *initial yield stress*. Use $K \varepsilon^n$ form consistently.

---

### Question 3A. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 14 Marks, Option A]

#### (a) [7 marks] Explain the different methods of forging with neat sketches (descriptions are accepted in lieu of sketches per KTU 2024 scheme): **(i) Precision forging, (ii) Coining, (iii) Heading, (iv) Piercing.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Precision Forging** (2 marks)
Precision forging is a near-net-shape forging process that uses tightly controlled die geometry, lubrication, and temperature to produce parts with tolerances of ±0.1–0.25 mm and surface roughness Ra 1–3 µm. Multi-stage preform → finish dies are used. Typical applications: bevel gears, connecting rods, CV joints, aerospace brackets.

**(ii) Coining** (1.5 marks)
Coining is a *cold* closed-die process where the slug is squeezed at 1500–2500 MPa so the die-cavity detail (including fine engraving) is fully transferred. Used for minting coins, medals, jewellery, embossing. Force: $F = \sigma_f \cdot A_{proj}$.

**(iii) Heading** (1.5 marks)
Heading is forging the end of a bar/wire to enlarge its cross-section — the workhorse process for bolt heads, rivets, screws, nails. Performed cold at 100–300 pieces/min on multi-station cold headers. Buckling limit: $L_0/D_0 \le 2.5–3.0$. Lubricant: phosphate + soap.

**(iv) Piercing** (2 marks)
Piercing is forcing a punch through a heated, enclosed slug to create a hole *without* removing material (the slug is *displaced*). Common in tube manufacturing (Mannesmann process) and closed-die forging of flanges, gears, and rings. Force: $F = \sigma_{f,hot} A_{punch}(1 + 4\mu h/D)$.

#### (b) [7 marks] A cylindrical steel slug of 60 mm diameter and 80 mm height is hot forged at 1100 °C with a friction coefficient of 0.12. The flow stress at this temperature is 175 MPa. Determine (i) the forging load when the height is reduced to 50 mm, using the friction-hill method; (ii) the ram energy required if the ram mass is 1500 kg and drop height is 2 m.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Calculate final radius using volume constancy.
$r_f = r_0 \sqrt{h_0/h_f} = 30 \cdot \sqrt{80/50} = 30 \cdot 1.2649 = 37.95$ mm.
Use $r_0 = 30$ mm for shape factor (standard convention in first iteration).

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Friction-hill maximum axial stress:
$\sigma_{z,max} = \sigma_f \cdot e^{2\mu R/h_f} = 175 \cdot e^{2 \cdot 0.12 \cdot 30 / 50}$
$= 175 \cdot e^{0.144} = 175 \cdot 1.1549 = 202.1$ MPa.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Average axial stress:
$\bar{\sigma_z} = \sigma_f \cdot \frac{h}{2\mu R}(e^{2\mu R/h} - 1) = 175 \cdot \frac{50}{2 \cdot 0.12 \cdot 30} \cdot 0.1549$
$= 175 \cdot 6.944 \cdot 0.1549 = 188.3$ MPa.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Forging load:
$F = \bar{\sigma_z} \cdot \pi R^2 = 188.3 \cdot \pi \cdot 30^2 = 188.3 \cdot 2827.4 = 532{,}396$ N ≈ **532.4 kN**.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Ram kinetic energy:
$E = m \cdot g \cdot H = 1500 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 2 = 29{,}430$ J ≈ **29.4 kJ**.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Energy required to deform the slug (plastic work):
$V = \pi R^2 h_0 = \pi \cdot 30^2 \cdot 80 = 226{,}195$ mm³ = 226.2 cm³.
$\varepsilon = \ln(80/50) = 0.470$.
$E_{plastic} = \bar{\sigma_f} \cdot V \cdot \varepsilon = 188.3 \cdot 226.2 \cdot 10^{-6} \cdot 0.470 \cdot 10^3$ (in N·m)
$\approx 188.3 \cdot 226.2 \cdot 0.470 \cdot 10^{-3} = 20.0$ kJ.
Hammer efficiency = 20.0/29.4 = **68%** (rest lost to vibration, sound, frame strain).

**Final Answers:**
- Forging load = **532 kN**
- Ram energy available = **29.4 kJ**
- Plastic work absorbed = **20.0 kJ**
- Hammer efficiency ≈ 68 %

---

### Question 3B. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 14 Marks, Option B (Internal Choice)]

#### (a) [7 marks] Describe the design of forging dies. Explain in detail (i) parting line, (ii) draft angle, (iii) flash gutter with flash ratio, and (iv) die material selection with examples.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Parting Line (PL)** (1.5 marks)
The PL is the line where the upper and lower die halves meet. Selection criteria:
- Place PL at the **largest cross-section** of the part (easiest forging + trimming).
- Avoid PL through critical functional surfaces.
- For symmetric parts, PL lies on the **symmetry plane**.
- PL must allow metal to flow *freely* into both die halves.

**(ii) Draft Angle** (1.5 marks)
Slight taper on die walls for part ejection.
- Conventional hot forging: **3°–7°**
- Precision hot forging: **1°–3°**
- Cold forging / coining: **0°–1°** (or 0° with knockout pins)

**(iii) Flash Gutter & Flash Ratio** (2 marks)
The flash gutter is a deliberately machined gap at the PL that controls metal flow. It has:
- **Land (bridge)** of thickness $T$ — builds back-pressure.
- **Well** of width $W$ — accepts excess material.

**Flash ratio:** $W/T = 4$ to $6$.
This is the *pressure regulator* of closed-die forging. If $W/T < 4$: too much back-pressure → die fracture. If $W/T > 6$: insufficient back-pressure → incomplete die fill.

**(iv) Die Material Selection** (2 marks)
Criteria: operating temperature, wear resistance, toughness, production volume.

| Material | HRC | Use |
|---|---|---|
| H11 | 48–54 | Hot forging of Al, Mg |
| **H13** | 48–54 | **General hot forging** (default) |
| H21 | 45–55 | Severe hot forging of steel |
| D2 | 58–62 | Cold heading, coining |
| WC-Co carbide | 70–80 HRA | High-volume precision inserts |

Heat treatment sequence: rough machine → harden & temper → EDM cavity → hand polish → try-out.

#### (b) [7 marks] With neat sketches (functional block diagrams accepted), explain the working of a **mechanical forging press** and a **hydraulic forging press**. Compare them in a table.

**Model Answer:**

**Mechanical Press** (3 marks)
- Energy stored in a **flywheel** rotating at high speed ($E = \frac{1}{2}I\omega^2$).
- The flywheel drives a **crank**, **eccentric**, or **screw** mechanism that converts rotation into linear ram motion.
- The **knuckle-joint press** is a special type that provides very high mechanical advantage near the bottom of the stroke — ideal for **coining**.
- The **screw press** uses a screw mechanism to convert flywheel rotation into high force at the bottom — ideal for **precision forging**.
- Force: **fixed maximum** (depends on flywheel energy and ram deceleration).
- Speed: 30–80 strokes/min.
- Stroke: **fixed** (not adjustable).

**Hydraulic Press** (3 marks)
- Energy provided by **hydraulic fluid** pressurised by a pump (often with a **nitrogen accumulator** for high-speed response).
- The fluid drives a **piston** that moves the ram.
- Force: **continuously variable** up to a set maximum.
- Speed: **variable** (5–20 strokes/min).
- Stroke: **adjustable** (no fixed bottom-dead-centre).
- Modern hydraulic presses can develop up to **750,000 kN** (75,000 tonnes) — used for the largest forged parts (nuclear reactor shells, ship propeller shafts).

**Comparison Table** (1 mark)

| Feature | Mechanical Press | Hydraulic Press |
|---|---|---|
| Energy source | Flywheel | Hydraulic fluid |
| Max force | 30,000 kN | 750,000 kN |
| Stroke | Fixed | Variable |
| Speed | High (30–80 SPM) | Low (5–20 SPM) |
| Force control | Limited | Excellent |
| Initial cost | Lower | Higher |
| Best for | Coining, small precision parts | Large parts, precision, extrusion |

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> This is your **2-minute revision sheet** before walking into the KTU exam hall.

### ✅ Key Definitions
- **Forging** — plastic deformation of metal between dies under compressive force.
- **Precision forging** — near-net-shape, ±0.1–0.25 mm tolerance, Ra 1–3 µm.
- **Coining** — *cold* closed-die squeezing, 1500–2500 MPa, for fine surface detail.
- **Heading** — upsetting the *end* of a bar/wire (bolts, rivets).
- **Piercing** — punching a hole through a heated slug *without* material removal.

### ✅ Essential Formulas
- Forging load: $F = K_f \cdot \sigma_f \cdot A$
- Friction-hill average stress: $\bar{\sigma_z} = \sigma_f \cdot \frac{h}{2\mu R}(e^{2\mu R/h} - 1)$
- True strain: $\varepsilon = \ln(h_0/h_f)$
- Coining force: $F = K \cdot [\ln(h_0/h_f)]^n \cdot A_{proj}$
- Heading force: $F = K_f \cdot \sigma_f \cdot \frac{\pi D_0^2}{4}$ with $K_f = 1 + 0.4\mu(D_0/d_0 - 1)/(h_0/d_0)$
- Piercing force: $F = \sigma_{f,hot} \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot (1 + 4\mu h/D)$
- Hammer energy: $E = m g H = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$
- Flash ratio: $W/T = 4$ to $6$
- Draft angle: 0°–7° (process-dependent)
- Heading buckling limit: $L_0/D_0 \le 2.5–3.0$

### ✅ Die Materials (Mnemonics)
- **H-series** for **H**ot-work dies (H11, H13, H21) — default is **H13**.
- **D-series** for **D**ies in cold-work (D2, D3) — high wear resistance.
- **A-series** for **A**ir-hardening cold-work (A2, A6).
- **M-series** for high-speed-steel **M**olds (M2, M4).
- **Tungsten carbide** (WC-Co) for ultra-high-volume inserts.
- **Inconel / Nimonic** for **superalloy forging** (titanium, jet-engine disks).

### ✅ Lubricants (Recall Table)
- **Hot forging** → **Graphite + water** (universal).
- **Cold forging** → **Phosphate + soap** (for steel), MoS₂ (for non-ferrous).
- **Titanium / superalloy** → **Glass-based lubricant** (high-T).
- **Cold heading** → **Phosphate coating + soap** (mandatory).

### ✅ Forging Machines
- **Hammers** → impact, kinetic energy, vibration. Subtypes: board, steam/air, counterblow, hydraulic.
- **Mechanical presses** → flywheel, fixed stroke, high speed. Subtypes: crank, eccentric, **knuckle-joint** (coining), **screw** (precision).
- **Hydraulic presses** → variable force & stroke, low speed, very high tonnage. Use for largest parts.

### ✅ Numerical Heuristics (Examiner Loves These)
- Hot forging of steel: $\sigma_f \approx 150$ MPa at 1200 °C.
- Cold forging of steel: $\sigma_f \approx 500–700$ MPa.
- Friction coefficient (graphite-water): $\mu \approx 0.05–0.1$.
- Friction coefficient (phosphate-soap): $\mu \approx 0.05$.
- Draft angle (precision): 1°–3°.
- Flash ratio: 4–6.
- Heading $L_0/D_0$ limit: 2.5–3.0.

### ✅ Common Exam Pitfalls
- ❌ Forgetting the **friction-hill factor** in forging-load calculations.
- ❌ Confusing **coining** (cold, sizing) with **stamping** (shearing).
- ❌ Confusing **piercing** (no material removal) with **punching** (material removed as slug).
- ❌ Using **HSS (M2)** for hot forging dies — wrong; use **H13**.
- ❌ Stating **graphite-oil** as the standard hot-forging lubricant — wrong; it is **graphite-water**.
- ❌ Omitting the **internal choice** in Part B (KTU's 2024 scheme allows the student to pick A or B — but both should be answered mentally for full preparation).

### ✅ Final Confidence Booster
- Every KTU 2024 question on this topic will fall into one of three buckets:
  1. **Describe + sketch** a forging method (or its die) → answer in 2–3 paragraphs + labelled block diagram.
  2. **Compare** two methods/machines → use a table with 5–6 parameters.
  3. **Calculate** forging load, coining force, heading force, or piercing force → state the formula, identify all variables, substitute, and box the answer with correct units (kN or tonnes-force).

---

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 3 — Topic Notes.**
> The notes above cover every line item in the KTU 2024 syllabus for the topic *"Forging methods: Precision forging, coining, heading, piercing, die design, die materials and lubrication, forging machines."* For Module 4 preparation, watch for the related topic on **extrusion and drawing processes**.
