# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the term "Draft" in rolling. State the relationship between draft, coefficient of friction, and bite angle. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Model Answer:**

**Draft** in rolling is defined as the **difference between the initial thickness and the final thickness** of the workpiece after one pass through the rolls:

$$\text{Draft } d = t_0 - t_1 = \Delta h$$

**Relationship:**

The bite angle $\alpha$ is related to draft and roll radius by:

$$\alpha = \sqrt{\frac{\Delta h}{R}}$$

For the rolls to **bite** the workpiece, the following entry condition must be satisfied:

$$\tan\alpha \leq \mu \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \mu \geq \tan\alpha$$

This means the **coefficient of friction** between the roll and the workpiece must be at least equal to the **tangent of the bite angle** for successful rolling to occur.

> [!NOTE]
> **Limit:** Maximum draft per pass = $\mu^2 R$ (since $\tan\alpha = \mu$ and $\tan\alpha \approx \alpha$ for small angles, $\alpha^2 = \mu^2 = \Delta h / R$, so $\Delta h_{max} = \mu^2 R$).

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between hot rolling and cold rolling. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Hot Rolling | Cold Rolling |
|---------|-------------|--------------|
| **Temperature** | Above recrystallization temperature (~0.6 $T_m$) | At or near room temperature |
| **Material state** | Soft, ductile | Work-hardened, harder |
| **Surface finish** | Rough with oxide scale | Smooth and bright |
| **Dimensional accuracy** | Lower | Higher |
| **Ductility** | High (continuous recrystallization) | Reduced (strain hardening) |
| **Lubrication** | Often not required | Essential |
| **Typical products** | Slabs, blooms, plates, structural sections | Sheets, strips, foils |
| **Reduction per pass** | 10–50% | 5–25% |
| **Force requirement** | Lower (lower yield strength) | Higher |

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 End Semester Examination (ESE) pattern, each Part B question carries **14 marks** and typically has **internal choice**. Two alternative 14-mark questions are provided below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — QP Code: PCRUT303

**Q1 (a)** Explain with neat sketches the principles of the rolling process. Derive the entry condition for rolling. **(7 marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Solution:**

**Principles of Rolling:**

Rolling is a metal forming process in which a **workpiece** is passed through a pair of **rotating cylindrical rolls** to reduce its thickness or change its cross-section. The rolls exert a **compressive normal force** on the workpiece; combined with **friction** at the roll-workpiece interface, this compressive force is converted into a **horizontal drawing force** that pulls the workpiece into the roll gap.

Key principles:
1. **Bulk plastic deformation** — the entire cross-section yields.
2. **Friction-driven entry** — friction must be sufficient to overcome the resistance to entry.
3. **Compressive stress state** — the principal stresses in the deformation zone are compressive.
4. **Plane strain condition** — for wide strips, the width direction strain is zero ($\varepsilon_w = 0$).

**Derivation of the Entry Condition:**

[Refer to the full derivation in Section 3.1 above]

$$\tan\alpha \leq \mu \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mu \geq \tan\alpha$$

This is the **entry condition** (also called the **friction condition** or **no-slip condition**) for the rolls to grip and pull in the workpiece.

**Sketches to be drawn in the exam:**
- A two-high mill with the bite angle, contact length, and workpiece dimensions labeled.
- A free body diagram of the elemental slab showing normal force $N$, friction force $\mu N$, and its horizontal component $N\sin\alpha$.

---

**Q1 (b)** Describe with sketches the following types of rolling mills: (i) Two-high reversing mill, (ii) Three-high mill, (iii) Four-high mill, (iv) Cluster mill. **(7 marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Two-High Reversing Mill:**
Consists of **two rolls** mounted in a single stand. The direction of rotation is **reversed** between passes, and the workpiece is fed back and forth. Used in **roughing stands** and **plate mills** for heavy reductions. High capacity but slower than continuous mills.

**(ii) Three-High Mill:**
Has **three rolls stacked vertically**. The upper and lower rolls rotate in one direction; the middle roll rotates in the opposite direction. The workpiece passes through the gap between the upper and middle rolls in one direction, and through the middle and lower rolls in the return direction. **No need to reverse the mill** — higher productivity than two-high reversing. Common in **breakdown mills** and **section mills** for billets and rails.

**(iii) Four-High Mill:**
Has **two small work rolls** and **two large backup rolls** supporting them. The backup rolls prevent the work rolls from deflecting elastically under high rolling loads. This allows **smaller work roll diameters** and hence **smaller contact length and lower rolling force** for the same reduction. Essential for **cold rolling mills** and **thin strip production**.

**(iv) Cluster Mill:**
A **multi-roll configuration** in which the small work rolls are supported by multiple intermediate and backup rolls. The **Sendzimir (Z-mill)** with 20 rolls is the most famous example. Work roll diameter can be as small as **5 to 25 mm**, enabling rolling of very thin and very hard strips (stainless steel, silicon steel for electrical laminations, titanium foils).

**Sketches Required:**

- Two-high reversing: Two rolls, sliding table, screwdown.
- Three-high: Three rolls, entry and exit roller tables.
- Four-high: Two work rolls (small) + two backup rolls (large).
- Cluster: Multi-roll concentric arrangement (Z-mill).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — QP Code: PCRUT303

**Q2 (a)** Explain the following processes with neat sketches: (i) Ring rolling, (ii) Thread rolling. **(7 marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Ring Rolling:**

Ring rolling is a specialized rolling process used to produce **large seamless rings** (e.g., railway wheel rims, bearing races, flanges, gear blanks).

- A **preform** (a thick-walled donut-shaped blank with a central hole) is heated to forging temperature.
- The preform is placed on a **mandrel** (a fixed idler roll inserted into the hole).
- A **main driven roll** rotates the ring and presses against its outer surface.
- As the ring rotates, it is squeezed between the main roll and the mandrel, causing the **wall thickness to reduce** and the **diameter to grow**.
- The process is continuous — the ring keeps growing in diameter while thinning.

**Advantages:**
- Material saving (less machining waste).
- Continuous grain flow around the circumference.
- Better mechanical properties (no fiber interruption).
- High productivity.

**Applications:** Railway wheel rims, large diameter ball-bearing races, flanges, gear blanks, nuclear reactor components.

**(ii) Thread Rolling:**

Thread rolling is a **cold forming process** for producing external threads on cylindrical parts (bolts, screws, studs). Threads are formed by **plastic deformation** of the workpiece surface, not by cutting.

- The cylindrical blank is placed between **two hardened steel dies** (flat dies or cylindrical dies) with the inverse thread profile.
- The dies rotate and translate the workpiece, or the workpiece is rolled between **two or three cylindrical dies**.
- Under high compressive pressure, the metal is **displaced** from the thread crests into the thread roots, forming the thread profile.

**Types:**
- **Flat (planar) die thread rolling** — two flat dies, used for short threads.
- **Cylindrical die thread rolling** — two or three cylindrical dies, used for long threads and high-volume production.

**Advantages over thread cutting:**
- Higher productivity (5–10 times faster).
- Better surface finish and dimensional accuracy.
- Higher strength (favorable grain flow, no cutting marks).
- No chips generated.

**Applications:** Bolts, screws, studs, lead screws, threaded rods.

---

**Q2 (b)** Describe the rolling of (i) seamless tubes, and (ii) I-beams. Explain the roll pass design considerations. **(7 marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Rolling of Seamless Tubes (Mannesmann Process):**

The **Mannesmann piercing mill** is used to produce seamless tubes from solid round billets.

- A **round solid billet** is heated to ~1200 °C and fed into a **cross-roll piercing mill** with two rolls set at an angle (typically **6° to 12°**) with the horizontal.
- The angled rolls rotate the billet and feed it forward over a **piercing point (mandrel)**.
- Due to the **cross-rolling action**, **tensile stresses** develop at the center of the billet, causing it to **fracture internally** and form a **hollow shell**.
- The hollow shell then passes through a **plug mill** or **mandrel mill** to refine the wall thickness and diameter.
- A **reeler mill** and **sizing mill** provide the final dimensional accuracy.

**Applications:** Boiler tubes, oil and gas pipelines, hydraulic cylinders, automotive drive shafts, bearing sleeves.

**(ii) Rolling of I-Beams (Beam Mill / Universal Mill):**

I-beams are rolled from **blooms** in a sequence of passes in a **section mill** or **universal mill**.

**Roll Pass Design Considerations:**
1. **Geometric progression** — each pass must achieve a controlled reduction in cross-sectional area (typically 10–20% per pass).
2. **Flange-web separation** — the bloom is first split into separate flange and web streams (V-passes), then progressively formed into the I-shape.
3. **Web stability** — the web must not buckle during rolling. Design of the **web passes** and **flange edging passes** must maintain the web-to-flange thickness ratio.
4. **Flange control** — the flange is shaped by **edging passes** (using vertical rolls) to control width and taper.
5. **Universal finishing stand (UFS)** — final pass uses **two horizontal rolls** (for web height) and **two vertical rolls** (for flange width) simultaneously, allowing precise control of all four dimensions.
6. **Total number of passes** — typically **7 to 15 passes** depending on the size of the beam.
7. **Temperature control** — the bloom is reheated between roughing and finishing to maintain hot-working temperature.

**Applications:** Structural beams for buildings, bridges, transmission towers, industrial sheds, and crane girders.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Rolling Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting units in the final answer** — KTU examiners deduct **0.5 to 1 mark** if roll separating force is given as a bare number without units (MN, kN, or N).
> 
> 2. **Confusing bite angle with neutral angle** — the **bite angle $\alpha$** is the entry contact angle, while the **neutral angle $\beta$** is from entry to the neutral (no-slip) point. Students often interchange them, costing **2 to 3 marks**.
> 
> 3. **Wrong roll radius** — the formula uses **roll radius R, not diameter D.** This is one of the **most common errors** in numerical problems.
> 
> 4. **Mixing hot rolling formulas with cold rolling** — the friction hill equation is strictly for **cold rolling** with significant friction. In hot rolling, friction is also present but the boundary conditions differ. Don't blindly apply the friction hill without checking the regime.
> 
> 5. **Missing the entry condition** — any rolling numerical problem **must** verify $\mu \geq \tan\alpha$. Examiners often award a **bonus mark** for this check.
> 
> 6. **Confusing draft, reduction, and strain** — draft is absolute (mm), reduction is fractional (dimensionless), and true strain is logarithmic. Use the correct one for the question.
> 
> 7. **Skipping the sketch** — for a 7-mark sub-question, a well-labeled diagram is worth **at least 2 marks**. Always include a clear schematic.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Rolling:**

### Core Concepts
- [ ] Rolling is a **bulk deformation process** using rotating cylindrical rolls.
- [ ] Bite angle: $\alpha = \sqrt{\Delta h / R}$
- [ ] Entry condition: $\mu \geq \tan\alpha$
- [ ] Contact length: $L = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$
- [ ] Roll separating force: $P = p_{avg} \cdot b \cdot L$
- [ ] Friction hill: $p_{avg} = Y_f \cdot e^{\mu L / \bar{h}} / (\mu L / \bar{h})$
- [ ] Power: $W = \pi N P L$
- [ ] Forward slip: $s = (v_f - v_r)/v_r$
- [ ] Max draft: $\Delta h_{max} = \mu^2 R$

### Hot vs. Cold Rolling
- [ ] **Hot rolling**: above recrystallization, rough, large reductions, no lubrication.
- [ ] **Cold rolling**: room temperature, smooth, tight tolerances, lubrication required.
- [ ] Hot rolling above $0.6 T_m$ (K); for steel: >~900 °C.

### Rolling Mills
- [ ] **Two-high reversing**: 2 rolls, reversal between passes.
- [ ] **Three-high**: 3 rolls, no reversal, alternating between top-middle and middle-bottom gaps.
- [ ] **Four-high**: 2 small work rolls + 2 large backup rolls.
- [ ] **Cluster (Sendzimir)**: 20 rolls, smallest work rolls, thinnest strips.
- [ ] **Continuous (tandem) mill**: 4–7 stands in series, high productivity.

### Specialized Rolling Processes
- [ ] **Ring rolling**: hot, produces large seamless rings (railway wheels, bearing races).
- [ ] **Thread rolling**: cold, forms threads by plastic displacement, no chips.
- [ ] **Cross-roll piercing (Mannesmann)**: produces seamless tubes from solid billets.
- [ ] **Universal beam mill**: rolls I-beams with simultaneous horizontal + vertical roll control.
- [ ] **Tube rolling (plug mill, mandrel mill)**: reduces wall thickness of pierced shells.

### Industrial Applications
- [ ] **Plates and sheets**: hot strip mill (HSM) + cold rolling mill.
- [ ] **Rails**: two-high reversing blooming mill + universal rail mill.
- [ ] **Wheels**: ring rolling (rim) + forging (hub and disc).
- [ ] **Axles**: hot rolling from billets, often with upsetting.
- [ ] **I-beams**: universal mill with V-passes and edging passes.
- [ ] **Seamless tubes**: Mannesmann piercing + plug mill + reeler.

### High-Yield Numerical Constants
- [ ] Bite angle range: **~14° to 18°** (friction limited).
- [ ] Friction in hot rolling: **$\mu = 0.3$ to $0.5$**.
- [ ] Friction in cold rolling: **$\mu = 0.05$ to $0.15$** (with lubricant).
- [ ] Hot rolling temperature for steel: **~900 °C to 1250 °C**.
- [ ] Cold rolling reduction per pass: **5% to 25%**.
- [ ] Hot rolling reduction per pass: **10% to 50%**.

### KTU 2024 Exam Marks Distribution (Typical)
- [ ] **Part A (2 marks × 2 = 4 marks):** Direct definitions and short answers.
- [ ] **Part A (3 marks × 4 = 12 marks):** Short conceptual questions, comparisons, sketches.
- [ ] **Part B (14 marks × 2 = 28 marks):** Full derivations, process explanations, numerical problems.
- [ ] **Total for Module 4 in ESE:** ~15–18% weightage of the full paper.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonics:**
> - **"Hard work causes friction"** → Cold rolling → high force due to friction.
> - **"Hot has it soft"** → Hot rolling → soft material → low force.
> - **"Ring, ring, ring — I grow in diameter"** → Ring rolling increases diameter while reducing wall thickness.
> - **"Thread, thread, thread — no chips I shed"** → Thread rolling is a chipless forming process.
> - **"Mannesmann's magic"** → Tensile stresses at the center create the hollow tube.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Importance of material selection for manufacturing processes, selection of primary forming process and suitable materials for various products, defects and design considerations in casting, forging and rolling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUT303 - MATERIALS AND MANUFACTURING PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Importance of material selection for manufacturing processes, selection of primary forming process and suitable materials for various products, defects and design considerations in casting, forging and rolling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Material Selection for Manufacturing Processes

**Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
Material selection for manufacturing is a systematic engineering decision-making process that establishes a correspondence between the functional performance requirements of a finished component and the intrinsic physical, mechanical, chemical, and economic properties of candidate engineering materials, while simultaneously optimizing for the chosen primary forming process, secondary operations, service life, and total cost of ownership.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Material selection is **NOT** a single-property decision. It is a multi-objective optimization balancing **four pillars**: (1) Functional Performance, (2) Manufacturability (Process Compatibility), (3) Service Environment Reliability, and (4) Economic Viability.

### The Four Pillars of Material Selection

| Pillar | Key Parameters |
|---|---|
| **1. Functional Performance** | Strength, Stiffness, Toughness, Fatigue Life, Hardness |
| **2. Manufacturability** | Castability, Forgeability, Rollability, Weldability, Machinability |
| **3. Service Environment** | Corrosion Resistance, Thermal Stability, Wear Resistance |
| **4. Economic Viability** | Raw Material Cost, Process Cost, Scrap Rate, Tooling Life |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe Selection" Analogy:**
> Think of designing a part like a chef preparing a dish. The chef (engineer) must select:
> - **Ingredients (Material)** — must suit the dish (function) and the cooking method (process).
> - **Cooking Method (Process)** — frying needs firm ingredients, boiling needs softer ones; you cannot fry ice-cream.
> - **Serving Condition (Service Environment)** — a salad does not survive baking.
> - **Budget (Cost)** — saffron is excellent, but onion may serve equally well for many recipes.
> Similarly, an engineer cannot use cast iron where high ductility is required, nor can brittle cast iron be rolled into thin sheets. **Material and process are interlocked decisions**, not independent ones.

---

## 1.2 Selection of Primary Forming Process

**Formal Definition:**
The **primary forming process** is the first major shaping operation that converts raw material (ingot, billet, slab, liquid melt) into a near-net shape intermediate product. Selection is based on **material, geometry, production volume, mechanical property targets, dimensional accuracy, and cost**.

### Comparison of Major Primary Forming Processes

| Forming Process | Starting Material | Typical Products | Property Signature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Casting** | Molten metal | Engine blocks, pump housings, impellers | Near-isotropic, variable ductility |
| **Forging** | Solid billet/bar | Crankshafts, connecting rods, gears | Fiber-flow aligned, high strength |
| **Rolling** | Ingot/bloom/slab | Plates, sheets, rails, structural sections | Anisotropic, directional grain |
| **Extrusion** | Billet (heated/cool) | Tubes, channels, window frames | Uniform cross-section, good surface |
| **Drawing** | Wire rod | Wires, rods, tubes | Strain-hardened, precise dimensions |
| **Sheet Metal Forming** | Sheet/strip | Car panels, cans, appliances | Thin-walled, strain-hardened |

---

## 1.3 Defects and Design Considerations — Overview

**Formal Definition:**
A **manufacturing defect** is any deviation from the intended geometry, microstructure, or mechanical property of a component caused by improper process parameters, material inconsistencies, tooling errors, or design limitations.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Birth Defect" Analogy:**
> A defect in a casting, forging, or rolled product is like a birth defect in a human baby — it can originate from three sources:
> 1. **Genetic origin (Material):** Impurities, inclusions, gas content.
> 2. **Pregnancy environment (Process):** Temperature, pressure, cooling rate, tooling condition.
> 3. **Anatomical design (Design):** Sharp corners, abrupt section changes, no draft, missing fillets.
> Most defects can be prevented at the **design stage** itself — this is why **Design for Manufacturing (DFM)** rules are essential KTU exam topics.

### Design Considerations — General Rules Common to Casting, Forging & Rolling

1. **Avoid sharp internal corners** — always provide fillet radii.
2. **Maintain uniform section thickness** wherever possible.
3. **Provide adequate draft** for part removal from die/mold.
4. **Locate parting line** on the largest cross-section and in a non-critical surface.
5. **Add machining/corrosion/finishing allowances**.
6. **Use symmetry** to reduce tooling complexity and distortion.
7. **Avoid abrupt changes in section** to reduce stress concentrations and shrinkage cavities.
8. **Account for shrinkage/contraction** during cooling/solidification.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Systematic Material Selection Methodology

The widely taught framework for material selection is structured into **five logical steps**:

### Step 1: Translation of Design Requirements
- Identify **function** of the part (e.g., "must transmit torque").
- Identify **constraints** (e.g., must be non-magnetic, must operate at 300°C).
- Identify **objectives** (e.g., minimize mass, minimize cost, maximize stiffness).

### Step 2: Screening Using Property Limits
Eliminate materials that fail the **boundary conditions**. For example, if operating temperature exceeds the recrystallization temperature of an aluminum alloy, aluminum is eliminated.

### Step 3: Ranking Using Material Indices
For a given design objective, derive a **Material Index (MI)**. Examples:

| Design Objective | Material Index (MI) | Preferred Material Family |
|---|---|---|
| **Minimize mass, stiffness-controlled** | $\dfrac{E^{1/3}}{\rho}$ | CFRP, Magnesium alloys |
| **Minimize mass, strength-controlled** | $\dfrac{\sigma_y^{2/3}}{\rho}$ | High-strength Al, Ti |
| **Minimize cost, stiffness-controlled** | $\dfrac{E^{1/3}}{C_m \cdot \rho}$ | Cast iron, low-carbon steel |
| **Minimize cost, strength-controlled** | $\dfrac{\sigma_y^{2/3}}{C_m \cdot \rho}$ | Low-carbon steel, cast iron |
| **Minimize mass, energy storage (spring)** | $\dfrac{\sigma_y^{2/3}}{\rho \cdot E}$ | Spring steels, composites |

Where:
- $E$ = Young's Modulus (Pa)
- $\rho$ = Density (kg/m³)
- $\sigma_y$ = Yield Strength (Pa)
- $C_m$ = Cost per unit mass (₹/kg)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Tip:** Students are frequently asked to derive or explain the **Material Index for a stiffness-limited, mass-minimized design**. The derivation follows in Section 3.

### Step 4: Documentation of Process Compatibility
For each shortlisted material, document compatibility with the **target forming process** (castability, forgeability, rollability index).

### Step 5: Final Selection Using Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis
Use **weighted-sum** or **Pugh matrix** method to incorporate qualitative factors.

---

## 2.2 Process Selection Logic Flow

A **K-T-M (Kind-Type-Method)** chart or a **process attribute matrix** is typically used. The key **process attributes** considered:

| Attribute | Why It Matters |
|---|---|
| **Part size & weight** | Determines press/mold capacity |
| **Geometric complexity** | Determines if casting is required |
| **Tolerance & surface finish** | Determines if machining/finishing is needed |
| **Mechanical property requirement** | Determines if forging is needed for grain flow |
| **Production volume** | Determines if tooling cost is amortized |
| **Material compatibility** | Determines feasibility of forming process |
| **Capital & unit cost** | Determines economic feasibility |

### Decision Heuristics (Commonly Tested in KTU)

- **Complex internal cavities** → **Casting** (sand, investment, die).
- **High mechanical strength + critical safety part** → **Forging**.
- **Large flat sheets/plates/structural sections** → **Rolling**.
- **Long constant cross-section profiles** → **Extrusion**.
- **Mass production of small accurate parts** → **Die casting / Powder metallurgy / Stamping**.
- **Single piece / low volume** → **Sand casting / Manual forging / Machining from billet**.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Material Selection & Indices

$$
\text{Material Index (Stiffness, min. mass)} = \frac{E^{1/3}}{\rho}
$$

$$
\text{Material Index (Strength, min. mass)} = \frac{\sigma_y^{2/3}}{\rho}
$$

$$
\text{Material Index (Stiffness, min. cost)} = \frac{E^{1/3}}{C_m \cdot \rho}
$$

$$
\text{Material Index (Strength, min. cost)} = \frac{\sigma_y^{2/3}}{C_m \cdot \rho}
$$

### Castability & Formability Numerical Indices

$$
\text{Castability (Fluidity)} \propto \frac{1}{\mu} \sqrt{\frac{2 g \gamma L}{\rho_m}}
$$

Where:
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of melt
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity ($\textbf{9.81 m/s}^2$)
- $\gamma$ = surface tension
- $L$ = length of flow channel
- $\rho_m$ = density of molten metal

### Shrinkage Allowance (Casting)

$$
L_{\text{pattern}} = L_{\text{casting}} \left(1 + \alpha_s\right)
$$

Where:
- $L_{\text{pattern}}$ = Pattern dimension
- $L_{\text{casting}}$ = Required casting dimension
- $\alpha_s$ = linear shrinkage fraction (e.g., 0.01–0.02 for steels, 0.012–0.015 for cast iron, 0.013 for aluminum)

### Draft Angle (Casting & Forging)

$$
\text{Draft} = L \cdot \tan(\theta)
$$

Where:
- $L$ = depth of cavity
- $\theta$ = draft angle (typically $1^{\circ}$ to $3^{\circ}$ for sand casting, $3^{\circ}$ to $7^{\circ}$ for die casting)

### Fillet Radius (Casting & Forging Design)

$$
r_{\min} \geq 0.5 \, t_{\min}
$$

Where:
- $r_{\min}$ = minimum fillet radius
- $t_{\min}$ = minimum wall thickness adjacent to the corner

### Rolling — True Strain & Reduction

$$
\varepsilon = \ln\left(\frac{h_0}{h_f}\right)
$$

$$
\% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{h_0 - h_f}{h_0} \times 100
$$

Where:
- $h_0$ = initial thickness
- $h_f$ = final thickness

### Rolling — Draft Per Pass

$$
\Delta h = h_0 - h_f
$$

Typical draft limits:
- Hot rolling of steel: $\Delta h = 20$–$40 \text{ mm/pass}$
- Cold rolling of steel: $\Delta h = 0.1$–$5 \text{ mm/pass}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** Always use **fractional units** in the material index formulas (Pa, kg/m³), **not MPa or g/cm³** — the units must be dimensionally consistent. Conversion: $1 \text{ MPa} = 10^6 \text{ Pa}$; $1 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Material-Process Decision Example |
|---|---|
| **Automotive** | Connecting rod: Medium-carbon steel → **Hot forging** (for fatigue strength and grain flow) |
| **Aerospace** | Turbine blade: Ni-based superalloy → **Investment casting** (complex cooling channels) |
| **Construction** | I-beam: Low-carbon steel → **Hot rolling** (mass production, structural shape) |
| **Marine** | Propeller: Manganese bronze → **Sand casting** (complex curved geometry) |
| **Piping** | Domestic water pipe: Copper → **Drawing/Extrusion** (long uniform cross-section) |
| **Cookware** | Non-stick pan body: Aluminum alloy → **Deep drawing** (thin-walled cup shape) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Material Index for a Stiffness-Limited, Mass-Minimized Tie

**Problem Setup:**
A tie (a tensile structural member) must support an axial load $F$ without exceeding a deflection $\delta$ at its own length $L$. We need to find the material index that minimizes the mass of the tie.

### Step 1: Express the Stiffness Constraint

The axial deflection of a tie under tensile load $F$ is:

$$
\delta = \frac{F \cdot L}{A \cdot E}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** This is the standard Hooke's law in axial form — $\text{stress} = F/A = E \cdot (\delta/L)$, rearranged for deflection.

Solving for the required cross-sectional area $A$:

$$
A = \frac{F \cdot L}{E \cdot \delta}
$$

### Step 2: Express the Mass of the Tie

$$
m = \rho \cdot A \cdot L
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Mass equals density times volume, and volume for a uniform cross-section tie is $A \cdot L$.

Substituting the area $A$ from Step 1:

$$
m = \rho \cdot L \cdot \frac{F \cdot L}{E \cdot \delta} = \frac{F \cdot L^2 \cdot \rho}{E \cdot \delta}
$$

### Step 3: Group the Variables to Minimize

The fixed (prescribed) variables are $F$, $L$, and $\delta$. The **free variable is the material**, characterized by $\rho$ and $E$.

For minimum mass, the material-dependent term must be minimized:

$$
m \propto \frac{\rho}{E}
$$

### Step 4: Eliminate the Functional Variable (E)

A tie is a stiffness-limited component, so the **functional performance metric** is the stiffness $E$. To compare materials on an equal-performance basis, we **eliminate $E$** by writing it as a function of mass:

$$
m = \left(\frac{F \cdot L^2}{\delta}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{\rho}{E}\right)
$$

To remove $E$ as a "free parameter" (since it defines the constraint, not a knob), we raise both sides to the power $1/3$ after recognizing a **cube** hidden in stiffness for beam-like members. The generalized stiffness-limited, mass-minimized index for ties is:

$$
\boxed{M = \frac{E^{1/3}}{\rho}}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The cube root arises because, for a stiffness-limited part, eliminating $E$ (the stiffness) requires the index to scale as $E^{+1/3}$ so that **larger $E$** (stiffer material) wins, and **larger $\rho$** (heavier material) loses. The exact exponent comes from dimensional analysis of the specific loading case (tensile tie = $1/3$, beam bending = $1/2$, plate bending = $1/3$, etc.).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Shrinkage Allowance for a Casting Pattern

**Problem:**
A gray cast iron casting must be produced with a final length of $L_c = 500 \text{ mm}$. The shrinkage fraction for gray cast iron is $\alpha_s = 0.012$. Find the required pattern length.

### Step 1: State the Shrinkage Formula

$$
L_p = L_c (1 + \alpha_s)
$$

### Step 2: Substitute Values

$$
L_p = 500 \times (1 + 0.012) = 500 \times 1.012
$$

### Step 3: Evaluate

$$
L_p = 500 \times 1.012 = 506 \text{ mm}
$$

**Final Answer:** The pattern must be $506 \text{ mm}$ long to produce a $500 \text{ mm}$ casting after solidification shrinkage.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Required Draft for a Sand Casting Cavity

**Problem:**
A sand casting has a vertical wall depth of $L = 200 \text{ mm}$. The recommended draft angle for green sand molding is $\theta = 1.5^{\circ}$. Calculate the total draft per side.

### Step 1: Write the Draft Formula

$$
\text{Draft} = L \cdot \tan(\theta)
$$

### Step 2: Substitute Values

$$
\text{Draft} = 200 \times \tan(1.5^{\circ})
$$

### Step 3: Evaluate

$$
\tan(1.5^{\circ}) = 0.02618
$$

$$
\text{Draft} = 200 \times 0.02618 = 5.236 \text{ mm}
$$

**Final Answer:** Each side of the wall must be tapered by approximately $\mathbf{5.24 \text{ mm}}$ to allow clean pattern withdrawal.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Rolling — Percentage Reduction and True Strain

**Problem:**
A steel slab is rolled from an initial thickness of $h_0 = 50 \text{ mm}$ to a final thickness of $h_f = 12.5 \text{ mm}$ in a single pass. Find the percentage reduction and the true strain.

### Step 1: Percentage Reduction

$$
\% \text{R} = \frac{h_0 - h_f}{h_0} \times 100
$$

$$
\% \text{R} = \frac{50 - 12.5}{50} \times 100 = \frac{37.5}{50} \times 100 = 75\%
$$

### Step 2: True Strain

$$
\varepsilon = \ln\left(\frac{h_0}{h_f}\right)
$$

$$
\varepsilon = \ln\left(\frac{50}{12.5}\right) = \ln(4) = 1.386
$$

**Conversion Logic:** True strain uses the natural logarithm of the **ratio of initial to final thickness** (not final to initial) because rolling is a compressive deformation that incrementally thins the material. The true strain in compression is positive in magnitude.

**Final Answer:** Reduction = $75\%$; True Strain = $1.386$.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Defects in Casting, Forging, and Rolling

### 3.5.1 Casting Defects — Master Matrix

| Defect | Description | Primary Cause | Design / Process Remedy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Porosity (gas)** | Spherical voids inside casting | Dissolved gases (H₂, N₂) on cooling | Degassing, venting, controlled pour temp |
| **Porosity (shrinkage)** | Irregular cavities at hot spots | Insufficient feed metal during solidification | Use risers, chills, uniform section thickness |
| **Cold shut** | A seam or fold on the surface | Two streams of metal meet but don't fuse | Increase pour temp, improve gating |
| **Hot tear** | Crack formed at high temp | Restricted contraction by mold | Avoid abrupt section changes, uniform cooling |
| **Sand inclusion** | Sand particles embedded in casting | Mold erosion or sand wash | Higher mold strength, filters in gating system |
| **Blow holes** | Cavities near upper surface | Mold gases escape into metal | Proper venting, lower moisture in mold |
| **Misrun** | Casting not fully formed | Metal freezes before filling | Increase pour temp, larger gates |
| **Flash / fins** | Thin extra metal at parting line | Insufficient clamping force or worn mold | Maintain mold, increase clamping |
| **Warpage** | Distortion after removal | Non-uniform cooling stresses | Use chills, normalize after casting |

### 3.5.2 Forging Defects — Master Matrix

| Defect | Description | Primary Cause | Design / Process Remedy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cold shut** | Discontinuity at metal flow meeting line | Low forging temp, poor die design | Increase billet temp, improve die prefill |
| **Cracks (longitudinal)** | Cracks parallel to forging axis | Excessive deformation, low ductility | Reduce reduction per blow, preheat |
| **Cracks (transverse / chevron)** | V-shaped internal cracks | Improper grain flow, tensile stresses | Improve die design, balanced blows |
| **Laps / Folds** | Surface fold that didn't weld | Sharp corners in die, large deformation | Add fillet radii, increase draft |
| **Flakes** | Internal hairline cracks | Hydrogen entrapment during cooling | Slow cool, use low-H₂ practices |
| **Unfilled sections** | Some regions not fully formed | Insufficient material, closed die cavity | Increase billet size, improve die prefill |
| **Scale pits** | Surface depressions | Heavy oxide scale during heating | Use protective atmosphere, descaling |
| **Die wear marks** | Surface impressions of worn die | Excessive die usage | Regular die maintenance |

### 3.5.3 Rolling Defects — Master Matrix

| Defect | Description | Primary Cause | Design / Process Remedy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Edge cracks** | Cracks along strip edges | Excessive reduction, low ductility | Reduce draft per pass, edge pre-heating |
| **Center cracks / bursts** | Internal cracks at mid-thickness | High roll-separating force, poor lubrication | Lower reduction, proper lubrication |
| **Alligator cracks** | Mid-thickness splitting when bent | Severe center segregation in ingot | Use continuous-cast slabs, edge reduction |
| **Wavy edges** | Undulating edge profile | Uneven roll pressure at edges | Roll edge crown, use edge guides |
| **Zebra stripes / pencil lines** | Alternate bright/dark bands | Carbon or impurity segregation | Homogenization heat treatment |
| **Scale marks** | Surface depressions from oxide | Improper descaling | Use high-pressure water jets before entering rolls |
| **Camber** | Sideways curvature of strip | Uneven roll gap across width | Adjust roll bending / skew control |
| **Flatness defects (buckling, wavy center)** | Non-flat sheet | Non-uniform temperature, residual stress | Use leveler, tension leveler, temper pass |

---

## 3.6 Design Considerations — Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Design Rule | Casting | Forging | Rolling |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fillet radius** | $\geq 1.5\,t$ at junctions | $\geq 0.5\,t_{\min}$ minimum | N/A (sheet has no internal fillets) |
| **Draft angle** | $1^{\circ}$–$3^{\circ}$ (sand), $3^{\circ}$–$7^{\circ}$ (die) | $5^{\circ}$–$10^{\circ}$ for die forging | N/A |
| **Web & rib thickness** | $0.6$–$0.8$ times nominal wall | $0.6$–$0.8$ times adjacent wall | N/A |
| **Parting line** | On largest cross-section, non-critical | On maximum projected area, in trim line | N/A |
| **Section thickness** | Uniform, avoid hot spots | Gradual transitions, no sharp steps | Uniform through-thickness reduction |
| **Corner design** | Generous fillets at all intersections | Generous fillets to avoid laps | Smooth entry/exit edges |
| **Shrinkage allowance** | $1\%$–$2\%$ of linear dimension | Thermal contraction considered | Elastic recovery on exit |
| **Machining allowance** | $1.5$–$3$ mm per surface | $0.5$–$1.5$ mm per surface | Negligible (near-net shape) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Note:** The "$\geq 1.5\,t$" fillet rule for castings is more generous than the forging rule because the molten metal flow is sensitive to thermal gradients and abrupt corner cooling. Sharp corners in castings are also stress-raisers and crack-initiation sites.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Material Selection Flowchart (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Part Function] --> B[Define Constraints]
    B --> C[Define Objective]
    C --> D[Calculate Material Indices]
    D --> E[Screening by Property Limits]
    E --> F[Process Compatibility Check]
    F --> G[Multi-Criteria Weighted Decision]
    G --> H[Final Material Selection]
    H --> I[Verify with Prototype / Simulation]
    I --> J{Acceptable?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Document in Design Report]
    J -->|No| D
```

**Conversion Logic:** This block diagram represents the **iterative** nature of material selection — the loop from `D` (re-calculate indices) is critical, as is the final `K` (documentation), which is a KTU 2024 syllabus requirement for **Design Documentation** in the manufacturing cycle.

---

## 4.2 Primary Forming Process Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Starting Material State] --> B{Molten?}
    A --> C{Solid Bulk?}
    B -->|Yes| D[CASTING]
    D --> D1[Sand / Investment / Die / Centrifugal]
    C -->|Yes| E[Compressive Plastic Deformation]
    E --> E1[FORGING - Discrete Parts]
    E --> E2[ROLLING - Continuous Stock]
    E --> E3[EXTRUSION - Constant Cross-Section]
    E --> E4[DRAWING - Wires / Tubes]
    C -->|Yes| F[Tensile / Tensile-like]
    F --> F1[SHEET FORMING - Stamping / Deep Drawing]
    A --> G[Particulate]
    G --> G1[POWDER METALLURGY]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[Molten Metal] --> P1[Pouring Stage]
    P1 --> P2[Solidification Stage]
    P2 --> P3[Cooling Stage]
    P3 --> P4[Final Casting]
    P1 --> D1[Gas Porosity]
```

**Conversion Logic:** This functional architecture groups defects by the **stage in the casting process** at which they originate. This matches the KTU 2024 Module 4 expectation that students classify, not just list, defects.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3 MARK QUESTIONS (Short Answer)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List any four factors that govern the selection of a primary forming process for a given component.**

**Model Answer:**
The selection of a primary forming process depends on:

1. **Geometric complexity** of the part (e.g., internal cavities require casting; simple prismatic shapes are suited for forging/rolling).
2. **Mechanical property requirements** (e.g., high fatigue strength → forging for favorable grain flow).
3. **Production volume** (e.g., high volume → die casting or hot rolling; low volume → sand casting or hand forging).
4. **Material compatibility** with the forming process (e.g., cast iron cannot be rolled; high-strength aluminum is hard to cast).
5. **Cost considerations** — both unit cost and tooling cost.
6. **Dimensional accuracy and surface finish** requirements.

*(Any four points — 3 marks)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between shrinkage porosity and gas porosity in castings.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Shrinkage Porosity | Gas Porosity |
|---|---|---|
| **Cause** | Insufficient feed metal during solidification | Evolution of dissolved gases (H₂, N₂) during cooling |
| **Shape** | Irregular, dendritic, often in hot spots | Spherical, smooth-walled |
| **Location** | Near the top / last-to-freeze regions | Distributed throughout casting |
| **Surface** | Rough, jagged | Smooth, rounded |
| **Remedy** | Use risers, chills, uniform section thickness | Degassing, controlled pour temperature, venting |

*(2 marks for difference table, 1 mark for remedies)*

---

## PART B — 14 MARK QUESTIONS (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split into **two sub-parts of 7 marks each** (e.g., (a) and (b)). Students must answer EITHER **Question A** OR **Question B** from the same module.

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]`
**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat sketch the various **defects in sand casting**. Discuss their causes and remedies.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the important **design considerations** to be followed while designing a casting. How do these differ from forging design rules?

---

### **Model Solution — Question A (a): Casting Defects**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
A casting defect is any irregularity in the chemical composition, structure, or geometric form of a cast product that reduces its serviceability. Casting defects may be classified by the **stage of origin** (pouring, solidification, cooling) or by **nature** (surface, subsurface, internal).

**Major Casting Defects (5 marks):**

1. **Porosity:**
   - *Gas porosity:* Spherical voids due to dissolved gases (H₂, N₂) that come out of solution on cooling. **Cause:** High gas content in melt, moisture in mold. **Remedy:** Degassing (e.g., chlorine treatment for aluminum), pre-drying the mold, venting.
   - *Shrinkage porosity:* Irregular cavities in the last-to-freeze region. **Cause:** Insufficient feed metal. **Remedy:** Proper riser design, chills, directional solidification.

2. **Cold Shut:** A discontinuity where two metal streams fail to fuse. **Cause:** Low pouring temperature, poor gating. **Remedy:** Increase pour temperature, modify sprue/runner system.

3. **Hot Tears:** Irregular cracks through the section formed at elevated temperature. **Cause:** Restricted contraction by mold rigidity. **Remedy:** Reduce mold constraint, add fillets, uniform cooling.

4. **Sand Inclusion:** Sand particles embedded in casting. **Cause:** Mold erosion by high-velocity metal. **Remedy:** Strengthen mold, add strainer cores, use filters in gating.

5. **Blow Holes:** Cavities near upper surface due to mold gases. **Cause:** Moisture in mold, inadequate venting. **Remedy:** Vents at high points, dry sand.

6. **Misrun:** Incomplete filling. **Cause:** Pour temperature too low, thin sections. **Remedy:** Increase pouring temperature, thicken section.

7. **Warpage:** Distortion after shake-out. **Cause:** Non-uniform cooling stresses. **Remedy:** Chills, normalizing heat treatment.

**Neat Sketch (1 mark):**
A hand-drawn diagram showing a casting cross-section with labeled defects (gas pores, shrinkage cavity, sand inclusion, hot tear, cold shut) in their typical locations earns full sketch credit.

---

### **Model Solution — Question A (b): Design Considerations for Casting vs. Forging**

**Casting Design Rules (3 marks):**

1. **Uniform wall thickness** — to avoid hot spots and shrinkage cavities.
2. **Generous fillet radii** — minimum $1.5 \times$ wall thickness at all internal corners.
3. **Adequate draft angle** — $1^{\circ}$–$3^{\circ}$ for sand casting, $3^{\circ}$–$7^{\circ}$ for die casting.
4. **Parting line on maximum cross-section** — to simplify mold making.
5. **Provision for machining allowance** — $1.5$–$3$ mm per machined surface.
6. **Risers and gates** — to feed shrinkage and direct metal flow.
7. **Bosses and ribs** — designed with rounded bases, not sharp transitions.
8. **Avoid large flat areas** — they warp; use corrugation or stiffening ribs.

**Forging Design Rules (3 marks):**

1. **Generous fillet radii** — minimum $0.5 \times$ minimum wall thickness.
2. **Draft angle** — $5^{\circ}$–$10^{\circ}$ on sidewalls for die forging.
3. **Parting line on maximum projected area** — to minimize flash.
4. **Web and rib thickness** — $0.6$–$0.8 \times$ nominal wall thickness.
5. **Grain flow continuity** — design to align fiber flow with principal stress.
6. **Sharp corner avoidance** — to prevent laps and folds.
7. **No undercuts** in closed-die forging; use split dies if unavoidable.

**Comparative Difference (1 mark):**
- Casting design emphasizes **molten metal flow** (draft, fillets, risers, parting line) and **solidification** (uniform section, chills).
- Forging design emphasizes **plastic flow** (fillet radii, draft, web-rib ratios) and **grain flow** (continuous fiber, no abrupt section changes).
- Casting is more tolerant of complex geometry; forging is more constrained but yields superior mechanical properties.

---

### **Question B (Alternative to Question A)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 1]`
**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **importance of material selection** in manufacturing. Discuss the systematic methodology for selecting a material for a given component.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of neat sketches, explain the various **defects in rolling**. Discuss the design considerations for rolled products.

---

### **Model Solution — Question B (a): Material Selection**

**Importance (2 marks):**
Material selection is a critical design decision because it directly determines:
- **Functional performance** of the part (strength, stiffness, toughness).
- **Manufacturability** (whether the chosen forming process is feasible).
- **Service life** (corrosion, wear, fatigue resistance).
- **Total cost** of the product (raw material, processing, life-cycle).
- **Environmental and sustainability** considerations.

A wrong material choice may lead to premature failure, excessive cost, or even safety hazards. Hence, material selection is treated as a **multi-objective optimization** problem in modern design.

**Systematic Methodology (4 marks):**

The Ashby-style selection methodology is widely used:

1. **Translation of design requirements** — define function, constraints, objectives, and free variables.
2. **Screening using property limits** — eliminate materials that fail a constraint (e.g., max service temperature > melting point).
3. **Ranking using Material Index** — for a stiffness-limited, mass-minimized design: $M = E^{1/3}/\rho$. For a strength-limited, cost-minimized design: $M = \sigma_y^{2/3}/(C_m \cdot \rho)$.
4. **Process compatibility check** — verify that the shortlisted material is compatible with the target forming process.
5. **Multi-criteria decision** — apply weighted-sum or Pugh matrix for final selection.
6. **Verification** — prototype, simulation, or testing.

**Example (1 mark):**
For a lightweight aerospace tie, the objective is *minimum mass, stiffness-controlled*. The material index $M = E^{1/3}/\rho$ is highest for carbon-fiber composites and lowest for steels. Hence, CFRP ranks at the top.

---

### **Model Solution — Question B (b): Rolling Defects & Design Considerations**

**Common Rolling Defects (4 marks):**

1. **Edge Cracks:** Cracks along the longitudinal edges of the rolled product. **Cause:** Excessive reduction, low ductility, edge cooling. **Remedy:** Edge-heating, lower draft per pass.
2. **Center Cracks (Bursts):** Internal cracks at mid-thickness. **Cause:** High compressive stress at the roll center, secondary tensile stress, poor lubrication. **Remedy:** Reduce roll force, improve lubrication, lower reduction.
3. **Alligator Cracks (Fish-Mouth):** Mid-thickness splitting when the strip is bent. **Cause:** Severe centerline segregation, excess reduction. **Remedy:** Use continuous-cast slabs, edge-reduction passes.
4. **Wavy Edges:** Undulating edge profile. **Cause:** Uneven roll pressure at the edge, lack of edge guide. **Remedy:** Use edge rollers, edge-crown profile.
5. **Zebra Stripes / Pencil Lines:** Alternate bright and dark bands on the surface. **Cause:** Segregation of carbon, sulfur, or phosphorus. **Remedy:** Homogenization annealing before rolling.
6. **Scale Marks:** Surface depressions from oxide scale rolled into the surface. **Cause:** Inadequate descaling before rolls. **Remedy:** High-pressure water jets, pickling.
7. **Camber:** Lateral curvature of the strip. **Cause:** Uneven roll gap, misaligned rolls. **Remedy:** Roll alignment, roll-bending control.
8. **Flatness Defects (Wavy Center, Buckles, Edge Wave):** Caused by non-uniform plastic flow. **Remedy:** Use leveler, tension leveler, temper pass.

**Neat Sketch (1 mark):**
Cross-sectional sketches showing edge crack, center crack, and alligator crack earn full marks.

**Design Considerations for Rolled Products (2 marks):**

1. **Uniform stock temperature** — to avoid differential flow and residual stress.
2. **Adequate reduction per pass** — within the safe limit (typically $\leq 20\%$–$40\%$ for hot rolling, $\leq 2\%$–$10\%$ for cold rolling).
3. **Proper lubrication** — to reduce roll-separating force and prevent sticking.
4. **Edge preparation** — to prevent edge cracking.
5. **Inclusion control** — to prevent internal cracking.
6. **Crown and roll-bending** — to ensure flatness.
7. **Cooling uniformity** — to avoid residual stresses and warpage.
8. **Slab quality** — sound ingot/bloom with minimal segregation and porosity.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up processes:** A common error is to attribute casting defects to forging or list forging design rules for casting. Always **read the question carefully** and answer for the specific process asked.
> 2. **Listing without classifying:** Just writing "Porosity, Cold Shut, Hot Tear…" without grouping (pouring, solidification, cooling) loses marks. KTU examiners reward **classification** and **causal reasoning**.
> 3. **Forgetting remedies:** For every defect, students must state both **cause** AND **remedy**. A defect without a remedy typically gets only **1 of 2 marks** in the sub-part.
> 4. **Skipping the neat sketch:** A "neat sketch" is worth **at least 1 mark** in any defect question. Omitting it is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 5. **No numerical content where expected:** When asked to "compare" or "justify with calculations," including even a simple shrinkage calculation (e.g., pattern length for 500 mm casting) signals higher Bloom's level and earns full marks.
> 6. **Unit errors in material index:** Mixing MPa with kg/m³ or g/cm³ with Pa gives dimensionally incorrect indices — a **silent mark-killer**.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

### **Material Selection (Must Remember)**
- [x] Material selection balances **Function + Process + Environment + Cost**.
- [x] Material Index for **stiffness, min. mass** = $E^{1/3}/\rho$.
- [x] Material Index for **strength, min. mass** = $\sigma_y^{2/3}/\rho$.
- [x] Material Index for **stiffness, min. cost** = $E^{1/3}/(C_m \cdot \rho)$.
- [x] Material Index for **strength, min. cost** = $\sigma_y^{2/3}/(C_m \cdot \rho)$.
- [x] **Ashby methodology**: Translation → Screening → Ranking → Process → Multi-criteria.
- [x] Material and process decisions are **interlocked** — never make them independently.

### **Primary Forming Process Selection (Must Remember)**
- [x] **Casting** → complex geometry, large parts, low/medium volume.
- [x] **Forging** → high strength, critical safety parts, grain-flow alignment.
- [x] **Rolling** → sheets, plates, structural sections, mass production.
- [x] **Extrusion** → long uniform cross-sections.
- [x] **Drawing** → wires, tubes, precision dimensions.
- [x] Selection criteria: **geometry, material, volume, properties, tolerance, cost**.

### **Casting Defects (Must Remember)**
- [x] Gas porosity, shrinkage porosity, cold shut, hot tear, sand inclusion, blow holes, misrun, warpage, flash.
- [x] Always mention **cause + remedy**.

### **Casting Design (Must Remember)**
- [x] Uniform wall thickness, fillet radii $\geq 1.5\,t$, draft angle $1^{\circ}$–$3^{\circ}$ (sand) and $3^{\circ}$–$7^{\circ}$ (die), parting line on max cross-section, machining allowance $1.5$–$3$ mm.

### **Forging Defects (Must Remember)**
- [x] Cold shut, longitudinal/transverse cracks, laps/folds, flakes, scale pits, underfilled sections, die wear marks.
- [x] Cause + remedy for each.

### **Forging Design (Must Remember)**
- [x] Fillet radii $\geq 0.5\,t_{\min}$, draft $5^{\circ}$–$10^{\circ}$, grain-flow continuity, web/rib $0.6$–$0.8\,t$, parting line on max projected area.

### **Rolling Defects (Must Remember)**
- [x] Edge cracks, center cracks, alligator cracks, wavy edges, zebra stripes, scale marks, camber, flatness issues.

### **Rolling Design (Must Remember)**
- [x] Uniform stock temperature, safe reduction per pass, proper lubrication, edge preparation, inclusion control, roll crown, tension leveler.

### **Universal Design Rules**
- [x] Avoid sharp corners → always use fillets.
- [x] Avoid abrupt section changes → use gradual transitions.
- [x] Provide adequate draft → for part removal.
- [x] Account for shrinkage/contraction → pattern oversize / process compensation.

### **Key Numerical Tools (Exam Favorites)**
- [x] Shrinkage allowance: $L_p = L_c(1 + \alpha_s)$.
- [x] Draft: $\text{Draft} = L \tan(\theta)$.
- [x] Rolling reduction: $\%R = (h_0 - h_f)/h_0 \times 100$.
- [x] True strain: $\varepsilon = \ln(h_0/h_f)$.
